CONSENT CALENDAR
All matters listed under the Consent Calendar are considered by the Council to be routine and will be enacted by one motion without
discussion. If discussion is desired, that item may be removed and considered separately.
 
3.
City Council Meeting Minutes — Approval
Approve the minutes of the special and regular meetings of the City Council held on January 26, 2026, and
February 2, 2026. Approve the minutes of the special meeting of the City Council held on February 7, 2026.
CEQA: This minutes approval is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or administrative
activity that will not result in direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section
15378(b)(5).)
4.
Senior Center and Beresford Recreation Center Roof Improvements — Agreements
Award agreements to Bartos Architecture, Inc. and Stantec Consulting Services, Inc. for design professional
services for the Senior Center and Beresford Recreation Center Roof Improvements Projects in amounts not to
1 of 719

--- page 2 ---
exceed $120,820 and $141,776, respectively, with contingency reserves of $12,000 and $14,000, respectively;
and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the agreements in substantially the form presented and issue
amendments within the respective contingency amounts.
CEQA: These projects are categorically exempt from CEQA as an “existing facility,” because they consist of the
operation, repair, maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of existing public or private
structures, facilities, mechanical equipment, or topographical features, involving negligible or no expansion of
existing or former use. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15301.)
5.
Commercial Property Management – Agreement
Approve an agreement with Prodesse Property Group, in an amount not to exceed $125,000 for a term from
March 1, 2026 through February 28, 2029, with an optional two-year extension, to manage City-owned
properties and authorize the City Manager to execute the agreement in substantially the form presented.
CEQA: This agreement is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or administrative activity
that will not result in direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)
(5).)
6.
City of San Mateo and All Union/Association MOUs — Amendment
Adopt a Resolution to amend the San Mateo City Employees’ Association (SMCEA), San Mateo Police Officers
Association (SMPOA), San Mateo Police Sergeants Association (SMPSA), San Mateo Safety Management
Association (SMSMA), San Mateo Management Employees’ Association (SMMEA), SEIU Library Merit Unit (SEIU
LIB), SEIU Library Per Diem Unit (SEIU LIB PD), SEIU Maintenance Unit (SEIU M) and SEIU Non-Merit Per Diem
Unit (SEIU NMPT) MOUs pertaining to the total compensation survey sections and approve side letters of
agreement with SMCEA, SMPOA, SMPSA, SMSMA, SMMEA, SEIU LIB, SEIU LIB PD, SEIU M and SEIU NMPT for
total compensation survey methodology and authorize the City and its unions/associations to execute the
agreement in substantially the form presented.
CEQA: This side letter of agreements is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is a government fiscal activity
which does not involve any commitment to any specific project which may result in a potentially significant
physical impact on the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(4).)
PUBLIC COMMENT
Members of the public wishing to comment on any item not appearing on the agenda may address the City Council at this time. State
law prevents Council from taking action on any matter not on the agenda; your comments may be referred to staff for follow up.
Public comment is limited to a total of 15 minutes; however, an opportunity for additional public comment may be provided later in
the agenda.
 
OLD BUSINESS
7.
Grand Boulevard Initiative and Central El Camino Real Plan — Appropriation
Adopt a Resolution to appropriate $70,000 of the City’s Measure A funds to support additional San Mateo-
specific studies and community engagement; and receive an informational presentation on the Central El Camino
Multimodal Plan being developed in partnership with SamTrans and the Cities of San Mateo, Belmont, San Carlos,
and Redwood City.
CEQA: This Council action is exempt from CEQA, as it involves feasibility or planning studies for future City actions
which has not been approved, adopted, or funded. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15262.)
8.
Comprehensive Zoning Code Update Project — Direction
2 of 719

--- page 3 ---
Receive a presentation on the Comprehensive Zoning Code Update Project, receive public comments, and
provide staff with direction on how to proceed with updating the City’s Zoning Code.
CEQA: This action is not subject to the California Environmental Quality Act (“CEQA”) pursuant to Section 15060(c)
(2) of the CEQA Guidelines, because the activity, which consists of discussion of updates to the City’s Zoning Code
but no actions or amendments, will not result in a direct or reasonably foreseeable indirect physical change in the
environment; and Section 15060(c)(3), because the activity is not a project as defined in Section 15378 of the
CEQA Guidelines, since it has no potential for resulting in a direct or indirect physical change to the environment.
PUBLIC HEARING
City Council action on the following public hearing(s) represents a final decision concluding all administrative proceedings. Judicial
review may be had only if a petition is filed with the Court not later than the 90th day following the date the decision is made.
Judicial review of environmental determinations may be subject to a time period for litigation as short as 30 days following the date
the decision is made.
 
9.
General Plan - San Mateo's 2023-2031 Housing Element — Amendments
Adopt a Resolution to approve amendments to the General Plan’s Housing Element for the Sixth Housing Cycle
(2023-2031) that is compliant with State law, provides a plan for at least 7,015 new housing units, affirmatively
furthers fair housing, and is exempt from review under the California Environmental Quality Act, based on the
listed findings.
CEQA: The adoption of the 2023-2031 Housing Element Amendments are considered exempt under the “common
sense” exemption under state CEQA Guidelines Section 15061(b)(3) because the project involves policies,
programs, and actions to meet the City’s RHNA and AFFH goals that would not have any potential for causing a
significant effect on the environment, and identifies programs and actions to remove constraints and promote
affordable housing development. The proposed amendments involve an update to the Housing Element’s Sites
Inventory, associated text amendments to align with these Sites Inventory updates and minor updates to various
policies, programs, and actions to support meeting the City’s RHNA and AFFH goals.
CLOSED SESSION
The public will have an opportunity to comment before the Council’s consideration of these items. Following the meeting a report of
action from Closed Session will be provided if required.

ADJOURNMENT
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
AGENDAS: Agendas and material are posted on the City’s website on the Friday preceding each Council Meeting and can be viewed on the City's
website at www.cityofsanmateo.org . Any supplemental material distributed to the Council after the posting of the agenda will be made part of the
official record.
WATCHING A MEETING ON TV: City Council meetings are broadcast live on Comcast/channel 27, Wave/channel 26, or AT&T/channel 99.
For transmission problems during the broadcast, please call (650) 522-7099. For all other broadcast comments, call (650) 522-7040, Monday-Friday,
8 a.m. - 5 p.m.
WATCHING A MEETING ON A COMPUTER: There are three ways to stream.
1) Public Meeting Portal www.cityofsanmateo.org/publicmeetings
2) City YouTube channel and stream it on YouTube: http://youtube.com/CityofSanMateo
3) Watch TV live stream: https://www.cityofsanmateo.org/193/Channel-San-Mateo-Live-Stream 
PUBLIC COMMENTS/REQUEST TO SPEAK
Prior to the Meeting 
Send comments to: clerk@cityofsanmateo.org until 4 p.m. the day of the meeting.
During the meeting
By Zoom: Click the link at the top of the agenda and you’ll be added to the meeting. All attendees are muted by default. When the item of interest
is open for consideration, select the “Raise Your Hand” icon and you will be called on at the appropriate time.
By telephone: Call (408) 638-0968 and enter the conference ID found at the top of the meeting agenda. When the item of interest is open for
consideration, select *9 to raise your hand. When called upon, press *6 to unmute, state your name and provide your comments.
In Person: At the meeting complete a “Request to Speak” form. Organized Group and Presentations: Up to five (5) minutes for a group presentation,
with as many speakers as can be accommodated within that time allowance. To qualify for a group presentation, all individuals associated with the
presentation must be clearly identified and confirmed as in attendance at the meeting. Presentation materials must be submitted to
clerk@cityofsanmateo.org one (1) business day in advance of the meeting.
ACCESSIBILITY: In compliance with the Americans with Disabilities Act and to ensure meaningful access for all participants, including those with
limited English proficiency, individuals requiring special accommodations or language assistance to participate in this meeting may contact the City
Clerk’s Office at (650) 522-7040 or clerk@cityofsanmateo.org . Notification 48 hours prior to the meeting will enable the City to make reasonable
arrangements to ensure accessibility to this meeting.

CITY OF SAN MATEO
Special Meeting Minutes
City Council
City Hall
330 W. 20th Avenue
San Mateo CA 94403
www.cityofsanmateo.org
 
January 26, 2026
6:00 PM
Special Meeting
 
CALL TO ORDER at 6:00 p.m.
Roll Call
Present: Mayor Loraine, Deputy Mayor Fernandez, Council Members: Cwirko-Godycki and Diaz Nash, Newsom
AB 2449 Requests and Considerations for Meeting Participation by Teleconference due to Just Cause or Emergency 
Circumstances – there were no requests. 
STUDY SESSION
1.
Storm Drain Master Plan — Informational Update
Jimmy Vo, Senior Engineer and Dan Schaaf from Schaaf and Wheeler gave the presentation.
Public Comment: there were no speakers.
The Council expressed support for the proposed project priorities, use of stormwater fee revenues to support 
bonding for high-priority projects, and setting aside $250,000 annually to address minor ponding. The Council 
also supported continued coordination with private development and related community outreach efforts, 
including the launch of a public stormwater mapping tool. Staff was directed to return with bonding options and 
updated funding projections at a future meeting.
ADJOURNMENT –The meeting adjourned at 6:46 p.m.
APPROVED BY:                                                        
SUBMITTED BY:
Adam Loraine, Mayor                      
Martin McTaggart, City Clerk

City Council Meeting Minutes - Approval

3.
Ceremonial Street Naming Application Honoring Former Mayor Jerry Hill — Approval
Approve the application from Deputy Mayor Fernandez and Council Member Diaz Nash for the ceremonial street 
naming of O’Farrell Street to honor former Mayor Jerry Hill, consistent with the City’s Ceremonial Street Naming 
Policy adopted by Resolution No. 109 (2025).
4.
2033 La Salle Drive - Zoning Reclassification, Zoning Code Amendment, and Planning Application for an Early 
Childhood Education Center — Ordinance Adoption
Adopt an Ordinance to approve a Zoning Reclassification from R1-B to R1-B/Q8, and a Zoning Code Amendment to 
provide development standards for an educational facility and find it exempt from CEQA pursuant to Section 
15061(b)(3) (Common Sense Exemption).
Enactment: Ordinance 2026-01

for a new total amount of $4,513,883; establish a contingency reserve of $30,000; and authorize the Finance

to one optional one-year extension, and to update the payment rates; increase Kittelson and Associates 
agreement by $200,000 for a total not-to-exceed amount of $600,000; and authorize the Public Works Director to 
execute the amendments in substantially the form presented and execute any future term extensions or 
amendments that do not change the not-to-exceed amount of the agreements.

14
Lakeshore Park Batting Cage, Ball Fields, and Concession Building – Lease Agreement
Approve a five-year lease agreement with San Mateo National Little League for the batting cage, ball fields, and 
concessions building at Lakeshore Park, for the period of February 1, 2026, through December 31, 2031, and 
authorize the Parks and Recreation Director to sign the agreement on behalf of the City in substantially the form

15.
Annual Comprehensive Financial Report for the Fiscal Year Ended June 30, 2025 — Approval
Accept the Annual Comprehensive Financial Report (ACFR) for the fiscal year ended June 30, 2025.
PUBLIC COMMENT
Mei Li expressed concerns regarding radio frequency activity, tree damage, and possible environmental impacts in 
neighborhoods, and requested public awareness and follow-up. Kevin Simpson urged Council to prioritize street safety 
following the recent death of Veronica Vasquez and called for stronger action to prevent further traffic fatalities. Rich 
Hedges spoke about the fatal collision at 17th Avenue and El Camino Real, advocated for safety improvements at the 
intersection, and called for action from Caltrans. Mike Swire commented on pedestrian safety at El Camino Real, urged 
the City to prioritize protected infrastructure, and expressed opposition to removing bike lane safety improvements. 
Trina Pierce spoke in opposition to the Humboldt Street bike lanes, stated they negatively impact North Central 
residents and seniors, and questioned the use of related funding. Allison MacQueen shared personal experiences of 
near-collisions while biking, emphasized the importance of pedestrian and bicyclist safety, and urged the Council to 
maintain safe bike infrastructure.
OLD BUSINESS
16.
Historic Policy Effort – Update on Draft Historic Policies and Council Direction
Zachary Dahl, Community Development Director and JulieAnn Murphy from Rincon Consultants presented an 
update on the City’s historic preservation policy effort, including outreach activities, the draft citywide historic 
context statement, and the draft historic preservation ordinance.
Public Comment: Doug D’Anna (organized presentation) spoke in support of restoring and clarifying 
Glazenwood’s status as a historic district. Frank Elliott (A Fair Process) (organized presentation) presented a 
consent-based framework for historic district designation, urged Council to ensure strong property-owner 
approval standards, and provided a summary handout. Geriko Stokes stated he is a homeowner who has 
experienced negative impacts under the historic preservation ordinance and referenced a letter outlining his 
concerns. Shawn Fahrenbruch encouraged the Council to require a plurality of affirmative survey responses to 
move forward and to count non-responses as opposition. Richard Delaney stated there is not universal 
agreement within the community and requested additional outreach. Courtney Krause said she purchased 
property in Glazenwood relying on City materials identifying it as a historic district and requested the City 
confirm that status. Deborah Athens stated that Glazenwood residents are not seeking new policy but are 
requesting restoration and clarification of what she described as the district’s established legal status. Thomas 
Braham requested that the City clarify and formally recognize Glazenwood as a historic district. Gregory Zintel 
asked the Council to confirm Glazenwood’s historic district status. Chris Eckert requested that the Council 
reaffirm Glazenwood’s status as a historic district. Joe Volponi raised concerns regarding potential insurance 
implications associated with historic district designation. Brian Sampson spoke in support of reaffirming 
Glazenwood’s historic district status. Carol Gillman shared positive experiences with Glazenwood’s historic 
district designation. Mike Etheridge stated that a majority of Baywood residents do not support formation of a 
historic district in their neighborhood. Gene Alston expressed concerns regarding lack of clarity in the consent 
process for district designation. Brendan Lawry requested that the City slow the process and allow additional 
time for review and outreach. Shelly Decorte expressed concerns regarding the outreach and engagement 
process. David Light requested that the Central neighborhood be included in the historic context study. Laurie 
Watanuki recommended enhancements to the historic resources inventory and designation criteria. Christina 
Brobst stated that limiting nominations to property owners overlooks broader community heritage interests and 
suggested the City retain the ability to initiate nominations. Eleanore Dogan expressed concerns that expanded 
9 of 719

--- page 10 ---
City Council Regular Meeting Minutes – 1/26/2026
Page 4
historic efforts could conflict with housing goals and questioned continued consultant expenditures. Andrew 
Ryan commented on incomplete outreach, the need for clear local consent requirements, and suggested 
inclusion of anti-bypass provisions for state designation. Zev Simon stated that as a builder he would avoid 
projects located within historic districts due to regulatory constraints. Jordan Grimes expressed concern that 
historic designation could be used in ways that restrict housing development. Ken Abreu raised concerns that 
one neighborhood within a district could impose restrictions on another area. Jeremy Levine expressed concerns 
regarding the consent metrics proposed for creating historic districts. Kolja Schluetter stated that consent 
should require at least 75% approval, with non-responses counted as opposition. Mike Swire expressed concerns 
regarding the amount of public funds being spent on consultants related to the ordinance update.
City Council asked questions of staff. The City Council provided direction to staff to conduct additional 
community outreach prior to the next public hearing, including meetings with the Glazenwood neighborhood, to 
gather further input on the draft Historic Preservation Ordinance and Citywide Historic Context Statement. The 
Council supported modifying the ordinance to include a two-step application qualification process for historic 
district designation consisting of: (1) an applicant-initiated petition requiring signatures from at least 20% of 
property owners (one signature per property), followed by (2) a City-conducted survey requiring approval from 
60% plus one of property owners to proceed. Council directed staff to add language to the Historic Context 
Statement acknowledging the potential for additional time and costs associated with historic designation. The 
Council expressed support for using the term “Landmarks” to describe designated historic properties. The 
Council supported establishing a dedicated Historic Advisory Committee rather than assigning those 
responsibilities to the Community Relations Commission or Planning Commission. Staff will incorporate 
community feedback from the study session and additional outreach prior to presenting final draft documents 
to the Planning Commission and City Council in March and April 2026.
A motion was made by Council Member Cwirko-Godycki to extend meeting beyond 11 p.m.
Moved: Cwirko-Godycki, Seconded: Fernandez
Ayes: Newsom, Loraine, Fernandez, Cwirko-Godycki, and Diaz Nash
Noes: None
Motion passed 5-0.
PUBLIC HEARING
17.
Accessory Dwelling Unit Ordinance - Zoning Code Amendments — Ordinance Introduction
Elizabeth Gagliardi, Associate Planner provided a presentation on an ordinance introduction recommended for 
San Mateo Municipal Code Chapter 27.19, “Accessory Dwelling Unit and Junior Accessory Dwelling Unit – 
Residential Zones”. Public Comment: there were no speakers.
The City Council asked questions from staff. Council voted to introduce an ordinance to amend the City's 
Accessory Dwelling Unit Ordinance (Zoning Code Chapter 27.19) to align with current state law, address local 
needs and improve the functionality of the ordinance. The Council action included Planning Commission 
recommendations to clarify the Accessory Dwelling Unit Discretionary Review (ADUDR) process and to require 
small second-story decks or balconies with an ADU to not face toward any adjacent residential properties. The 
ordinance will return to the Council for adoption at a future meeting and will go into effect 30 days after the 
date of adoption.
Moved: Cwirko-Godycki, Seconded: Fernandez
Ayes: Newsom, Loraine, Fernandez, Cwirko-Godycki, and Diaz Nash
Noes: None
Motion passed 5-0.
10 of 719

REPORTS, ANNOUNCEMENTS, AND COMMITTEE UPDATES
City Manager, City Attorney and Council Members reported on their various assignments and liaison roles and noted 
past and future events, along with updates. Council Member Newsom requested Council approval for the City to 
correspond with Caltrans and regional elected representatives to help address traffic safety concerns on El Camino Real, 
the Council approved.  
ADJOURNMENT –The meeting adjourned at 11:51 p.m. in memory of the pedestrian who was struck on the 
intersection of El Camino Real and 17th Avenue, Diana and Robert Caughlan, and Daniel Gilbrech.

impacting departmental operations; major City issues, projects, and initiatives; key policy areas requiring closer

City Council asked questions of staff. The City Council provided direction to staff to retain the existing Class II 
bicycle lanes on Humboldt Street; evaluate options to increase speeding enforcement in the North Central 
neighborhood; evaluate enhanced parking enforcement, with particular focus on abandoned vehicles; advance 
exploration of a Residential Parking Permit Program (RPPP) in North Central, emphasizing parking access for 
residents on Humboldt Street; explore a temporary rideshare pilot program for Humboldt Street residents 
pending potential implementation of an RPPP; include streetlighting upgrades on Humboldt Street, Idaho Street, 
and Fremont Street; implement traffic calming measures on Humboldt Street; and conduct a high-level 
feasibility analysis, including community outreach, regarding a potential one-way street configuration for 
Humboldt Street. Additionally, Council directed staff to incorporate findings from the bike boulevard concept 
designs into future bicycle planning and infrastructure projects and indicated it will consider appropriation of 
funding to implement these actions at a future meeting.

past and future events, along with updates. Council Member Cwirko-Godycki requested Council agendize a discussion 
item for Council to consider adopting resolution to recognize Glazenwood as an historic district prior to the new historic 
ordinance is adopted. This request received Council approval.

Special Meeting
 
CALL TO ORDER at 9:00 a.m.
Roll Call
Present: Mayor Loraine, Deputy Mayor Fernandez, Council Members: Cwirko-Godycki and Diaz Nash, Newsom
The mayor opened the workshop and outlined the purpose of the workshop and called for public comment.
PUBLIC COMMENT
Michael Ragan raised concerns regarding traffic safety, speeding, the 31st Avenue project, and impacts from the Hillsdale 
development. He urged prioritization of a speed survey, funding for traffic mitigation, and upgrades to the 42nd Avenue 
pump station, and thanked staff for communication. Teri Whitehair and Jayden Wan presented on the Elders Action 
Network and Future Design initiative, promoting multigenerational programs and long-term, future-focused community 
planning through role-playing and strategic visioning. Allison MacQueen advocated for expanded traffic calming, 
increased enforcement, “no right on red” restrictions near schools and downtown, and quarterly crash data review. Mike 
Swire supported advancing the Bike and Pedestrian Master Plan, improving parking management strategies, and 
increasing transparency regarding project prioritization. David Light thanked staff for pedestrian improvements, including 
leading pedestrian signals, flashing beacons, and speed monitoring efforts. Laurie Watanuki supported the Neighborhood 
Traffic Management Program and use of speed bumps as an effective traffic calming tool. Jordan Grimes emphasized 
housing affordability and urged accelerating housing production, advancing projects, and prioritizing pedestrian safety 
and traffic calming. Moises Guilleda encouraged inclusion of enhanced prequalification standards for publicly funded 
projects to support quality contractors and workforce development.
STUDY SESSION
1.
City Council “Blue Sky” Goal Setting Workshop
The City Council conducted a strategic priorities workshop. City Manager Alex Khojikian outlined the process and 
noted this was the first workshop held under the City’s newly adopted Strategic Plan. With facilitation by Shawn 
Spano, Council reviewed the 2026 Strategic Plan, evaluated the existing 2025–26 Council Priority List, and provided 
recommendations to remove items or convert them to the Strategic Plan’s SMART activities framework. Council 
then reviewed proposed 2026–27 departmental initiatives and discussed new priorities for 2026–27.
Council provided direction to staff on draft revisions and a proposed priority list. Staff will review each revision and 
evaluate resource investments. Staff will return with updates at a March 16 Study Session for public comment and 
further Council direction, with formal adoption anticipated at a future meeting in April or May 2026.
Council recessed from 11:13 am to 11:25 am; then broke for lunch at 12:15 pm and reconvened at 12:30 pm. 
ADJOURNMENT –The meeting adjourned at 1:36 p.m.

CITY OF SAN MATEO
CITY OF SAN MATEO
Agenda Report
City Hall
330 W. 20th Avenue
San Mateo CA 94403
www.cityofsanmateo.org
Agenda Number:  4
Section Name: CONSENT CALENDAR
Account Number: 82-4681
File ID: 26-10849
TO:
City Council
FROM:
Alex Khojikian, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
Public Works Department
MEETING DATE:
February 23, 2026 
SUBJECT:
Senior Center and Beresford Recreation Center Roof Improvements — Agreements
RECOMMENDATION:
Award agreements to Bartos Architecture, Inc. and Stantec Consulting Services, Inc. for design professional services for the 
Senior Center and Beresford Recreation Center Roof Improvements Projects in amounts not to exceed $120,820 and 
$141,776, respectively, with contingency reserves of $12,000 and $14,000, respectively; and authorize the Public Works 
Director to execute the agreements in substantially the form presented and issue amendments within the respective 
contingency amounts.
BACKGROUND:
The Senior Center has served the community for over thirty years and the Beresford Recreation Center has served the 
community for over sixty years.  Both facilities have roof systems that include flat gravel roofing and sloped wood shake 
shingles. Over decades of exposure to moisture, both the gravel and wood shake shingles at each facility have 
deteriorated, resulting in persistent leaks and ongoing maintenance challenges. Currently, multiple roof penetrations and 
areas of trapped moisture exist throughout the two facilities.
In Fiscal Year (FY) 2023–24, the City retained McGinnis Chen Associates, Inc. to conduct a Citywide roof assessment. The 
final report identified the Senior Center and Beresford Recreation Center, among other facilities, as posing risks due to 
compromised roof structures and recommended immediate corrective action.  In response, the City established a Capital 
Improvement Program project for roof replacements in 2024 for the following facilities identified in the report: Central 
Recreation Center (FY 2024–25), Beresford Recreation Center (FY 2025–26), Senior Center (FY 2025–26), and City Hall (FY 
2026–27).
In November 2025, staff issued a Request for Proposals (RFP) for remedial architectural services, including investigation, 
testing, design, construction documentation, bidding support, and construction administration. Six proposals were 
submitted in December 2025 and City staff evaluated them based on criteria including understanding of the project scope, 
experience with similar public-sector projects, and availability of qualified staffing. In January 2026, the City interviewed 
three firms and ultimately selected two: Bartos Architecture, Inc. for the Senior Center, and Stantec Consulting Services, 
Inc. for the Beresford Recreation Center.
Following the design efforts by both firms, staff anticipates securing building permits and awarding construction in early 
Summer 2026. Construction is anticipated to last approximately three months and must occur during dry weather. 
Therefore, it is critical that design documents are completed and permits obtained in advance of the construction window.  
Based on the above, staff recommends awarding agreements to both firms. 
19 of 719

--- page 20 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
BUDGET IMPACT:
There is sufficient funding in the Citywide Facilities Roof Replacements (46F056) project budget to fund these agreements 
and the proposed contingency reserve.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
These projects are categorically exempt from CEQA as an “existing facility,” because they consist of the operation, repair, 
maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of existing public or private structures, facilities, 
mechanical equipment, or topographical features, involving negligible or no expansion of existing or former use. (CEQA 
Guidelines Section 15301.)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

This Agreement, made and entered into this day of ____________________, by and between 
the CITY OF SAN MATEO, a municipal corporation existing under the laws of the State of California 
(“CITY”), and BARTOS ARCHITECTURE, INC., a Corporation (“CONSULTANT”), whose address is 300 8th 
Avenue, Suite 202, San Mateo, CA 94401.
RECITALS:
A.
CITY desires certain design professional services hereinafter described.
B.
CITY desires to engage CONSULTANT to provide these design professional services by 
reason of its qualifications and experience for performing such services and CONSULTANT has offered 
to provide the required services on the terms and in the manner set forth herein.
NOW, THEREFORE, IT IS AGREED as follows:
SECTION 1 - SCOPE OF SERVICES
The scope of services to be performed by CONSULTANT under this Agreement is as described in 
Exhibit A to this Agreement, attached and incorporated by reference.
SECTION 2 - DUTIES OF CONSULTANT
CONSULTANT shall be responsible for the professional quality, technical accuracy and 
coordination of all work furnished by CONSULTANT under this Agreement.  CONSULTANT shall, without 
additional compensation, correct or revise any errors or deficiencies in its work.
CONSULTANT represents that it is qualified to furnish the services described under this 
Agreement.
CONSULTANT shall be responsible for employing or engaging all persons necessary to perform 
the services of CONSULTANT.
CONSULTANT agrees to comply with the City’s minimum wage ordinance as codified in 
Municipal Code Chapter 5.92, which differs from the state minimum wage and includes a Consumer 
Price Index escalator.
SECTION 3 - DUTIES OF CITY
CITY shall provide pertinent information regarding its requirements for the project.

Rev 1/24
CITY shall examine documents submitted by CONSULTANT and shall render decisions pertaining 
thereto promptly, to avoid unreasonable delay in the progress of CONSULTANT's work.
SECTION 4 - TERM
The services to be performed under this Agreement shall commence upon execution of this 
agreement and be completed on December 31, 2026.
SECTION 5 - PAYMENT
Payment shall be made by CITY only for services rendered and upon submission of a payment 
request upon completion and CITY approval of the work performed.  In consideration for the full 
performance of the services set forth in Exhibit A, CITY agrees to pay CONSULTANT a fee in an amount 
of $120,820.00, pursuant to rates stated in Exhibit B to this Agreement, attached and incorporated by 
reference.
SECTION 6 - TERMINATION
Without limitation to such rights or remedies as CITY shall otherwise have by law, CITY shall have 
the right to terminate this Agreement or suspend work on the Project for any reason, upon ten (10) days' 
written notice to CONSULTANT.  CONSULTANT agrees to cease all work under this Agreement upon 
receipt of said written notice.
Upon termination and upon CITY's payment of the amount required to be paid, documents 
become the property of CITY, and CONSULTANT shall transfer them to CITY upon request without 
additional compensation.
SECTION 7 - OWNERSHIP OF DOCUMENTS
All documents prepared by CONSULTANT in the performance of this Agreement, although 
instruments of professional service, are and shall be the property of CITY, whether the project for 
which they are made is executed or not.
SECTION 8 - CONFIDENTIALITY
All reports and documents prepared by CONSULTANT in connection with the performance of 
this Agreement are confidential until released by CITY to the public.  CONSULTANT shall not make any 
such documents or information available to any individual or organization not employed by 
CONSULTANT or CITY without the written consent of CITY before any such release.
SECTION 9 - INTEREST OF CONSULTANT
CONSULTANT covenants that it presently has no interest, and shall not acquire any interest, 
direct or indirect, financial or otherwise, which would conflict in any manner or degree with the 
performance of the services under this Agreement.

SECTION 10 - CONSULTANT'S STATUS
It is expressly agreed that in the performance of the professional services required under this 
Agreement, CONSULTANT shall at all times be considered an independent CONSULTANT as defined in 
Labor Code Section 3353, under control of the CITY as to the result of the work but not the means by 
which the result is accomplished.  Nothing herein shall be construed to make CONSULTANT an agent or 
employee of CITY while providing services under this Agreement.
SECTION 11 - INDEMNITY
CONSULTANT agrees to hold harmless and indemnify CITY, its elected and appointed officials, 
employees, and agents from and against any and all claims, loss, liability, damage, and expense arising 
out of CONSULTANT’s negligence, recklessness or willful misconduct to the maximum extent permitted 
by state law.  To the extent permitted by Civil Code section 2782.8, CONSULTANT agrees to defend City, its 
elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents against any such claims. The CONSULTANT’S 
duty to indemnify shall survive expiration or early termination of this Agreement.
SECTION 12 - INSURANCE
CONSULTANT shall procure and maintain for the duration of the contract and three years 
thereafter (five years for building or major improvements) the insurance specified in Exhibit C to this 
Agreement.
SECTION 13 - NONASSIGNABILITY
Both parties hereto recognize that this Agreement is for the personal services of CONSULTANT 
and cannot be transferred, assigned, or subcontracted by CONSULTANT without the prior written 
consent of CITY.
SECTION 14 - RELIANCE UPON PROFESSIONAL
SKILL OF CONSULTANT
It is mutually understood and agreed by and between the parties hereto that CONSULTANT is 
skilled in the professional calling necessary to perform the work agreed to be done under this 
Agreement and that CITY relies upon the skill of CONSULTANT to do and perform the work in the most 
skillful manner, and CONSULTANT agrees to thus perform the work.  The acceptance of CONSULTANT's 
work by CITY does not operate as a release of CONSULTANT from said obligation.
SECTION 15 - WAIVERS
The waiver by either party of any breach or violation of any term, covenant, or condition of this 
Agreement or of any provisions of any ordinance or law shall not be deemed to be a waiver of such 
term, covenant, condition, ordinance or law or of any subsequent breach or violation of the same or of 
any other term, condition, ordinance, or law. The subsequent acceptance by either party of any fee or 
other money which may become due hereunder shall not be deemed to be a waiver of any preceding 
breach or violation by the other party of any term, covenant, or condition of this Agreement or of any

applicable law or ordinance.
SECTION 16 – SEVERABILITY
If any term or portion of this Agreement is held to be invalid, illegal, or otherwise 
unenforceable by a court of competent jurisdiction, the remaining provisions of this Agreement shall 
continue in full force and effect.
SECTION 17 - COSTS AND ATTORNEY FEES
Attorney fees in total amount not exceeding $5000, shall be recoverable as costs (by the filing 
of a cost bill) by the prevailing party in any action or actions to enforce the provisions of this 
Agreement.  The above $5000 limit is the total of attorney fees recoverable whether in the trial court, 
appellate court, or otherwise, and regardless of the number of attorneys, trials, appeals, or actions.  It 
is the intent of this Agreement that neither party shall have to pay the other more than $5000 for 
attorney fees arising out of an action, or actions to enforce the provisions of this Agreement.
SECTION 18 - NON-DISCRIMINATION
CONSULTANT warrants that it is an Equal Opportunity Employer and shall comply with 
applicable regulations governing equal employment opportunity.  Neither CONSULTANT nor any of its 
sub-consultants shall discriminate in the employment of any person because of race, color, national 
origin, ancestry, physical handicap, medical condition, marital status, sex, or age, unless based upon a 
bona fide occupational qualification pursuant to the California Fair Employment and Housing Act.
SECTION 19 - MEDIATION
Should any dispute arise out of this Agreement, any party may request that it be submitted to 
mediation.  The parties shall meet in mediation within 30 days of a request.  The mediator shall be 
agreed to by the mediating parties; in the absence of an agreement, the parties shall each submit one 
name from mediators listed by either the American Arbitration Association, the State Mediation and 
Conciliation Service, or other agreed-upon service.  The mediator shall be selected by a blind draw.
The cost of mediation shall be borne equally by the parties.  Neither party shall be deemed the 
prevailing party.  No party shall be permitted to file a legal action without first meeting in mediation 
and making a good faith attempt to reach a mediated settlement.  The mediation process, once 
commenced by a meeting with the mediator, shall last until agreement is reached by the parties but 
not more than 60 days, unless the maximum time is extended by the parties.

EXHIBIT C
INSURANCE REQUIREMENTS
MINIMUM SCOPE OF INSURANCE 
Coverage shall be at least as broad as:
1. Commercial General Liability (CGL):  Insurance Services Office (ISO) Form CG 00 01 12 07 
covering CGL on an “occurrence” basis, including products-completed operations, personal & 
advertising injury, with limits no less than $2,000,000 per occurrence. If a general aggregate limit 
applies, either the general aggregate limit shall apply separately to this project/location or the 
general aggregate limit shall be twice the required occurrence limit.
2. Automobile Liability:  ISO Form Number CA 00 01 covering any auto (Code 1), or if CONSULTANT 
has no owned autos, hired, (Code 8) and non-owned autos (Code 9), with limit no less than 
$1,000,000 per accident for bodily injury and property damage.
3. Workers’ Compensation: as required by the State of California, with Statutory Limits, and 
Employer’s Liability Insurance with limit of no less than $1,000,000 per accident for bodily injury 
or disease. 
4. Professional Liability (Errors and Omissions): Insurance appropriate to the CONSULTANT’s 
profession, with limit no less than $1,000,000 per occurrence or claim, $2,000,000 aggregate
If the CONSULTANT maintains higher limits than the minimums shown above, the City requires and shall 
be entitled to coverage for the higher limits maintained by the CONSULTANT.
Other Insurance Provisions
The insurance policies are to contain, or be endorsed to contain, the following provisions:
Additional Insured Status
The City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents are to be covered as insureds on 
the auto policy for liability arising out of automobiles owned, leased, hired or borrowed by or on behalf 
of the CONSULTANT; and on the CGL policy with respect to liability arising out of work or operations 
performed by or on behalf of the CONSULTANT including materials, parts or equipment furnished in 
connection with such work or operations. General liability coverage can be provided in the form of an 
endorsement to the CONSULTANT’s insurance (at least as broad as ISO Form CG 20 10, 11 85 or both 
CG 20 10 and CG 20 37 forms if later revisions used).
Primary Coverage
For any claims related to this contract, the CONSULTANT’s insurance coverage shall be primary 
insurance as respects the City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents. Any 
insurance or self-insurance maintained by the City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, or 
agents shall be excess of the CONSULTANT’s insurance and shall not contribute with it.
Notice of Cancellation
Each insurance policy required above shall provide that coverage shall not be canceled, except after 
thirty (30) days’ prior written notice (10 days for non-payment) has been given to the City. If a Notice 
of Cancellation endorsement is not available, CONSULTANT shall ensure the requisite Notice of 
32 of 719

--- page 33 ---
Design Professional Services 2024
Page 10 of 10
Rev 1/24
Cancellation is specified in the Certificate of Insurance.  In the event the Notice of Cancellation is not 
specified in the Certificate of Insurance, Consultant shall be responsible for providing the City the 
requisite notice as specified in this Exhibit C. 
Waiver of Subrogation
CONSULTANT hereby grants to City a waiver of any right to subrogation which any insurer of said 
CONSULTANT may acquire against the City by virtue of the payment of any loss under such insurance.  
CONSULTANT agrees to obtain any endorsement that may be necessary to effect this waiver of 
subrogation, but this provision applies regardless of whether or not the City has received a waiver of 
subrogation endorsement from the insurer.  
Deductibles and Self-Insured Retentions
Any deductibles or self-insured retentions must be declared to and approved by the City. The City may 
require the CONSULTANT to purchase coverage with a lower deductible or retention or provide proof 
of ability to pay losses and related investigations, claim administration, and defense expenses within 
the retention.  
Acceptability of Insurers
Insurance is to be placed with insurers with a current A.M. Best’s rating of no less than A:VII, unless 
otherwise acceptable to the City.
Verification of Coverage
CONSULTANT shall furnish the City with original certificates and amendatory endorsements or copies 
of the applicable policy language effecting coverage required by this clause.  All certificates and 
endorsements are to be received and approved by the City before work commences.  However, failure 
to obtain the required documents prior to the work beginning shall not waive the CONSULTANT’s 
obligation to provide them.  The City reserves the right to require complete, certified copies of all 
required insurance policies, including endorsements required by these specifications, at any time.

This Agreement, made and entered into this day of ____________________, by and between 
the CITY OF SAN MATEO, a municipal corporation existing under the laws of the State of California 
(“CITY”), and STANTEC CONSULTING SERVICES, INC., a Corporation (“CONSULTANT”), whose address is 
300 Montgomery Street, Suite 1200, San Francisco, CA 94104.
RECITALS:
A.
CITY desires certain design professional services hereinafter described.
B.
CITY desires to engage CONSULTANT to provide these design professional services by 
reason of its qualifications and experience for performing such services and CONSULTANT has offered 
to provide the required services on the terms and in the manner set forth herein.
NOW, THEREFORE, IT IS AGREED as follows:
SECTION 1 - SCOPE OF SERVICES
The scope of services to be performed by CONSULTANT under this Agreement is as described in 
Exhibit A to this Agreement, attached and incorporated by reference.
SECTION 2 - DUTIES OF CONSULTANT
CONSULTANT shall be responsible for the professional quality, technical accuracy and 
coordination of all work furnished by CONSULTANT under this Agreement.  CONSULTANT shall, without 
additional compensation, correct or revise any errors or deficiencies in its work.
CONSULTANT represents that it is qualified to furnish the services described under this 
Agreement.
CONSULTANT shall be responsible for employing or engaging all persons necessary to perform 
the services of CONSULTANT.
CONSULTANT agrees to comply with the City’s minimum wage ordinance as codified in 
Municipal Code Chapter 5.92, which differs from the state minimum wage and includes a Consumer 
Price Index escalator.
SECTION 3 - DUTIES OF CITY
CITY shall provide pertinent information regarding its requirements for the project.

CITY shall examine documents submitted by CONSULTANT and shall render decisions pertaining

written notice to CONSULTANT.  CONSULTANT agrees to cease all work under this Agreement upon

become the property of CITY, and CONSULTANT shall transfer them to CITY upon request without 
additional compensation.

All documents prepared by CONSULTANT in the performance of this Agreement, although 
instruments of professional service, are and shall be the property of CITY, whether the project for 
which they are made is executed or not.
SECTION 8 - CONFIDENTIALITY
All reports and documents prepared by CONSULTANT in connection with the performance of 
this Agreement are confidential until released by CITY to the public.  CONSULTANT shall not make any 
such documents or information available to any individual or organization not employed by 
CONSULTANT or CITY without the written consent of CITY before any such release.
SECTION 9 - INTEREST OF CONSULTANT
CONSULTANT covenants that it presently has no interest, and shall not acquire any interest, 
direct or indirect, financial or otherwise, which would conflict in any manner or degree with the 
performance of the services under this Agreement.

SECTION 10 - CONSULTANT'S STATUS
It is expressly agreed that in the performance of the professional services required under this

SECTION 11 - INDEMNITY
CONSULTANT agrees to hold harmless and indemnify CITY, its elected and appointed officials, 
employees, and agents from and against any and all claims, loss, liability, damage, and expense arising 
out of CONSULTANT’s negligence, recklessness or willful misconduct to the maximum extent permitted 
by state law.  To the extent permitted by Civil Code section 2782.8, CONSULTANT agrees to defend City, its 
elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents against any such claims. The CONSULTANT’S 
duty to indemnify shall survive expiration or early termination of this Agreement.
SECTION 12 - INSURANCE
CONSULTANT shall procure and maintain for the duration of the contract and three years 
thereafter (five years for building or major improvements) the insurance specified in Exhibit C to this 
Agreement.
SECTION 13 - NONASSIGNABILITY
Both parties hereto recognize that this Agreement is for the personal services of CONSULTANT 
and cannot be transferred, assigned, or subcontracted by CONSULTANT without the prior written 
consent of CITY.
SECTION 14 - RELIANCE UPON PROFESSIONAL
SKILL OF CONSULTANT
It is mutually understood and agreed by and between the parties hereto that CONSULTANT is 
skilled in the professional calling necessary to perform the work agreed to be done under this 
Agreement and that CITY relies upon the skill of CONSULTANT to do and perform the work in the most 
skillful manner, and CONSULTANT agrees to thus perform the work.  The acceptance of CONSULTANT's 
work by CITY does not operate as a release of CONSULTANT from said obligation.
SECTION 15 - WAIVERS
The waiver by either party of any breach or violation of any term, covenant, or condition of this 
Agreement or of any provisions of any ordinance or law shall not be deemed to be a waiver of such 
term, covenant, condition, ordinance or law or of any subsequent breach or violation of the same or of 
any other term, condition, ordinance, or law. The subsequent acceptance by either party of any fee or 
other money which may become due hereunder shall not be deemed to be a waiver of any preceding 
breach or violation by the other party of any term, covenant, or condition of this Agreement or of any

applicable law or ordinance.
SECTION 16 – SEVERABILITY
If any term or portion of this Agreement is held to be invalid, illegal, or otherwise 
unenforceable by a court of competent jurisdiction, the remaining provisions of this Agreement shall 
continue in full force and effect.
SECTION 17 - COSTS AND ATTORNEY FEES
Attorney fees in total amount not exceeding $5000, shall be recoverable as costs (by the filing 
of a cost bill) by the prevailing party in any action or actions to enforce the provisions of this 
Agreement.  The above $5000 limit is the total of attorney fees recoverable whether in the trial court, 
appellate court, or otherwise, and regardless of the number of attorneys, trials, appeals, or actions.  It 
is the intent of this Agreement that neither party shall have to pay the other more than $5000 for 
attorney fees arising out of an action, or actions to enforce the provisions of this Agreement.
SECTION 18 - NON-DISCRIMINATION
CONSULTANT warrants that it is an Equal Opportunity Employer and shall comply with 
applicable regulations governing equal employment opportunity.  Neither CONSULTANT nor any of its 
sub-consultants shall discriminate in the employment of any person because of race, color, national 
origin, ancestry, physical handicap, medical condition, marital status, sex, or age, unless based upon a 
bona fide occupational qualification pursuant to the California Fair Employment and Housing Act.
SECTION 19 - MEDIATION
Should any dispute arise out of this Agreement, any party may request that it be submitted to 
mediation.  The parties shall meet in mediation within 30 days of a request.  The mediator shall be 
agreed to by the mediating parties; in the absence of an agreement, the parties shall each submit one 
name from mediators listed by either the American Arbitration Association, the State Mediation and 
Conciliation Service, or other agreed-upon service.  The mediator shall be selected by a blind draw.
The cost of mediation shall be borne equally by the parties.  Neither party shall be deemed the 
prevailing party.  No party shall be permitted to file a legal action without first meeting in mediation 
and making a good faith attempt to reach a mediated settlement.  The mediation process, once 
commenced by a meeting with the mediator, shall last until agreement is reached by the parties but 
not more than 60 days, unless the maximum time is extended by the parties.

CITY OF SAN MATEO
CITY OF SAN MATEO
Agenda Report
City Hall
330 W. 20th Avenue
San Mateo CA 94403
www.cityofsanmateo.org
Agenda Number:  5
Section Name: CONSENT CALENDAR
Account Number: 14-1311
File ID: 26-10880
TO:
City Council
FROM:
Alex Khojikian, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
City Manager's Office
MEETING DATE:
February 23, 2026 
SUBJECT:
Commercial Property Management — Agreement
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve an agreement with Prodesse Property Group, in an amount not to exceed $125,000 for a term from March 1, 
2026 through February 28, 2029, with an optional two-year extension, to manage City-owned properties and authorize the 
City Manager to execute the agreement in substantially the form presented.
BACKGROUND:
The City owns and manages eight commercial properties in the Downtown. Tenants at the Main Street Garage include 
Beard Papa’s, Cold Stone Creamery, Fletch’s Taqueria, and Anton’s Patisserie, with one additional space currently vacant. 
Rise Woodfire Pizza is the primary tenant at the Transit Center and there is also one vacant space at that location. The 
Century Theatres is the long-term ground lessee on the block of B Street between 2nd and 3rd Avenues. The Downtown 
commercial property management contract excludes the ground lease with Century Theatres, as it is managed internally 
by staff.
In September 2025, the City issued a Request for Proposals (RFP) for commercial property management services and 
received a high level of interest, along with several competitive proposals. Following a competitive review, staff selected 
Prodesse Property Group as the City’s commercial property manager based on its property management expertise, 
customer service record, competitive rates, and ability to provide sound solicited and unsolicited advice about the 
commercial rental market.
Headquartered in San Mateo, Prodesse Property Group has 30 years of experience in property management and 
specializes in providing comprehensive property management services. The firm manages extensive residential investment 
properties and more than 250,000 square feet of retail, light industrial, medical-dental, and office space throughout the 
Bay Area. Michael Pierce, President, and Gilbert Sanchez, Commercial Portfolio Manager, are the principals of the 
company. Prodesse is currently on contract with the City and is familiar with the City’s property portfolio, including 
recurring conversations, and knows its current tenants and leasing documents. These factors combined with their 
experience, expertise, and pricing make Prodesse a sound choice from those who responded to the RFP. More information 
on pricing is described below.
The agreement includes an initial three-year term, with an option for one two-year extension. Prodesse submitted the 
most cost-effective proposal among the RFP responses and received the highest overall score for service. The management 
fee would continue at six percent (6%) of collected monthly gross income (base rent and CAM) from all managed 
properties and spaces, or a minimum of twelve thousand dollars ($12,000) annually, whichever is greater, and would be 
deducted from collected rental revenues.. In addition to the management fee, the agreement includes leasing and renewal 
fees, a per-unit monthly data access fee to cover software and data costs, and a construction management fee for projects 
47 of 719

--- page 48 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
exceeding $25,000 to account for additional staffing and oversight administered by Prodesse.  Such repairs would incur an 
administrative cost born by the City should they occur; however, this has not occurred in staff’s experience and is not 
expected, as major property repairs to City property would typically be administered by the City. 
BUDGET IMPACT:
Although the agreement authorizes a total amount not to exceed $125,000 over the term, no net budget impact is 
anticipated, as the costs paid to Prodesse are deducted from rental revenues collected for City-owned properties. 
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This agreement is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or administrative activity that will not 
result in direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(5).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

All documents and records prepared, maintained and stored by CONSULTANT on behalf of CITY 
in the performance of CONSULTANT’s duties hereunder, such as receipts, cancelled checks, and 
other records shall be stored in electronic digital format.  CONSULTANT’s obligation to store 
such electronic digital records shall end upon termination of this Agreement.
SECTION 8 - CONFIDENTIALITY
All reports and documents prepared by CONSULTANT in connection with the performance of 
this Agreement are confidential until released by CITY to the public. CONSULTANT shall not 
make any such documents or information available to any individual or organization not 
employed by CONSULTANT or CITY without the written consent of CITY before any such release.
SECTION 9 - INTEREST OF CONSULTANT
CONSULTANT covenants that it presently has no interest, and shall not acquire any interest, 
direct or indirect, financial or otherwise, which would conflict in any manner or degree with the 
performance of the services under this Agreement. 
SECTION 10 - CONSULTANT'S STATUS
It is expressly agreed that in the performance of the professional services required under this 
Agreement, CONSULTANT shall at all times be considered an independent contractor as defined 
in Labor Code Section 3353, under control of the CITY as to the result of the work but not the 
means by which the result is accomplished. Nothing herein shall be construed to make 
CONSULTANT an agent or employee of CITY while providing services under this Agreement.
SECTION 11 - INDEMNITY
CONSULTANT agrees to hold harmless and indemnify CITY, its elected and appointed officials, 
employees, and agents from and against any and all claims, loss, liability, damage, and expense 
arising out of CONSULTANT’s performance of this Agreement, except those claims arising out of 
CITY’s sole negligence or willful misconduct. CONSULTANT agrees to defend City, its elected and 
appointed officials, employees, and agents against any such claims. 
CITY agrees to hold harmless and indemnify CONSULTANT from and against any claims, loss, 
liability, damage, and expense arising out of the condition of the property being managed or 
acts of God(“Covered Claims”) ; except to the extent any such claims result from a breach of 
CONSULTANT’S duties hereunder or negligence or willful misconduct of CONSULTANT or its 
vendors, agents or employees, in which case CONSULTANT shall indemnify, hold harmless, and 
defend CITY. CITY agrees to defend CONSULTANT against any Covered Claims.
The provisions of the indemnification provision in Section 11 shall survive any cancellation, 
termination or expiration of this Agreement and shall remain in full force and effect until the

applicable statute of limitations shall have expired for all claims that are the subject of said

SECTION 12 - INSURANCE
CONSULTANT shall procure and maintain for the duration of the contract and three years 
thereafter (five years for building or major improvements) the insurance specified in Exhibit C 
to this Agreement. 
With regard to Covered Claims described above in Section 11, the CITY’s insurance will be 
primary for CONTRACTOR and CONTRACTOR will be listed as an additional insured.
SECTION 13 - NON-ASSIGNABILITY
Both parties hereto recognize that this Agreement is for the personal services of CONSULTANT 
and cannot be transferred, assigned, or subcontracted by CONSULTANT without the prior 
written consent of CITY. 
SECTION 14 - RELIANCE UPON PROFESSIONAL SKILL OF CONSULTANT
It is mutually understood and agreed by and between the parties hereto that CONSULTANT is 
skilled in the professional calling necessary to perform the work agreed to be done under this 
Agreement and that CITY relies upon the skill of CONSULTANT to do and perform the work in 
the most skillful manner, and CONSULTANT agrees to thus perform the work. The acceptance of 
CONSULTANT's work by CITY does not operate as a release of CONSULTANT from said 
obligation.
SECTION 15 - WAIVERS
The waiver by either party of any breach or violation of any term, covenant, or condition of this 
Agreement or of any provisions of any ordinance or law shall not be deemed to be a waiver of 
such term, covenant, condition, ordinance or law or of any subsequent breach or violation of 
the same or of any other term, condition, ordinance, or law. The subsequent acceptance by 
either party of any fee or other money which may become due hereunder shall not be deemed 
to be a waiver of any preceding breach or violation by the other party of any term, covenant, or 
condition of this Agreement or of any applicable law or ordinance. 
SECTION 16 - SEVERABILITY
If any term or portion of this Agreement is held to be invalid, illegal, or otherwise 
unenforceable by a court of competent jurisdiction, the remaining provisions of this Agreement 
shall continue in full force and effect. 
SECTION 17 - COSTS AND ATTORNEY FEES

Attorney fees in total amount not exceeding $5000, shall be recoverable as costs (by the filing 
of a cost bill) by the prevailing party in any action or actions to enforce the provisions of this 
Agreement. The above $5000 limit is the total of attorney fees recoverable whether in the trial 
court, appellate court, or otherwise, and regardless of the number of attorneys, trials, appeals, 
or actions. It is the intent of this Agreement that neither party shall have to pay the other more 
than $5000 for attorney fees arising out of an action, or actions to enforce the provisions of this 
Agreement. 
SECTION 18 - NON-DISCRIMINATION
CONSULTANT warrants that it is an Equal Opportunity Employer and shall comply with 
applicable regulations governing equal employment opportunity. Neither CONSULTANT nor any 
of its subcontractors shall discriminate in the employment of any person because of race, color, 
national origin, ancestry, physical handicap, medical condition, marital status, sex, or age, 
unless based upon a bona fide occupational qualification pursuant to the California Fair 
Employment and Housing Act.
SECTION 19 - MEDIATION
Should any dispute arise out of this Agreement, any party may request that it be submitted to 
mediation. The parties shall meet in mediation within 30 days of a request. The mediator shall 
be agreed to by the mediating parties; in the absence of an agreement, the parties shall each 
submit one name from mediators listed by either the American Arbitration Association, the 
State Mediation and Conciliation Service, or other agreed-upon service. The mediator shall be 
selected by a blind draw. 
The cost of mediation shall be borne equally by the parties. Neither party shall be deemed the 
prevailing party. No party shall be permitted to file a legal action without first meeting in 
mediation and making a good faith attempt to reach a mediated settlement. The mediation 
process, once commenced by a meeting with the mediator, shall last until agreement is reached 
by the parties but not more than 60 days, unless the maximum time is extended by the parties. 
SECTION 20 - LITIGATION
CONSULTANT shall testify at CITY'S request if litigation is brought against CITY in connection 
with CONSULTANT's services under this Agreement. Unless the action is brought by 
CONSULTANT, or is based upon CONSULTANT's wrongdoing, CITY shall compensate 
CONSULTANT for preparation for testimony, testimony, and travel at CONSULTANT's standard 
hourly rates at the time of actual testimony.

SECTION 26 – ADDITIONAL PROVISIONS: REPAIRS, SIGNS AND BANKING
CONSULTANT shall have the right to make repairs, emergency or otherwise, at the CITY’s 
request, for the benefit of the CITY’s properties.  All such repairs shall be performed at the 
CITY’s expense.
CONSULTANT shall have the right to place leasing signs on the properties CONSULTANT is 
managing for the CITY, to advertise vacant space(s) and to place other required signage as 
Federal, State and local ordinances require.
CONSULTANT shall have the right to establish a bank account for the CITY’s properties at a 
Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation (FDIC) insured bank selected by CONSULTANT (the 
Operating Account), to deposit income from the CITY’s properties and to pay property 
expenses. To ensure the orderly payment of bills, CONSULTANT shall maintain a cash reserve in 
the Operating Account equal to Five Thousand dollars ($5,000).  After meeting the cash 
reserve, on or before the 15th day of each calendar month, CONSULTANT shall transmit to CITY 
all other funds remaining in the Operating Account as of the end of the prior calendar month.  If 
the Operating Account has insufficient funds to pay the properties financial obligations, then 
such obligations will be paid in this order: insurance premiums; payroll and payroll taxes, 
Management Fee and reimbursements, mortgage payment; property taxes; utilities; operating 
expenses; reserves.

EXHIBIT A
SCOPE OF SERVICES
Prodesse shall provide the day-to-day commercial property management for the City’s 
currently owned ground floor commercial spaces within the Main Street Garage and the 
Transit Center in downtown San Mateo, which includes the current occupied and vacant 
properties at 2 N. B Street, 360 First Avenue, 380 First Avenue, 385 First Avenue, 385 
First Avenue, 390 First Avenue, 365 Second avenue, 275 Second Avenue and 385 
Second Avenue, plus additional City owned sites that the City may elect to place under 
management in the future.  These management functions would include, but not be 
limited to the following:
Tenant Management
•
Facilitate the execution of lease extensions, amendments, renewals and 
terminations with the prior approval of the City.
•
Maintain a relationship with each tenant to better know their needs and concerns, 
and to ensure tenant compliance with lease terms and conditions.  Provide 24-
hour response to any emergency situations, reporting such events to the City by 
the next business day at the latest.  Address any tenant issues with leases and/or 
occupied spaces.
•
Serve delinquency notices and assist the City with tenant eviction processes, 
whether for nonpayment of rent or violation of lease provision(s)
•
Advise the City on needed repairs to preserve long-term property value and make 
recommendations as to potential improvements that could enhance the value of 
the properties.
•
Walk the interior and exterior of the properties periodically after lease-required 
notices to the tenants, to ensure the maintenance of the properties, making sure 
they are safe and sanitary, and notifying the City of any identified leaks, damage 
or necessary repairs either within the premises or in the common areas of the 
Garage and Transit Center within one business day.
•
Represent the City in all matters relating to each tenant, under their lease 
agreement.
•
Record the water meter readings for the Transit Center, Main Street Garage and 
other managed properties on a monthly basis and report the same to City staff.
•
Walk any vacated properties and coordinate any necessary repairs so that the 
property shows well.
•
Collect monthly lease payments from tenants and remit to the City on a monthly 
basis net of property related expenses.
•
Oversee any maintenance work approved by the City, including ensuring all 
contractors have City-approved licenses and insurance coverage, and pay 
prevailing wages if required.
60 of 719

--- page 61 ---
AGREEMENT WITH PRODESSE PROPERTY GROUP
Page 10 of 14
Tenant Attraction/Leasing
•
Advertise vacancies on the properties and on commercial property listing sites and 
prepare all necessary marketing and advertising collateral materials.
•
Oversee minor repairs to prepare spaces for leasing.
•
Screen applicants for compatibility of use and check credit and references and 
make recommendations to City with regard to tenant selection.
•
Provide guidance to the City on fair market rent and tenant selection.  Final tenant 
selection and approval to be at the discretion and approval of the City Council.
•
Represent the City’s interest in lease negotiations as its broker and advisor for 
vacant spaces, including reviewing lease documents for conformity to those 
negotiations.
•
Ensure lease documents reflect negotiated terms and conditions and make 
recommendations on updating the City’s lease forms to align with industry 
standards.
Management Services, Reporting & Administration
•
Attend meetings with City staff, including those held outside of regular business 
hours, as requested.
•
Establish, manage and maintain a separate FDIC insured bank account for the 
deposit of rents and payment of operating expenses for the City’s leased 
properties, with a $5,000.00 reserve.
•
Provide monthly bank statements and reconciliations.
•
Provide itemized monthly reports and statements on property receipts, expenses 
and charges, including reports on rent collections, late fees, delinquencies, 
delinquency aging, and a rent roll including upcoming rent increases.  Reports to 
be delivered to the City by the 10th of each succeeding month.
•
Review annual possessory interest audit as requested by the City’s Finance 
Department for accuracy and completeness.
•
Prodesse uses the same Yardi Voyager software that much larger property 
management firms use, which generate the reports detailed below.
•
Provide other property management service support to the City, as needed.
As is detailed above, each month, consultant team would collect all rents from the tenants 
that are leasing space at the City’s properties in and around the train station and parking 
garage.  Consultant would serve as the primary contact for all tenants, helping to relieve 
that burden from City staff.  All rents collected would be deposited into a Trust account 
which consultant would establish and maintain for the City.  All tenant concerns regarding 
the property would be directed to consultant team and handled.
61 of 719

--- page 62 ---
AGREEMENT WITH PRODESSE PROPERTY GROUP
Page 11 of 14
Each month, consultant accounting team will prepare a monthly financial statement 
package that will include an income statement, balance sheet, general ledger, rent roll 
and 12-month rolling income statement, which will be forwarded electronically to the 
appropriate City staff members for review.  The net cash flow from the properties, 
following payment of all expenses and withholding the pre-determined $5,000.00 reserve 
amount will be deposited into the City’s designated bank account each month.
Prodesse will continue to provide the City with general property analysis and risk 
assessment based upon consultant observations from visits to the City’s properties, and 
ongoing review of the existing leases, utility bills and rent roll.  
PROJECT SCHEDULE & TIMELINE
Prodesse will continue providing services under this Agreement, with no interruption in 
services to the City.

AGREEMENT WITH PRODESSE PROPERTY GROUP
Page 11 of 14
63 of 719

--- page 64 ---
AGREEMENT WITH PRODESSE PROPERTY GROUP
Page 12 of 14
EXHIBIT B
FEE RATES
1. A management fee of 6.0% of the collected monthly gross income (base rent and 
CAM) from all properties and spaces managed, with a floor of $1,000.00 per 
month. 
2. A leasing fee for leases obtained for new tenants of 6.0% of the total base rent for 
lease years 1 and 2; 4.0% of the total base rent for lease years 3, 4 and 5; and 
3.0% of the total base rent for lease years 6 through 10.  These fees would be 
shared on a 50/50 basis with any procuring brokers.  For current tenants, a lease 
renewal fee of $1,000.00 for each lease that is renegotiated and/or renewed.
3. A Percentage Rent fee of 4%, calculated annually, will apply for Rise Wood-Fired 
Pizza on any Gross Sales exceeding $2,000,000.00; this structure shall also apply 
to any other tenant whose lease includes a percentage rent clause.  
4. A data access fee of $5.25 per unit, per month to offset Prodesse’s software 
licensing and support costs. Management may adjust the per unit per month fee 
from time to time in recognition of increased costs for the purchase, licensing, 
training and operation of property management software as well as the costs of 
data processing and data storage.  
5. A construction management fee of 5.0% of the total costs for any projects that 
exceed $25,000 to cover the additional staffing time and costs we would bear to 
manage large repairs and renovation projects at the properties.

EXHIBIT C
INSURANCE REQUIREMENTS
MINIMUM SCOPE OF INSURANCE 
Coverage shall be at least as broad as: 
1. Commercial General Liability (CGL): Insurance Services Office (ISO) Form CG 00 01 12 07 
covering CGL on an “occurrence” basis, including products-completed operations, personal & 
advertising injury, with limits no less than $1,000,000 per occurrence with a $2,000,000 general 
aggregate. 
2. Automobile Liability: ISO Form Number CA 00 01 covering any auto (Code 1), or if 
CONSULTANT has no owned autos, hired, (Code 8) and non-owned autos (Code 9), with limit no 
less than $1,000,000 per accident for bodily injury and property damage. 
3. Workers’ Compensation: as required by the State of California, with Statutory Limits, and 
Employer’s Liability Insurance with limit of no less than $1,000,000 per accident for bodily 
injury or disease. 
4. Professional Liability (Errors and Omissions): Insurance appropriate to the CONSULTANT’s 
profession, with limit no less than $1,000,000 per occurrence or claim, $2,000,000 aggregate. 
If the CONSULTANT maintains higher limits than the minimums shown above, the City requires 
and shall be entitled to coverage for the higher limits maintained by the CONSULTANT. 
Other Insurance Provisions 
The insurance policies are to contain, or be endorsed to contain, the following provisions: 
Additional Insured Status 
The City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents are to be covered as 
insureds on the auto policy for liability arising out of automobiles owned, leased, hired or 
borrowed by or on behalf of the CONSULTANT; and on the CGL policy with respect to liability 
arising out of work or operations performed by or on behalf of the CONSULTANT including 
materials, parts or equipment furnished in connection with such work or operations. General 
liability coverage can be provided in the form of an endorsement to the CONSULTANT’s 
insurance (at least as broad as ISO Form CG 20 10, 11 85 or both CG 20 10 and CG 20 37 forms if 
later revisions used). 
Primary Coverage 
For any claims related to this contract, the CONSULTANT’s insurance coverage shall be primary 
insurance as respects the City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents. Any 
66 of 719

--- page 67 ---
AGREEMENT WITH PRODESSE PROPERTY GROUP
Page 14 of 14
insurance or self-insurance maintained by the City, its elected and appointed officials, 
employees, or agents shall be excess of the CONSULTANT’s insurance and shall not contribute 
with it.
Notice of Cancellation 
Each insurance policy required above shall provide that coverage shall not be canceled, except 
after thirty (30) days’ prior written notice (10 days for non-payment) has been given to the 
City. 
Waiver of Subrogation 
CONSULTANT hereby grants to City a waiver of any right to subrogation which any insurer of 
said CONSULTANT may acquire against the City by virtue of the payment of any loss under such 
insurance. CONSULTANT agrees to obtain any endorsement that may be necessary to effect this 
waiver of subrogation, but this provision applies regardless of whether or not the City has 
received a waiver of subrogation endorsement from the insurer. 
Deductibles and Self-Insured Retentions 
Any deductibles or self-insured retentions must be declared to and approved by the City. The 
City may require the CONSULTANT to purchase coverage with a lower deductible or retention 
or provide proof of ability to pay losses and related investigations, claim administration, and 
defense expenses within the retention. 
Acceptability of Insurers 
Insurance is to be placed with insurers with a current A.M. Best’s rating of no less than A:VII, 
unless otherwise acceptable to the City. 
Verification of Coverage 
CONSULTANT shall furnish the City with original certificates and amendatory endorsements or 
copies of the applicable policy language effecting coverage required by this clause. All 
certificates and endorsements are to be received and approved by the City before work 
commences. However, failure to obtain the required documents prior to the work beginning 
shall not waive the CONSULTANT’s obligation to provide them. The City reserves the right to 
require complete, certified copies of all required insurance policies, including endorsements 
required by these specifications, at any time.
