SUBJECT: 
City of San Mateo and All Union/Association MOUs — Amendment 
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt a Resolution to amend the San Mateo City Employees’ Association (SMCEA), San Mateo Police Officers Association 
(SMPOA), San Mateo Police Sergeants Association (SMPSA), San Mateo Safety Management Association (SMSMA), San 
Mateo Management Employees’ Association (SMMEA), SEIU Library Merit Unit (SEIU LIB), SEIU Library Per Diem Unit (SEIU 
LIB PD), SEIU Maintenance Unit (SEIU M) and SEIU Non-Merit Per Diem Unit (SEIU NMPT) MOUs pertaining to the total 
compensation survey sections and approve side letters of agreement with SMCEA, SMPOA, SMPSA, SMSMA, SMMEA, SEIU 
LIB, SEIU LIB PD, SEIU M and SEIU NMPT for total compensation survey methodology and authorize the City and its 
unions/associations to execute the agreement in substantially the form presented.
BACKGROUND:
During the last contract negotiations with the various unions/associations, concerns were raised regarding how total 
compensation surveys were conducted. Specifically, certain benchmark classifications, total compensation survey 
components, and jurisdictions used in previous surveys were questioned. As a result, the City proposed a limited reopener 
for the purpose of reviewing the comparable city requirements and factors, including benchmark classifications to be used 
in future total compensation surveys during contract negotiations. After meeting and conferring since August 7, 2025, the 
City has reached agreement with all bargaining units over the Total Compensation Survey Methodology. As a result, we 
will now have a consistent and fair survey methodology aimed at attracting and retaining talent with competitive wages 
and benefits. Furthermore, all classifications will be linked to a relevant “Benchmark” classification within the same 
bargaining unit. A summary of the agreement is as follows: 
Jurisdictions 
The following twelve (12) cities will be surveyed from each of the following counties - San Mateo, Santa Clara, and 
Alameda, as follows: 
San Mateo County: 
• Daly City 
• Redwood City 
• South San Francisco 
Santa Clara County: 
• Milpitas 
• Mountain View 
• Palo Alto 
• Santa Clara 
68 of 719

--- page 69 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
Alameda County: 
• Alameda 
• Hayward 
• Livermore 
• San Leandro 
• Union City 
For the Wastewater Treatment Plant, the following eight (8) jurisdictions will be surveyed: 
• Dublin/San Ramon District 
• Hayward 
• North Sanitation District 
• Oro Loma 
• Palo Alto 
• Silicon Valley Clean Water 
• South San Francisco 
• Sunnyvale 
Data 
The following data will be included in the total compensation survey for each identified benchmark classification: 
• Top Step of Base Salary 
• Maximum Education/Certification Pay Premium when available to all employees in the classification 
• Employer’s Portion of Retirement Paid by the Employee (this amount is subtracted from the Total 
Compensation) - PEPRA Rate 
• Deferred Compensation 
• Cafeteria Plan 
• Health (Most Expensive Plan) 
• Dental 
• Vision 
• Life insurance
• Long-term Disability (LTD) 
• Social Security and/or Medicare (if applicable)
BUDGET IMPACT:
The recommended amendments regarding total compensation surveys for bargaining units does not have a quantifiable 
fiscal impact.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This side letter of agreements is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is a government fiscal activity which does not 
involve any commitment to any specific project which may result in a potentially significant physical impact on the 
environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(4).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

CITY OF SAN MATEO 
RESOLUTION NO. __ (2026) 
 
APPROVING AMENDMENTS TO THE MEMORANDUMS OF UNDERSTANDING BETWEEN THE CITY OF SAN 
MATEO AND ALL OF ITS UNIONS/ASSOCIATIONS   
 
WHEREAS, during the last contract negotiations with the San Mateo City Employees’ Association 
(SMCEA), San Mateo Police Officers’ Association (SMPOA),  San Mateo Police Sergeants’ Association (SMPSA),  
San Mateo Safety Management Association (SMSMA), San Mateo Management Employees’ Association 
(SMMEA), SEIU Library Merit Unit (SEIU LIB), SEIU Library Per Diem Unit (SEIU LIB PD), SEIU Maintenance Unit 
(SEIU M), and SEIU Non-Merit Part-Time Unit (SEIU NMPT), concerns were raised regarding how total 
compensation surveys were conducted; and 
 
WHEREAS, the City proposed a limited reopener for the purpose of reviewing the comparable city 
requirements and factors, including benchmark classifications to be used in future total compensation surveys 
during contract negotiations; and 
 
WHEREAS, the City’s intent was to have a survey methodology that was consistent citywide with the 
following goals:  
 
• 
consistent and fair survey methodology aimed at attracting and retaining talent with competitive wages 
and benefits.  
• 
All classifications to be linked to a relevant “Benchmark” classification within the same bargaining unit. 
No classifications will be left behind.  
• 
Ensure surveys include a “balanced approach” and relevant compensation components consistent with 
widely acceptable practices.  
• 
Identify jurisdictions to be surveyed that are comparable to the City of San Mateo.  
• 
All special adjustments are to be completed during upcoming contract negotiations.  
• 
Limit existing language in the Memorandum of Understanding (“MOU”) related to classification and 
compensation review to classification studies only. The purpose of the classification study is to: 1) 
determine the appropriate classification based on the duties performed on a permanent, regular and 
consistent basis, and 2) allocate such position(s) to the appropriate classification.   
 
WHEREAS, the parties have met and conferred and reached agreement; and 
 
WHEREAS, the side letters of agreement are attached hereto as Exhibit A. 
 
 
NOW, THEREFORE, THE CITY COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF SAN MATEO, CALIFORNIA, HEREBY RESOLVES 
that:  
 
1. The side letters of agreement are not subject to California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA 
Guidelines Section 15061(b)(3)) because it can be seen with certainty that it will not cause a physical 
change in the environment. 
2. The side letters of agreement are attached hereto as Exhibit A and incorporated by reference.  
3. The side letters of agreement are approved.

will be amended to include Side Letters of Agreement Between the City and its unions/associations 
pertaining to the total compensation survey methodology.

Total Compensation Survey Methodology 
 
 
The Total Compensation Survey Methodology outlined below will be applied during upcoming contract 
negotiations with the SEIU Non-Merit Part-Time Unit. 
 
 
1. Jurisdictions 
The following twelve (12) cities will be surveyed from each of the following counties- San Mateo, 
Santa Clara, and Alameda, as follows:  
 
San Mateo County 
Santa Clara County 
Alameda County  
Daly City 
Milpitas 
Alameda 
Redwood City 
Mountain View 
Hayward  
South San Francisco  
Palo Alto  
Livermore 
 
Santa Clara 
San Leandro  
 
 
Union City  
 
The cities above were identified based on the services provided, geographic proximity, budget, full-
time equivalent positions, and population.  
 
2. Data  
The following data will be included in the total compensation survey for each identified benchmark 
classification:  
• 
Top Step of Base Salary  
• 
Maximum Education/Certification Pay Premium when available to all employees in the 
classification 
• 
Employer’s Portion of Retirement Paid by the Employee (this amount is subtracted from the 
Total Compensation)- PEPRA Rate   
• 
Deferred Compensation 
• 
Cafeteria Plan  
• 
Health (Most Expensive Plan)  
• 
Dental 
• 
Vision 
• 
Life insurance, and  
• 
Long-term Disability (LTD)  
• 
Social Security and/or Medicare (if applicable) 
 
The data will be compiled and provided to the union/association during contract negotiations, but no 
later than two (2) months prior to the expiration of the Memorandum of Understanding. The market 
median for benchmark classifications is determined by a total compensation survey of the comparable 
benchmark classifications using those cities identified above under section #1. At the time the survey 
is finalized, the survey shall include known and approved future increases in wages and benefits from 
other cities that are subject to implementation within thirty (30) calendar days.

time equivalent positions, and population.

classification:  
•

• 
Maximum Education/Certification Pay Premium when available to all employees in the 
classification

other cities that are subject to implementation within thirty (30) calendar days.

Agenda Report
City Hall
330 W. 20th Avenue
San Mateo CA 94403
www.cityofsanmateo.org
Agenda Number:  7
Section Name: OLD BUSINESS
Account Number: 
File ID: 26-10873
TO:
City Council
FROM:
Alex Khojikian, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
Public Works Department
MEETING DATE:
February 23, 2026 
SUBJECT:
Grand Boulevard Initiative and Central El Camino Real Plan — Appropriation
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt a Resolution to appropriate $70,000 of the City’s Measure A funds to support additional San Mateo-specific studies 
and community engagement; and receive an informational presentation on the Central El Camino Multimodal Plan being 
developed in partnership with SamTrans and the Cities of San Mateo, Belmont, San Carlos, and Redwood City.
BACKGROUND:
In February 2025, SamTrans launched the Central El Camino Real Multimodal Plan (Central El Camino Plan), a corridor 
study for an 8.5-mile segment of El Camino Real (Caltrans State Route 82) within the cities of San Mateo, Belmont, San 
Carlos and a short segment of Redwood City. 
This Central El Camino Plan is a part of a broader effort called the Grand Boulevard Initiative (GBI), which is a collaboration 
in San Mateo County between 15 local jurisdictions, four countywide agencies, and Caltrans. This effort is supporting cities 
along the corridor by leading a joint, coordinated Caltrans process to advance local city visions. GBI aims to make El 
Camino Real a safer and more comfortable place to walk, bike, drive, and take transit by installing streetscape 
improvements along the corridor.
Over the past year, GBI convened a Task Force and a Working Group comprised of staff representing all cities and various 
agencies and organizations along the corridor in a series of workshops. These groups provide direction on corridor-wide 
challenges and needs, potential solutions, public and stakeholder engagement, and the vision for the corridor. These 
groups meet bi-monthly and SamTrans used this feedback to develop a GBI Action Plan. 
The GBI Action Plan is a planning document that evaluates corridor-wide needs, establishes a cohesive vision, and builds 
momentum toward implementation. Once the Action Plan is complete, SamTrans will lead the first phase of a coordinated 
Caltrans project development process, called a Project Initiation Document (PID), for all of El Camino Real in San Mateo 
County. The PID will define the purpose and need for transportation improvements to the corridor as well as establish 
potential design alternatives. 
Because El Camino Real is a state highway, Caltrans has the final authority over design approvals, right-of-way changes, 
signal modifications, and construction standards. No changes can be implemented on the state highway without Caltrans 
review and approval, so SamTrans must coordinate closely with Caltrans throughout the PID process.
Central El Camino Real Plan:
The Central El Camino Plan identifies corridor needs, defines alternatives, and selects alternative(s) to be advanced to the 
next phase based on an analysis of tradeoffs as well as community and stakeholder feedback. This will allow San Mateo to 
97 of 719

--- page 98 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
identify selected alternative(s) for inclusion in the countywide PID led by GBI. 
El Camino Real is the main arterial of San Mateo County, connecting downtowns, businesses, schools, regional transit 
hubs, and other key destinations. While El Camino Real serves a range of trip purposes and users, its design reflects its 
historic role as a state highway focused on facilitating high speed auto travel. Consequently, the corridor is a barrier for 
pedestrians and cyclists and is challenging to navigate efficiently for buses. 
Undertaking the Central El Camino Plan provides an opportunity to build upon San Mateo’s efforts related to El Camino 
Real over the years, including its TOD Pedestrian Access Plan (2022), Bicycle Master Plan (2020), and ongoing Complete 
Streets Plan. In outreach conducted during these plans, San Mateo residents have indicated concerns related to the heavy 
traffic volumes, including the lack of safe routes to schools, high-speed traffic, and a lack of bicycle and pedestrian 
facilities. Themes from past outreach conducted for El Camino Real include: 
•
Complete Streets: safety and connectivity gaps for walking and bicycling 
•
Pedestrian Access: difficult crossings, narrow/obstructed sidewalks, and limited bus stop amenities 
•
Safety: concerns about speeding, red light running, and crossing conditions 
The Central El Camino Plan incorporates this input and gathered additional feedback on the Plan through conducting pop-
ups, community-based organization meetings, and listening sessions. The Central El Camino Plan identifies similar findings 
in Phase 1 of engagement, including the need for bus stop amenities and higher bus frequencies, safer pedestrian 
crossings and wider and better maintained sidewalks, more pedestrian-scale lighting, separated bike facilities, and better 
bus connections to Caltrain stations. This engagement, as well as findings from local and regional plans, was used to define 
potential design alternatives for El Camino Real.
Historically, implementing changes on this Caltrans facility has been difficult for cities with only a patchwork of 
improvements occurring over the past several decades. The development of the Central El Camino Plan is essential for San 
Mateo, in partnership with SamTrans and neighboring cities, to advance roadway safety and active transportation 
improvements. The Central El Camino Plan will fill a critical corridor planning gap that is necessary for San Mateo and its 
neighboring cities to complete the requisite planning for the first stage of the Caltrans project development process being 
led through GBI. 
Relevance to Caltrans Paving Project:
It is important to note that the Central El Camino Real Plan and GBI are separate from the 04-4W730 State Highway 
Operation and Protection Program (SHOPP) project, which involves pavement resurfacing and upgrading of curb ramps to 
Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA) standards on State Route 82 / El Camino Real from 43rd Avenue to E. Santa Inez 
Avenue in the City of San Mateo. The 04-4W730 SHOPP project is led by Caltrans, but City staff and SamTrans staff have 
been engaged throughout the project development process. This collaboration is critical to ensure that spot improvements 
that are safety critical are incorporated in the repaving effort as they can be delivered on a faster timeline.
Feedback Received from Sustainability and Infrastructure Commission:
SamTrans presented the Central El Camino Real Plan to the Sustainability and Infrastructure Committee (SIC) during Phase 
1 of outreach on July 9, 2025. The following feedback was received:
•
Commissioners encouraged SamTrans staff consider prohibiting and/or restricting left turns during rush hours in 
the downtown areas of the City on El Camino Real. SamTrans staff confirmed that this is a feature that is being 
considered as part of the Central El Camino Real Plan.  
•
Commissioners reinforced accessibility and narrow sidewalk concerns and curb cut issues. 
SamTrans presented the Grand Boulevard Initiative to SIC on October 8, 2025. The following feedback was received:
•
Commissioners expressed support for the Grand Boulevard Initiative but frustration over how long planning has 
taken. They raised concerns about El Camino’s poor pavement, traffic backups, and the need to explore limiting 
left turns during peak hours. They emphasized challenges around adding protected bike lanes due to parking or 
lane-removal trade-offs. Finally, they asked whether a full feasibility study of transit, biking, and walking 
improvements on El Camino has ever been done.
98 of 719

--- page 99 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
Public Engagement: 
SamTrans, in collaboration with the cities of San Mateo, Belmont, San Carlos and Redwood City, is conducting public 
engagement in three phases: 
•
Phase 1 (Spring/Summer 2025): Identify corridor gaps and needs 
•
Phase 2 (Winter 2026): Present corridor alternatives and trade-offs 
•
Phase 3 (Summer 2026): Present preferred alternative and implementation plan
Phase 1 engagement included community pop-ups, meetings with community-based organizations, multilingual listening 
sessions, presentations, and an online interactive mapping tool along the corridor limits: 
https://fp.mysocialpinpoint.com/central-el-camino-plan. For San Mateo, SamTrans hosted a pop-up table on the 4th of 
July at Central Park. A total of 310 comments collected through the Phase 1 engagement interactive mapping tool related 
to San Mateo.
Phase 2 engagement will run from January – February 2026 and will include the following activities in San Mateo listed 
below:

newsletters, printed materials
The online survey provides cross section examples for the cities of San Mateo, Belmont, San Carlos and Redwood City, and 
prompts the user to select which alternative they prefer. For the City of San Mateo, the alternatives are as follows: 
•
Existing: What El Camino Real looks like today
•
Transit: Faster bus service, wider sidewalks, and more trees
•
Pedestrian: Wider sidewalks and more trees
•
Bicycle: Continuous separated bike lanes, wider sidewalks, and more trees
Spot improvements which are anticipated to be included in all build alternatives are as follows:
•
Safety
o
Traffic calming
o
Traffic signal gap closures
o
Medians
o
Lighting
•
Active Transportation
o
Crosswalk improvements
o
More opportunities for crossing

--- page 100 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
o
Street trees or urban greening
•
Transit
o
Bus stop enhancements
o
Transit signal priority
o
Access improvements
The focus of Phase 3 of the engagement process is to gather further input from City Council and stakeholders, to assist in 
selecting alternatives to be advanced into the Caltrans project development process. 
The proposed alternatives have the potential to remove parking along El Camino Real. In Spring, City staff is proposing to 
supplement SamTrans’s engagement process by conducting an expanded parking occupancy study along El Camino Real to 
build upon SamTrans’s August 2025 parking occupancy study and to provide door-to-door engagement for businesses and 
gather additional feedback about the proposed alternatives. Staff is requesting a $70,000 appropriation of Measure A 
funds to support this effort.  Feedback from this data gathering efforts will be summarized and presented to City Council 
together with the findings of the Phase 2 Engagement effort later this year. A resolution is proposed to appropriate these 
Measure A funds. If funds are not appropriated, staff would be reliant on the August 2025 parking occupancy study, which 
evaluated parking conditions only along El Camino Real and did not assess side-street or on-site parking capacity. Staff 
would also be unable to conduct door-to-door business engagement or support other additional data collection to inform 
Council’s selection of a preferred alternative.
More information about the project, the online survey, and upcoming engagement efforts is available at: 
https://samtrans.com/CentralElCamino.
Next Steps:
Following completion of the Phase 2 engagement period, in late Spring or early Summer 2026, SamTrans staff will return 
to City Council with a summary of the feedback received through this community engagement and seek feedback from the 
City Council to select an alternative or alternatives for inclusion in the countywide PID led by GBI. 
It is anticipated that it could take up to 8-10 years for full implementation of the project including construction, following 
completion of the Central El Camino Plan. 
BUDGET IMPACT:
The Central El Camino Plan is funded by SamTrans and a Transit Performance Initiative (TPI) grant awarded by the 
Metropolitan Transportation Commission (MTC). The study cost is $732,000, funded by a $407,000 MTC Transit 
Performance Initiative grant and $325,000 in SamTrans funds. 
The GBI Action Plan and Project Initiation Document are funded by SamTrans, through a $2 million grant from the MTC, as 
well as a $250,000 grant of local transportation sales tax dollars from the San Mateo County Transportation Authority. 
The City is providing in-kind staff support. Funding for in-kind staff time is supported by the Regional & Citywide 
Transportation Program Management (46r030) project. Funding for supplemental outreach for the Central El Camino Real 
project beyond in-kind staff time was not included in the Fiscal Year 2025-26 project budget but is recommended through 
the requested appropriation of $70,000 in the City’s Measure A funds to support a more robust data gathering and 
community engagement effort as part of this project. 
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This Council action is exempt from CEQA, as it involves feasibility or planning studies for future City actions which has not 
been approved, adopted, or funded. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15262.)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

RESOLUTION NO. ____ (2026)
AUTHORIZING AN APPROPRIATION of $70,000 FROM THE CITY’S MEASURE A FUNDS TO THE REGIONAL & 
CITYWIDE TRANSPORTATION PROGRAM MANAGEMENT PROJECT IN THE CAPITAL PROJECTS FUND
WHEREAS, in February 2025, SamTrans launched the Central El Camino Real Multimodal Plan (Central El 
Camino Plan), a corridor study for an 8.5-mile segment of El Camino Real (Caltrans State Route 82) within the 
cities of San Mateo, Belmont, San Carlos, and a short segment of Redwood City; and
WHEREAS, the Central El Camino Plan is part of a broader effort called the Grand Boulevard Initiative 
(GBI), which aims to make El Camino Real a safer and more comfortable place to walk, bike, drive, and take 
transit by installing streetscape improvements along the corridor; and
WHEREAS, the City would like to supplement SamTrans’s community engagement efforts in Fiscal Year 
2025-26; and  
WHEREAS, an appropriation of $70,000 from Measure A funds to the Project account is necessary to 
appropriately allocate the funds to finance the Project.
NOW, THEREFORE, THE CITY COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF SAN MATEO, CALIFORNIA, HEREBY RESOLVES 
that:
1.
This Council action is exempt from CEQA, because it involves feasibility or planning studies for future 
City actions which has not been approved, adopted, or funded.. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15262.) 
2.
An appropriation of $70,000 of the City’s Measure A funds to the Regional & Citywide 
Transportation Program Management Project (46r030) in the Capital Projects Fund is authorized.

A Brief History of El Camino Real
(State Route 82)
5
1920s
El Camino Real paved 
as the Peninsula’s first 
highway
1940s-1970s
101 and 280 freeways built, 
shifting regional travel away 
from El Camino Real
2006
The Grand Boulevard Initiative 
(GBI) launched to transform the 
built environment on 
El Camino Real
2024
SamTrans reassembles GBI 
focused on advancing 
transportation improvements 
in San Mateo County 
109 of 719

--- page 110 ---
What is the Grand Boulevard Initiative?
6
Grand Boulevard Initiative
The Grand Boulevard Initiative (GBI) is a partnership 
between cities, countywide agencies, Caltrans, advocates, 
and business groups.
The purpose of GBI is to establish a cohesive countywide vision 
for modernizing El Camino Real (SR 82) to improve safety and 
mobility.
110 of 719

--- page 111 ---
GBI Vision
El Camino Real is a safe and 
vibrant street where people of 
all ages and abilities travel 
comfortably.
7
A ‘safe street’ eliminates fatalities and serious injuries by managing 
speeds, conflicts, and travel behavior.
A ‘vibrant street’ supports local businesses, strengthens sense of 
community, and is a place people want to spend time.
‘All ages and abilities’ means that everyone has the freedom to travel 
comfortably, including children, seniors, and people with disabilities.
111 of 719

--- page 112 ---
GBI Builds Upon Local Corridor Studies
Every city in San Mateo County has been working on complete streets studies for El 
Camino.
In parallel, GBI serves as a forum for collaboration and continuity across these local 
studies.
8

--- page 115 ---
The Central El Camino Real Multimodal Plan: 
Envisioning a Vibrant Corridor
Project Overview and Purpose
The Central El Camino Plan is working to create a shared 
vision for El Camino Real across the cities of San Mateo, 
Belmont, San Carlos, and a small portion of Redwood City
The goal of this project is to improve El Camino to better serve 
people walking, biking, and taking transit
The Plan will be advanced as part of a countywide Caltrans 
project development process facilitated by GBI
11

Relevance to Caltrans Repaving Project
•
This Plan is separate from Caltrans State Highway 
Operation and Protection Program (SHOPP) EA-04-4W730
•
Caltrans’ project extents are from El Camino Real 
from 43rd Ave to E. Santa Inez 
•
Caltrans’ SHOPP project is a $34M repaving project with a 
planned completion in 2030 that includes:
•
140 curb ramp upgrades 
•
Two traffic signal upgrades (Hobart/12th, Hillsdale)
•
Two pedestrian hybrid beacons at Monte Diablo and 
10th Avenue
•
City staff and SamTrans have been engaged throughout 
Caltrans’ project

What is the
Central El Camino Real
Multimodal Plan?

Project Factsheet

INITIATIVE

April 2025

About the Project Project Area

Originally designed as a state highway for The Plan focuses on San Mateo, Belmont, San
high-speed auto travel, El] Camino Real (El Carlos, and a small portion of Redwood City.
Camino) now faces challenges in providing a

safe and efficient trip for the many people “hy. A
using it every day. As the main roadway that :

connects San Mateo County, El Camino needs NN

essential improvements to better connect
people to key downtowns, businesses, schools,
transit stations, and other destinations.

Led by SamTrans and the cities of San Mateo,
Belmont, San Carlos, and Redwood City, the
Central El Camino Real Multimodal Plan
(Central El Camino Plan) is a planning project
that will improve transportation and safety
along an eight and a half-mile stretch of El
Camino.

The Plan aims to transform El Camino
into a safe, inviting, connected, and
transit-oriented boulevard.

This project is funded by SamTrans and a Central El Camino \Y
Transit Performance Initiative (TPI) grant Study Area %
awarded by the Metropolitan Transportation — El Camino Real ;

Commission (MTC).

--- page 140 ---
Connection to Other
Planning Efforts

The Central El Camino Plan builds on
local and countywide efforts and the
Grand Boulevard Initiative (GBI), to
create a clear vision of the corridor
that meets both local and countywide
needs for all types of transportation.

The design alternatives that will be
identified as part of the Central El
Camino Plan will be used to start the
Caltrans project development process.
The first step of this process, called a
Project Initiation Document (PID), is a
critical step in getting future funding
from the state. The Grand Boulevard
Initiative (GBI) will help cities along El
Camino through this PID process.

Que es el

Central El Camino Real
Multimodal Plan?

Ficha técnica del proyecto
Abril de 2025

Acerca del Proyecto

Originalmente disefhada como una carretera
estatal para el transporte vehicular de alta
velocidad, El Camino Real (El Camino) ahora
enfrenta desafios para brindar un viaje seguro y
eficiente a las numerosas personas que la
utilizan a diario. Como la principal via que
conecta el condado de San Mateo, El Camino
necesita mejoras esenciales para conectar mejor
a las personas con centros urbanos, negocios,
escuelas, estaciones de transporte publico y
otros destinos clave.

Liderado por SamtTrans y las ciudades de San
Mateo, Belmont, San Carlos y Redwood City, el
Central El Camino Real Multimodal Plan es un
proyecto de planificacién que mejorara el
transporte y la seguridad a lo largo de un tramo
de ocho millas y media de El Camino.

EI plan tiene como objetivo transformar El
Camino en un bulevar seguro, acogedor,
conectado y orientado al transito.

Este proyecto esta financiado por SamTrans y
una subvencion de la Iniciativa de Desempeno
de Transito otorgada por la Comisién de
Transporte Metropolitano.

de caminar, andar en

en El Camino

principales problemas de
seguridad como parte de la

CITY OF SAN MATEO
 
CITY OF SAN MATEO
Agenda Report
City Hall
330 W. 20th Avenue

Agenda Number:  8

TO:
City Council
FROM:
Alex Khojikian, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
Community Development Department
MEETING DATE:
February 23, 2026 
SUBJECT:
Comprehensive Zoning Code Update Project — Direction 
RECOMMENDATION:
Receive a presentation on the Comprehensive Zoning Code Update Project, receive public comments, and provide staff with 
direction on how to proceed with updating the City’s Zoning Code.
BACKGROUND:
Project Overview and Objectives
Strive San Mateo General Plan 2040 (“General Plan”) was adopted by the City Council on March 18, 2024. The General Plan 
is the City’s long-range planning document that guides decision-making and establishes policies and standards for future 
development and improvements citywide. The General Plan includes a Vision Statement and Guiding Principles, along with 
goals, policies, and actions that outline how the City will address key community issues such as land use, circulation, public 
services and facilities, and housing. The General Plan includes several implementation programs which outline necessary 
amendments to the City’s Zoning Code. In November 2024, San Mateo voters passed Measure T, which increased height and 
density limits within ten specified study areas, and allowed for the full implementation of the General Plan’s Land Use 
Element. Building on the foundation of the General Plan, the Planning Division is working towards a comprehensive update 
of the City’s existing Zoning Code (Title 27). This will be the first comprehensive update of the City's Zoning Code in over 40 
years. This update would include implementation of all applicable goals and policies in the General Plan, the 2023-2031 
Housing Element (“Housing Element”), as well as address compliance with State law, meet current best practices and include 
internal consistencies for an effective and user-friendly Zoning Code. 
 
Over time, the City's Zoning Code has been updated and amended in various ways. While these updates addressed the City's 
evolving needs, they have also resulted in inconsistencies and redundancies within other sections of the Code. These issues 
will be addressed as part of this comprehensive update. Beyond General Plan implementation, this comprehensive update 
will focus on alignment with the Housing Element; establishing a clear, intuitive, and user-friendly framework; eliminating 
conflicts to create an internally consistent document; complying with recent state legislation; modernizing the Zoning Code 
to reflect best practices; and focusing on streamlining the entitlement process. The update will also support balanced and 
equitable housing production, economic development, transportation access, and sustainability. Additionally, this effort will 
include an updated zoning map to ensure consistency and usability for the property owners, applicants, and City staff. 
The Comprehensive Zoning Code Update Project will focus primarily on Title 27 (Zoning) of the San Mateo Municipal Code 
(SMMC). This title is most frequented by City staff and the public; thus the goal is to create a user-friendly Zoning Code that 
will make it easier for all stakeholders to navigate and understand the development regulations in our community. This study 
session is intended to provide an update on the progress made and the overall project timeline, summarize the outreach 
and engagement strategy, and seek high level direction on several key topics.
145 of 719

--- page 146 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
This report focuses and summarizes the major updates, and principal conclusions based on input gathered from interviews 
and discussions with City staff, stakeholder surveys to date, and a review of the current Zoning Code, General Plan, Housing 
Element, and applicable provisions of State law, as well as research on best practices from other jurisdictions. 
The focus has been on the following topics: 
•
Code Accessibility and Usability
•
Administrative Procedures
•
Land Use Regulations
•
Zoning Districts and Development Standards
Related Planning Efforts
As part of the Zoning Code Update, the team is reviewing the various adopted plans that interact and/or are referenced in 
the Zoning Code, such as specific plans, area plans and master plans, to assess what needs be updated in the Zoning Code.  
The team will also assess each of these adopted plans for consistency with the General Plan and identify what needs to be 
updated, but the work to bring each plan into alignment with the General Plan will happen separately from this Project.
There are other related planning efforts that are happening separately but will be incorporated into the Zoning Code Update. 
Such efforts include the following:
ADU Ordinance 
The City's Accessory Dwelling Unit (ADU) Ordinance (SMMC Chapter 27.19) was just recently updated to comply with State 
law, address local needs and improve ordinance functionality. This updated chapter will be incorporated into the Zoning 
Code but is not planned for any further updates or amendments at this time.
Historic Preservation Ordinance
Staff is currently working on amendments to the City’s Historic Preservation Ordinance (SMMC Chapter 27.66), which will 
set updated requirements and procedures for designation and administration of historic resources. This work is part of the 
implementation of the General Plan and will be incorporated into the Zoning Code once the effort is completed. 
Housing Element
The Housing Element includes multiple policies and programs that require Zoning Code amendments to implement. For code 
amendments that have already happened, this work will be incorporated into the Zoning Code. For other necessary code 
amendments that have not happened yet, this work is being incorporated into the Zoning Code Update.
High Density Zone Districts
The current Zoning Code does not allow for the higher-density residential and mixed-use development envisioned by the 
General Plan through the establishment of the Mixed-Use Medium II, Mixed-Use High, Residential Medium II, and Residential 
High land use designations. It was necessary to establish new zoning districts to allow for timely implementation of the 
General Plan’s adopted land use designations. The proposed high density zone ordinance would implement the General 
Plan’s land use framework by establishing clear and objective standards for permitted uses, development form, and 
intensity. The work on this ordinance is ongoing, and staff anticipates that this effort will move forward in the first half of 
2026 ahead of the Zoning Code Update.
Zoning Code Update Timeline
In March of 2025, staff issued a Request for Proposals (RFP) for a qualified consultant to support the Comprehensive Zoning 
Code Update Project. Staff received a total of four proposals in response to the RFP. Based on overall professional 
experience, quality of proposal, responses to interview questions and composition of the consultant team, staff identified 
PlaceWorks as being the best qualified to support the Zoning Code Update. On June 16, 2025, the City Council voted to 
appropriate funding to complete the Zoning Code Update Project and to execute a professional services agreement with 
PlaceWorks to support this effort. 
Following approval, PlaceWorks and City staff held the project kick-off meeting beginning of July, 2025.  Since the kick-off 
meeting, PlaceWorks has worked closely with staff in assessing the current Zoning Code regulations and other background 
146 of 719

--- page 147 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
research. After a full review and assessment of the existing regulations, the team, in coordination with the City Attorney’s 
Office, developed a plan for how to organize and restructure the existing Zoning Code for efficiency and legibility. 
Concurrently, the team evaluated and identified best practices that could help inform this Code Update and conducted 
background research on multiple zoning-related topics, which led to the identification of six key issues to solicit community 
input on. White papers were then developed for each of the six key issues, which are summarized in the next section of this 
report. These white papers and associated online surveys will be used to receive community input on a variety of issues. 
A Community Outreach and Engagement Plan (discussed in detail later in the report) has been developed to guide how 
community and stakeholder input is solicited, and how it will help guide the drafting and development of the updated Zoning 
Code. The goal is to have a draft ready for public review in the Fall of 2026.
Concurrently with this Code Update work, the team is analyzing parcels that have inconsistencies between the current 
Zoning Map and the General Plan 2040 Land Use Map to develop a list of Zoning Map amendments. Focused outreach to 
owners and tenants of properties identified for Zoning map amendments will occur, followed by study sessions with the 
Planning Commission, to ensure awareness and collaboration before final recommendations are developed. Final adoption 
hearings for the updated Zoning Code and Zoning Map are anticipated in the Summer of 2027. The proposed timeline for 
the Zoning Code Update is included as Attachment 1.
DISCUSSION:
Key Issue White Papers
While the General Plan provides a broad and long-term vision for the City, the Zoning Code specifies how each individual 
property can be used to achieve those objectives. Zoning codes are the body of rules and regulations that control what is 
built on the ground, as well as what uses occupy buildings and sites. They determine the form and character of development, 
such as the size and height of buildings, and include provisions to ensure that new development and uses will fit into existing 
and surrounding neighborhoods. Zoning codes are also used to implement the community goals and policies expressed in 
the General Plan. However, there are some things that development codes cannot do, since they are limited in some respects 
by State law and other legal precedent. Further, not all issues outlined in the General Plan are addressed in the Zoning Code; 
instead utilizing other sections of the Municipal Code or other planning tools, such as specific plans and design standards 
and other adopted policies, to implement.
As mentioned earlier, the team identified six key issues to focus early community and stakeholder discussions and input. A 
white paper has been prepared for each of these six key issues, which include:
•
Building Height Limits
•
Commercial Uses and Regulations
•
Single-Family Residential Regulations
•
Public Noticing Requirements
•
Open Space Requirements
•
Setbacks/Buffers
The white papers include an overview of community goals, key issues, current regulations, General Plan direction and 
policies, applicable State laws, best practices, and questions for public input. A summary for each topic is included below, 
with the full white papers included as Attachment 2. 
Building Height Limits
Building height limits in San Mateo have been regulated by voter approved initiatives (Measures H, P, and Y) since 1992, and 
this topic was a key issue of community discussion during the General Plan update process. In November 2024, following 
adoption of General Plan 2040, Measure T was approved by the voters, which allowed for the City’s height limits to increase 
as outlined in the Land Use Element. The Zoning Code Update will establish updated height limits to align with the story 
limits contained in the General Plan. In the meantime, the Council adopted Zoning Code section 27.02.220 (Consistency with 
Measure T, General Plan Ballot Measure). This provision specifies that in the event of a conflict between the General Plan 
and the Zoning Code, the uses, densities, intensities and heights specified in the General Plan shall control.
147 of 719

--- page 148 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
Nearly all of the building height regulations noted in Title 27 of the Municipal Code refer to the General Plan 2030 Building 
Height Plan rather than being included directly in the Zoning Code.  One of the objectives of the Zoning Code Update is to 
clarify the height measurement rules for correlating the story limits set by the General Plan to the maximum allowed height 
in terms of feet.  In addition, the Zoning Code Update process will evaluate development feasibility to align with the densities 
(units per acre) and intensities (floor area ratio) identified in the General Plan while providing reasonable and context-
sensitive transitions between zoning districts and development projects in San Mateo.  
Commercial Uses and Regulations
Commercial uses and the associated regulations are a cornerstone of supporting San Mateo’s economic vitality and 
neighborhood livability.  The location, scale and mix of commercial activities play a key role in shaping neighborhood 
character and advancing the General Plan goals to support the local economy, enhance neighborhood fabric and quality of 
life, and provide a wide range of uses to meet the community's needs.  Through the Zoning Code update process, the City 
will examine how commercial land use regulations can balance economic vitality with neighborhood compatibility to support 
vibrant, walkable neighborhoods and meet the full spectrum of needs of the San Mateo residents and visitors.  
 
This white paper includes a review of the existing commercial use regulations in the Zoning Code, directions and policies in 
the General Plan, best practice options, and questions for public consideration. Reassessment of the commercial zoning 
districts will include an analysis of what uses are currently allowed in the districts, what specific regulations should be added 
or removed, and whether any uses should be added, revised, or removed in these zoning districts. The updated regulations 
for the commercial districts will be applied to other zoning designations that allow for commercial activities, such as Mixed-
Use and Office designations.
Single-Family Residential Regulations
Single-family, low-density residential districts are evolving to meet the changing needs of San Mateo’s residents and 
households. To address housing shortages, the State has adopted a significant number of new housing laws that aim to 
support the evolving housing needs of residents and address statewide housing shortages. Some of these laws focus on 
increasing housing opportunities in single-family residential districts, including measures like Senate Bill (SB) 9, SB 1123, and 
more flexible accessory dwelling units (ADUs) regulation. The City’s housing stock is also aging and in need of updates and 
investment, and updated regulations that reduce antiquated requirements and support current housing and family needs 
will support and encourage homeowner investments in their properties.
  
The City’s Single-Family Residential (SFR) Districts have not been comprehensively updated since 1992. The City has several 
goals for updating the current SFR regulations, including complying with recent state housing laws, converting subjective 
design guidelines into transparent and objective standards in the code and accompanying Objective Design Standards, 
simplifying development standards, and streamlining the approval process. This white paper notes best practices and 
questions for public consideration.
Public Noticing Requirements
As part of the Zoning Code update, the administrative and procedural requirements for public notification for new 
development will be updated to comply with recent state laws. Best practices for facilitating equitable community 
engagement, a General Plan priority, will also be considered. Appropriate public noticing periods are vital for the 
administration and procedural regulations in Zoning Codes, and this is an opportunity to clarify and right-size community 
engagement and public input expectations and opportunities. The appropriate level of participation from residents and 
stakeholders will be updated so that expectations are clear from the earliest stages of project initiation. Updates to this 
section of the Zoning Code will also consider streamlining the notice and entitlement process, especially for single-family 
and low-density residential projects.  
This white paper includes a review of the existing public noticing requirements in the Zoning Code, directions and policies in 
the General Plan, the current related Zoning Code provisions, recent state requirements, best practice options, and 
questions for public consideration.
148 of 719

--- page 149 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
Open Space Regulations
The Zoning Code Update will follow the General Plan’s direction to expand public access to open spaces and to encourage a 
variety of open space types, including privately-owned public open space, community gardens, multifamily open space, and 
public facilities such as parks and recreation centers. The white paper presents options for how open space, both private 
and publicly accessible, can be incorporated into new development. It also includes a review of the existing open space 
regulations in the Zoning Code, goals and policies in the General Plan, recent state bills, and best practice options and 
considerations.  
Setbacks/Buffers
Building setbacks and buffers play a critical role in shaping the physical form of San Mateo’s neighborhoods, influencing 
streetscape and architectural character, access to light and air, and privacy. Building setbacks establish the required distance 
between structures and property lines, streets, or other site features, and are a fundamental tool for regulating the scale, 
placement, and massing of new development. This white paper addresses the setback and buffer regulations for the 
City’s zoning districts. The white paper summary also includes an overview of applicable goals and policies in the General 
Plan, recent state laws, best practice options/considerations, and questions for public consideration.   
Community Outreach and Engagement Plan
As part of the Zoning Code Update scope, the team has developed a Community Outreach and Engagement Plan (Attachment 
3). The Engagement Plan provides the City with tools and strategies to solicit input from the San Mateo community and its 
stakeholders, through various outreach programs which will help inform what goes into the Zoning Code Update. This Plan 
is different from the approaches used during the General Plan update process as citywide goals and policies have now been 
set; and this effort is now working on developing the best ways to implement them.
The overarching objective of this Plan will be to engage the San Mateo community and key stakeholder groups to share 
information about the Zoning Code Update and gather ideas and input about how to approach and address key issues. 
Outreach strategies in the Engagement Plan include:

Two virtual and two in-person community meetings

Up to four online surveys

Up to 12 stakeholder meetings

Up to five study sessions with the Planning Commission or City Council (excluding public hearings for adoption)
The first virtual community workshop was held on January 15, 2026, at 6:30 p.m., and included an online survey (which was 
made available in English, Spanish, and Simplified Chinese). The survey was used to collect input on the key issues the Zoning 
Code Update should address. There were approximately 34 attendees at the workshop, and the main objective was to 
provide an overview of the project and gather input on key issues the Zoning Code Update should address. The workshop 
included time for questions and answers, and small group discussions that covered topics related to single-family residential 
districts, commercial uses, and building height. Highlights from the small group discussion included:
•
Find ways to ensure that there’s sufficient parking in single-family districts
•
Implement objective design standards that preserve community character
•
Consider allowing small-scale commercial uses in residential districts and discourage incompatible commercials 
adjacent to residential areas
•
Incorporate design requirements that reduce building massing
On January 26, 2026, two in-person focus group meetings were hosted at City Hall for architects, builders, and brokers who 
frequent the Zoning Code. The white papers on the six key identified issues helped guide discussions with the focus group 
attendees. 
The City plans to host several other community workshops prior to finalizing the Zoning Code. To support community 
awareness about the project and engagement opportunities, the team will use a variety of methods, including print 
newsletters, flyers, social media, eNews and eBlasts, press releases, and updates to the project website (www. 
StriveSanMateo.org).
149 of 719

--- page 150 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
Next Steps
Public participation will be ongoing throughout the Zoning Code Update process, and the public is invited to contact staff 
directly at any point outside of the scheduled workshops and activities. Once administrative drafts have been reviewed for 
public comment and changes made, the Planning Commission will be asked to review the administrative draft Zoning Code 
and give feedback to staff on what should be added, kept as proposed, modified, and/or removed altogether to help frame 
next steps with the update process. The final Zoning Code document will be brought before the Planning Commission for 
review and recommendation to Council for review for final adoption. Staff anticipates the final adoption hearings for the 
Zoning Code Update to take place in the Spring and/or Summer of 2027.

MEMORANDUM  
DATE 
January 16, 2026 
TO 
City of San Mateo 
FROM 
PlaceWorks 
SUBJECT 
Key Issues White Paper – Building Height 
Introduction 
Building height limits was a key issue of community interest during the Strive San Mateo General Plan 
2040 adoption process. Dating back to 1991, the City’s height limits were set by voter initiative 
(Measures H, P and Y) and implemented through the General Plan. With the adoption of General Plan 
2040 in March 2024, and the subsequent voter approval of Measure T in November 2024, the City’s 
height limits were effectively increased with reduced height regulation contained in the General Plan. 
As part of General Plan implementation, the Comprehensive Zoning Code Update (CZCU) project will 
establish updated height limits within the City’s Zoning Code (Title 27) to align with the story limits 
contained in the General Plan. Height regulations are most typically contained in a jurisdiction’s zoning 
code, and this transition is intended to align with that best practice and implement an updated approach 
for how the City sets and regulates height.  
Prior to starting the CZCU project, the City drafted four new high-density districts for residential and 
mixed-use zones to implement the highest allowed densities referenced in the General Plan (99 
units/acre and 130 units/acre). Public hearings to consider adoption of these new high density districts 
are planned for the first half of 2026. The work to draft these updated standards included assessments 
of appropriate building height limits for this level of density, and helped to inform the height 
requirements and regulations discussed in this white paper.  
This white paper includes a review of the existing height regulations in the Zoning Code, directions and 
policies in the General Plan, a recent state bill related to height and proximity to public transportation, 
best practice options and considerations, and questions that will be asked for the public to provide 
input.   
City and Community Goals 
The City is seeking to update the Zoning Code’s building height regulations to ensure development 
feasibility for the identified densities and/or intensities, while providing reasonable and context-
sensitive transitions between zoning districts and development projects in San Mateo. The City wants 
152 of 719

--- page 153 ---
January 16, 2026 | Page 2 
to clarify the height measurement rules for correlating the story limits set by the General Plan to the 
maximum allowed height in terms of feet. Additionally, the City wants to improve the clarity of the code 
and make the building height regulations more user-friendly and better aligned with current 
construction practices. For the new high density districts (discussed in detail below), the City asked for 
building height sections to help visualize the potential height and massing of buildings and illustrate 
how stories translate to feet for different building types (i.e., residential, mixed-use, commercial, etc.).  
Overview of Key Issue Topic 
In zoning codes, building height regulations are established to dictate the maximum allowed height in 
terms of feet and/or the number of stories, define how height is measured, provide parameters for 
allowed design features, and outline specific height exceptions. These regulations must balance 
multiple planning objectives, including meeting residential density requirements from the General Plan, 
accommodating appropriate building types, complementing the existing neighborhood character, and 
ensuring economic feasibility for developers.  
Properly setting height limits requires analyzing a community's existing regulations and conditions. It 
also means considering the California Building Code (CBC), which links building height to specific 
construction materials and methods. Failing to review these conditions can inadvertently make desired 
development types economically unfeasible, hinder density goals, and/or result in building styles that 
do not align with the community’s vision. 
To create projects that align with community objectives, height regulations often provide limits in both 
feet and the number of stories. Height is typically measured from the existing grade (i.e., the ground's 
surface level before construction, or the proposed exterior grade, whichever is lower) to the top of the 
roof structure. Adopting effective building height regulations requires them to correlate with density 
requirements, provide clear and adaptable rules, and allow for flexibility in design. 
High Density District Analysis 
As part of the process for drafting the four new high density districts, the Project Team analyzed how 
the General Plan’s new higher density requirements aligned with the existing height standards in the 
Zoning Code. This process involved developing a methodology to correlate building height, measured 
in both feet and the number of stories – the latter being specified in the General Plan – with density 
and other development standards. The Project Team worked together to arrive at assumptions for floor 
heights for different types of projects to calculate maximum height for each of the new districts based 
on the maximum number of stories allowed in the corresponding General Plan land use designation.  
Based on these height guidelines, the Project Team created illustrations for three types of example 
projects: (1) an eight-story residential-only project; (2) an eight-story mixed-use project with ground-
floor retail, office space on the second, third, and fourth stories, and residential on the fifth through 
eighth stories; and (3) an eight-story office-only project. The illustration below shows the residential-
only project type for an eight-story building, representing the General Plan’s Residential High land use 
designation. The building height standards in this illustration were designed based on a 13-foot floor-
153 of 719

--- page 154 ---
January 16, 2026 | Page 3 
to-floor height standard for the first story, 11-foot floor-to-floor height standard for all subsequent 
floors above, and a consistent 14-inch floor thickness throughout the building, which is not marked on 
the illustration. The first floor is designed with a higher floor-to-floor height to accommodate common 
area lobby activities and amenities, mechanical and utility spaces, and to provide a more flexible 
ground-floor presence along the street. 
 
Source: PlaceWorks (2025) 
For non-residential-only projects and mixed-use projects that include non-residential uses, the ground-
level and typical floor ceiling heights are calculated based on similar assumptions, with higher ceiling 
heights allowed for ground-level and non-residential floors. After specific heights were calculated for 
the four project types, the City made the decision to round the building height to the nearest “0” or “5” 
number in the ones-place to make the Zoning Code more user-friendly. For example, for the proposed 
Mixed-Use 4 District that would implement the General Plan’s Mixed-Use High land use designation, 
the maximum allowed building height for feet was increased from 98 feet to 100 feet to accommodate 
eight stories for a mixed-use project with residential units. The proposed height limits for the four high 
density districts are as follows:

Brief Summary of Current Title 27 (Zoning) Regulations 
The current regulations in the Zoning Code that address building height are summarized below: 
• 
The SMMC includes building height regulations throughout Title 27 (Zoning). However,  
building height regulations for nearly all of the zoning districts refer back to the General Plan 
2030 Building Height Plan rather than being included directly in the Zoning Code.  
• 
The R1 and R2 Districts are the only districts with specific height regulations provided in the 
Zoning Code. Section 27.18.050 (Building Height and Daylight Plane) provides the following 
two required maximum height limits: walls can have a maximum height of 24 feet, and the 
highest point of the roof can have a maximum height of 32 feet. Both of these limits are 
measured from the existing grade and apply in the R1 and R2 Districts. Existing homes that 
exceed the height limit are allowed to construct additions to the existing roof line height. The 
Section also provides daylight plane0F1 regulations in the R1 and R2 Districts for both the 
primary buildings and structures. The daylight plane is intended to prevent buildings from 
overshadowing their neighbors while allowing for encroachment features (e.g., dormers, 
architectural elements, antennas), hillside parcels, and other specific conditions.  
 
1 Section 27.04.143 (Daylight Plane) defines “daylight plane” as an “inclined plane, beginning at a 
stated height above grade and extending into the site at a stated upward angle to the horizontal, 
which may limit the height or horizontal extent of structures at any specific point on the site where 
the daylight plane is more restrictive than the height limit or the minimum yard applicable at such 
point on the site. 
 
155 of 719

--- page 156 ---
January 16, 2026 | Page 5 
• 
Section 27.02.060 (Height Limitations and Exceptions) provides supplemental building height 
regulations and exceptions to maximum allowed height regulations for accessory features, 
such as parapet walls, elevator enclosures and mechanical equipment, for individual zoning 
districts, except for the R1 and R2 Districts. This Section includes exceptions to maximum 
height and bulk regulations for improvements or additions to existing nonconforming 
buildings. This Section also gives the Zoning Administrator authority to require applications to 
go through additional review by the Planning Commission for specific project features.  
• 
Chapter 27.40 (Building Height and Bulk) has a chapter title that presumably is applicable for 
building height regulations citywide. However, the regulations in the chapter only apply to the 
Downtown Specific Plan area.  
• 
Section 27.04.080 (Building Height) defines the term “building height” in the SMMC as the 
following: The vertical distance, measured from existing grade at any point along the 
perimeter of a building, to the highest plateline of the structure directly above that point, 
regardless of whether that point is on the same plane as the building where it touches the 
ground. Different setback requirements as a result of different building heights shall be 
applied to different portions of the building. Setback requirements determined by building 
height may utilize the height of an intermediate plate height of the building plane that 
touches the ground where the building above that intermediate plate height is set back a 
distance equal to 25 percent of the width of the structure at that point from the building 
plane that meets the ground, as illustrated in the following figure: 
 
156 of 719

--- page 157 ---
January 16, 2026 | Page 6 
General Plan’s Direction and Policies  
The General Plan introduced four new land use designations (Residential Medium II, Residential High, 
Mixed-Use Medium II, Mixed-Use High) to allow for higher densities. The General Plan also provides the 
maximum number of stories, but not the corresponding maximum height measured in feet, that will be 
allowed for each of these four new land use designations. This was an intentional policy choice that was 
made in order to move specific height regulations out of the General Plan and into the Zoning Code. 
The following lists these four new land use designations and their corresponding allowed density, height 
limits, and floor area ratio (FAR) regulations, where applicable: 
• 
Residential Medium II:  
51 – 99 units/acre | 4 – 6 stories | N/A 
• 
Residential High:  
100 – 130 units/ acre | 5 – 8 stories | N/A 
• 
Mixed-Use Medium II:  
51 – 99 units/acre | 4 – 6 stories | 4.0 
• 
Mixed-Use High:  
100 – 130 units/acre | 5 – 8 stories | 4.5 
These General Plan height limits and densities are in effect, but the City does not have corresponding 
zoning districts or development standards that align with these higher heights and densities. To address 
this, the City will present four new high density districts to the Planning Commission and City Council to 
consider for adoption in the first half of 2026 to provide clarity to the higher densities and corresponding 
building height limits that are already in effect. These high density districts will be subsequently 
incorporated into the CZCU.  
State Law Compliance 
Senate Bill (SB) 79 will become effective on July 1, 2026. This law requires all jurisdictions in eight 
counties, including San Mateo County, to approve qualified transit-oriented development (TOD) 
housing projects near certain major transit stops, effectively increasing allowable density in those areas. 
Additionally, SB 79 sets specific density and height limits for projects that provide the required 
percentage of affordable housing units for lower-income households. The height limits range from 55 
feet to 95 feet depending on one of two TOD stop types and the project distance from the stop. As part 
of the CZCU, the City will need to analyze the building height regulations in the Zoning Code to meet 
the requirements of SB 79. More information about SB 79 and how it applies to sites within the City of 
San Mateo can be viewed here: https://www.cityofsanmateo.org/4922/State-Legislation-Information-
and-Resour. 
Best Practice Options and Considerations 
This section outlines best practices and key considerations drawn from City staff input, state guidance, 
and comparable jurisdictions. The Project Team reviewed best practices from comparable jurisdictions 
to ensure the updated regulations reflect current planning standards successfully applied in comparable 
157 of 719

--- page 158 ---
January 16, 2026 | Page 7 
communities. These references help identify approaches that are clear, consistent, and adaptable to 
San Mateo’s goals and current built environment. The comparable jurisdictions referenced in this white 
paper include the cities of Costa Mesa, Milpitas, Sacramento, and Stockton, and Santa Clara County.  
• 
Costa Mesa was chosen because part of the code was recently updated and was seen as a good 
example by city staff. 
• 
Milpitas was chosen because it was just comprehensively updated in 2025, the city has a similar 
built environment to San Mateo and is located nearby, and the code includes clear writing and 
consistent organization. 
• 
Sacramento was chosen because it was seen as a good example by city staff and contains a 
detailed list of administrative and permit processes and procedures. 
• 
Stockton was chosen because it was just comprehensively updated in 2025, includes Clear and 
concise writing, the use regulations and development standards are clear and well-written, and 
there are detailed and easy-to-use site development and specific to use regulations. 
• 
Santa Clara County was chosen because it is located nearby, it contains clear writing and 
organization, and is well-organized by different residential district types (e.g., Rural, Urban 
Residential). 
The following considerations will help inform how the San Mateo’s building height regulations are 
updated: 
• 
The definition for “building height” should be clarified. The current definition both defines the 
term and also provides the rules of measurement for the standard. Other jurisdictions 
separate the definition of “building height” and then provide the rules of measurement in a 
different section of the Zoning Code. The definition would also benefit by being simplified to 
improve clarity for the reader. Specifically, building height is measured from the existing grade 
rather than from the lower of the existing or proposed grade, which could result in an actual 
building height that exceeds the height calculated under the code. Furthermore, building 
height is measured to the top of the roof plate, which might differ from the requirements 
adopted in many other jurisdictions. 
• 
Most comparable jurisdictions reviewed include building height regulations that are measured 
in both feet and the number of stories. This practice is currently being utilized for the new 
high density districts and should continue to be implemented for all zoning districts in the 
CZCU. 
• 
Building height regulations for nearly all zoning districts (not including the R1 and R2 Districts) 
reference the General Plan rather than directly including height limits in the Zoning Code. 
However, all comparable jurisdictions align with standard zoning code practice by including 
height requirements directly in the zoning code rather than cross-referencing to the General 
Plan. This is convenient for users and mirrors other quantitative measurements provided in 
the City’s Zoning Code. Therefore, height regulations should directly be included in the Zoning 
Code. As noted above, General Plan 2040 supports this approach by not including any specific 
height limits, other than story ranges. 
158 of 719

--- page 159 ---
January 16, 2026 | Page 8 
• 
As discussed in the “Brief Summary of Current Title 27 (Zoning) Regulations” section above, 
the maximum height in the R1 and R2 Districts are currently provided and measured by two 
separate standards: the maximum height of the walls, which is 24 feet, and the highest point 
of the roof, which is 32 feet. However, all comparable jurisdictions measure maximum height 
only in terms of the maximum height of the building itself, rather than by separate wall plate 
and roof heights. Based on this review, in San Mateo’s R1 and R2 Districts, the City may want 
to adopt an updated limit that is only measured in terms of the maximum height of the 
building. Additional height measurement regulations and exceptions may be provided within 
the definition of building height in the new definition chapter of the Zoning Code. 
Additionally, further research will be conducted to determine and align the current maximum 
height of the R1 and R2 Districts with the maximum height provided in General Plan 2040. 
Since General Plan 2040 provides maximum height in terms of the number of stories only, the 
maximum allowed height in terms of feet will need to be aligned with the maximum number 
of stories for the R1 and R2 Districts. 
• 
The list of supplemental building height regulations and exceptions to maximum allowed 
height regulations for accessory features provided in Section 27.02.060 (Height Limitations 
and Exceptions) will need to be reviewed. As many other jurisdictions take a more balanced 
approach or establish explicit height limits for height exemptions, the City should determine if 
reconsidering the height exemption for mechanical systems is needed or consider setting up 
limitations on height exemptions for mechanical systems. The review could determine 
whether there are additional accessory rooftop and roofline features that should be 
incorporated into these regulations, such as rooftop telecommunication facilities. Additionally, 
this review will evaluate how height limitations and exceptions can support and facilitate high-
quality architectural design. Furthermore, this Section will need to be further reviewed to 
determine if any specific exceptions should be adopted to allow rooftops to be utilized as 
common usable open spaces for residents of a development project. 
• 
The existing maximum allowed building heights are not high enough to accommodate the 
higher densities allowed in the current code for the R4, R5, R4-D, R5-D, and R6-D zoning 
districts. The maximum allowed building heights will need to be updated to align with the 
story ranges and required densities contained in General Plan 2040. 
• 
Additional research is needed to determine how to best address the current daylight plane 
regulations in the Zoning Code. 
Measuring Building Height 
As discussed above in the “High Density District Analysis” section, the Project Team drafted the updated 
regulations by establishing assumptions about building height standards based on a review of the 
current City’s regulations and industry best practices. The Project Team considered how to correlate 
building height measured in both feet and the number of stories for multi-family residential, mixed-use, 
and office project types in relation to the number of required floors for each General Plan land use 
designation. 
159 of 719

--- page 160 ---
January 16, 2026 | Page 9 
For determining the height for all other zoning districts during the CZCU process, the Project Team is 
seeking additional input whether to continue with a similar or alternative approach to either simplify or 
be more exact when determining building height for a zoning district. Additional regulations the Project 
Team will add into the Zoning Code include, but are not limited to, flexibility for hillside and sloping sites 
and accommodations for rooftop features (e.g., mechanical equipment and parapets).

In San Mateo’s current Zoning Code (Title 27 of the San Mateo Municipal Code), to measure a 
building's height, one must measure the vertical distance from the existing grade at any point

on a building's perimeter to the highest plateline1F2 directly above that point. Which of the
