MEMORANDUM  
DATE 
January 9, 2026 
TO 
City of San Mateo 
FROM 
PlaceWorks 
SUBJECT 
Key Issues White Paper – Commercial (C1, C2, C3, and C4) Zoning District Uses  
Introduction 
The San Mateo Municipal Code (SMMC) includes four commercial zoning districts in Title 27 (Zoning) 
(i.e., the Zoning Code). The current commercial zoning districts incorporate a range of uses that are 
allowed to develop by right and uses that require a Special Use Permit (SUP), often requiring additional 
development and/or operating standards to permit a specific use on a parcel. The commercial zoning 
districts would benefit from a review of what uses are currently allowed in the districts, what specific 
additional regulations are required, and whether any uses should be added, revised, or removed in 
these zoning districts.  
This white paper includes a review of the use regulations in the commercial zoning districts in the Zoning 
Code, directions and policies in the General Plan, recent state bills, best practice options and 
considerations, and questions that will be asked during public input.   
City and Community Goals  
The City is seeking to update the uses within the C1 District (Neighborhood Commercial), C2 and C3 
Districts (Regional/Community Commercial), and C4 District (Service Commercial).1 This update is 
intended to align with the current local-serving commercial retail and service uses and General Plan 
land uses, and address the economic needs and trends in San Mateo. The updated commercial districts 
will be developed to align with and implement the City’s 2026-2029 Economic Development Work Plan, 
which strives to build a more welcoming, adaptable, inclusive, and resilient business and visitor 
community in order to support community vibrancy and long-term fiscal stability. This can be achieved 
by incorporating appropriate categories and types of uses to allow for future commercial growth and 
change and incorporate administrative and procedural regulations that help streamline permitting and 
review processes. The City intends to clarify ambiguities in the existing Zoning Code, particularly the 
distinction between different terminologies used to refer to the following two general categories for 
commercial uses: (1) office-/employment-based commercial uses, and (2) retail, restaurant, personal 
 
1 The downtown commercial uses are not being included in this white paper because they are governed 
by the Downtown Area Plan, which will be subject to a separate update process kicking off in 2026. 
 
(@ PLACEWORKS 
PLACEWORKS.COM • Orange County • Sacramento • Bay Area • Central Coast • Los Angeles • Inland Empire 
162 of 719

--- page 163 ---
January 9, 2026 | Page 2 
services, and entertainment uses. Overlapping definitions and inconsistent terminology in a zoning code 
often leads to unintended consequences, such as permitting high-traffic retail uses in office corridors 
or creating parking shortages. Clarifying these distinctions will ensure that development standards are 
appropriately aligned with the specific scale and function of each use. 
The City also wants to modernize the existing uses in these districts, improve the formatting of the Code, 
and address potentially controversial uses in commercial districts, such as service stations and on-site 
24-hour convenience stores, by establishing buffer requirements and performance standards for 
specific uses to better serve residents and business owners. Issues raised previously by the community 
regarding these uses include allowing them to operate overnight, and associated lighting, design 
aesthetics, and public safety concerns where these uses occur.  
Overview of Key Issue Topic 
In commercial zoning districts, adopting clearly defined uses is crucial for developing resident- and 
visitor-friendly areas for building the business and retail community. By designating specific types of 
commercial uses in a zoning code, such as retail stores, service uses, restaurants, entertainment 
facilities, and offices, the City can shape the character of redeveloped and new commercial areas and 
foster economic development in San Mateo to align with goals and objectives of the Strive San Mateo 
General Plan 2040. Commercial use regulations should be forward-looking and flexible to accommodate 
the development of new and emerging uses, including, but not limited to, market halls, fitness activities, 
cloud kitchens (i.e., a commercial kitchen used only for takeout or delivery, food preparation, and/or 
cooking facilities), small entertainment venues, and other uses not yet envisioned but compatible with 
appropriate districts. They should also minimize conflicts between incompatible types of commercial 
uses (for example, auto service adjacent to pedestrian-oriented retail) and integrate with the 
surrounding neighborhoods.  
In addition to establishing a zoning district’s character, suitable use regulations can promote economic 
growth and create an environment that reflects community, business, and economic goals, objectives, 
and needs. The City can tailor commercial zones to focus specific types of businesses or industries in a 
defined area and citywide, leading to a diverse local economy. Furthermore, by requiring specific 
development standards and buffers based on the type of use, the City can design commercial areas to 
be compatible with both the surrounding environment and the community's vision.  
Brief Summary of Current Title 27 (Zoning) Regulations 
The current zoning regulations in the Zoning Code that address uses in the C1, C2, C3 and C4 Districts 
are summarized below: 
• 
Chapter 27.30 (C1 Districts—Neighborhood Commercial):  
o 
The C1 District is intended to maintain and enhance neighborhood shopping areas 
with standards to ensure compatibility with surrounding residential properties. 
~ 
PLACEWORKS 
163 of 719

--- page 164 ---
January 9, 2026 | Page 3 
Permitted uses are designed to include retail sales, limited office space, and personal 
services for the local area.  
o 
The C1 District lists uses that are permitted and those that require a SUP. The 
permitted uses range from personal services (e.g., barber shops), small offices, retail 
stores, restaurants, affordable housing (i.e., low-barrier navigation centers) required 
by state law, and mixed-use developments (i.e., residential units above ground floors). 
The uses that require a SUP range from automobile gasoline service stations, fast food 
establishments without drive-through facilities, parking facilities, community services 
(i.e., religious institutions), firearms dealers, and a range of residential uses (e.g., 
community care facilities, nursing homes, boarding houses, etc.). 
• 
Chapter 27.32 (C2 Districts – Regional/Community Commercial):  
o 
The C2 District is intended to maintain and enhance major commercial centers 
accommodating a broad range of office, retail, and personal services of community-
wide or regional significance.  
o 
The C2 District lists uses that are permitted and those that require a SUP. The 
permitted uses include all permitted uses allowed in the C1 District, as well as 
additional uses such as offices, emergency shelters, hotels and motels, art and media 
uses (e.g. art galleries, publishing establishments), and retail and commercial services 
(e.g. automotive accessory stores, laundries, and catering services). The uses that 
require a SUP include all special uses permitted in the C1 Districts, such as automobile 
gasoline service stations, as well as additional uses such as clubs, hospitals, public 
transportation facilities and terminals, recreational vehicle storage, and theaters. 
• 
Chapter 27.34 (C3 Districts – Regional/Community Commercial):  
o 
The C3 District is intended to maintain and enhance major commercial centers 
accommodating a broader range of office, retail, and personal services of community-
wide or regional significance compared to the C2 District.  
o 
The C3 District lists uses that are permitted and those that require a SUP. The 
permitted uses include all permitted uses allowed in the C1 and C2 Districts, as well as 
additional uses such as ambulance service; automobile, boat, and truck sales and 
rental; car wash and detailing facilities; and laboratories. The uses that require a SUP 
include all special uses permitted in the C1 and C2 Districts, such as automobile 
gasoline service stations, as well as additional uses such as restaurants with drive-
through facilities and uses for public events (i.e., auditoriums, stadiums, arenas, 
armories, and gymnasiums). 
 
 
 
~ 
PLACEWORKS 
164 of 719

--- page 165 ---
January 9, 2026 | Page 4 
• 
Chapter 27.36 (C4 – Service Commercial): 
o 
The C4 District is intended to create and maintain a broad range of citywide and 
regional service uses, including major automobile and truck repair, construction 
materials yards, and limited processing of materials. 
o 
The C4 lists uses that are permitted and those that require a SUP. The permitted uses 
include all permitted uses allowed in the C1, C2, and C3 Districts, except for residential 
uses, including low-barrier navigation centers and emergency shelters. Office uses 
located on properties located along Palm Avenue between Twentieth and Twenty-fifth 
Avenues are prohibited. The uses that require a SUP include all special uses permitted 
in the C1, C2, and C3 Districts (except residential uses), as well as additional uses such 
as fuel bulk stations and the storage of impounded motor vehicles on an open lot.  
• 
In addition to listing the uses in these four zoning districts, each chapter provides the 
development standards that projects are required to follow. The development standards 
provided include building height, floor area ratios, setbacks, buffers, and build-to-line 
requirements. 
General Plan’s Direction and Policies  
This section provides an overview of the General Plan 2040 goals, policies, and actions (GPAs) that will 
inform the updated uses that go into the Zoning Code’s commercial districts. The General Plan GPAs 
will help inform how to allow and develop the range of uses that will go into each of the updated 
districts. How the commercial districts are delineated will also support the City’s economic development 
goals.  
A Diverse Range of Land Uses 
To support San Mateo’s existing and future businesses and to maintain the City as a vibrant place to 
live, work, visit, and invest, it is essential to plan for a diverse mix of land uses that bolster the City’s 
commercial businesses and activities. By concentrating commercial uses near transit and 
complementary types of land uses, the City’s commercial districts and neighborhoods can facilitate 
economic development, attractive commercial corridors, and retail areas where businesses can grow. 
In addition, residents and visitors can conveniently access goods and services and cultural and 
entertainment activities. 
Goal LU-3 
Goal LU-3 in the General Plan’s Land Use Element was incorporated as a guiding principle for the City’s 
future economic activities and growth. Goal LU-3 states that the City will “provide a wide range of land 
uses, including housing, parks, open space, recreation, retail, commercial services, office, and industrial 
to adequately meet the full spectrum of needs in the community.” The Comprehensive Zoning Code 
~ 
PLACEWORKS 
165 of 719

--- page 166 ---
January 9, 2026 | Page 5 
Update (CZCU) is a core tool to implement this Goal by modifying the City’s existing land use regulations 
and determining how to regulate existing and new uses.  
To implement Goal LU-3, the General Plan includes several policies to allow for a diverse range of land 
uses in San Mateo. Policies LU 3.3 through LU 3.8 collectively advance the General Plan’s goal of 
maintaining a diverse mix of land uses that strengthen San Mateo’s commercial districts and support 
vibrant, connected neighborhoods. Policies LU 3.3 (Neighborhood Commercial and Service Uses) and 
LU 3.4 (Neighborhood Commercial Preservation) focus on preserving and enhancing neighborhood-
serving commercial areas by retaining local retail and service uses, particularly on the ground floor of 
new development, to ensure residents can conveniently access goods and services directly in their 
neighborhoods. Policy LU 3.5 (Support Service Uses) expands this vision by encouraging a broad range 
of service-oriented businesses, prioritizing their location in areas where they best support residential 
and commercial uses, including equity priority communities. 
Policy LU 3.6 (Service Commercial/Light Industrial) reinforces the importance of service commercial and 
light industrial uses by preserving these areas for businesses that meet local needs and discouraging 
incompatible uses. Policies LU 3.7 (Hotels) and LU 3.8 (Visitor Economy) further diversify the commercial 
landscape by encouraging hotel development in commercial areas and allowing small hotels in mixed-
use districts when they are consistent with the density of adjacent uses, and strengthening the visitor 
economy by encouraging and incentivizing a mix of entertainment, cultural, and retail activities that 
draw both residents and visitors. 
Action LU 3.18 (Permitted Uses) provides a key implementation framework for these policies by 
directing the City to update and modernize the list of permitted uses and uses that require a special use 
permit in all commercial districts. Through the CZCU, this Action and the Policies above ensures that the 
City’s land use regulations remain aligned with evolving economic conditions and modern types of 
commercial uses. 
A Sustainable Economy 
San Mateo’s long-term fiscal health and overall livability depend on sustaining a diverse and resilient 
commercial economy supported by a strong tax base and reliable commercial retail and service uses. 
As regional industries continue to attract jobs to the Peninsula, housing demand has outpaced supply, 
leading to increased costs and financial constraints that affect both residents and the local workforce 
that serves the City’s commercial industry.  
Goal LU-11 
Goal LU-11 was incorporated in General Plan 2040 as a guiding principle to create a more sustainable, 
resilient local economy in San Mateo. Goal LU-11 states that the City will “cultivate a diverse, thriving, 
inclusive, and green economy.”  
The CZCU aims to strengthen and modernize uses in the City’s Commercial Districts to leverage the 
regional job market to create quality jobs, support the growth of local businesses, and ensure that 
workers at all income levels can live closer to employment centers. The CZCU plays a key role in creating 
~ 
PLACEWORKS 
166 of 719

--- page 167 ---
January 9, 2026 | Page 6 
a thriving, adaptable commercial environment that supports businesses, increases access to goods and 
services, and contributes to the City’s economic future. 
The CZCU will advance Goal LU-11 by aligning the City’s commercial district regulations with policies 
that strengthen, diversify, and stabilize San Mateo’s economic base. By modernizing permitted uses, 
clarifying development standards, and supporting the retention and growth of local businesses, the 
CZCU will help address Policies LU 11.1 (Economic Development) and LU 11.4 (Diverse Economic Base) 
by creating an environment where a broad mix of commercial, service, and employment-generating 
uses can be located. These changes will also promote a better jobs-to-housing relationship consistent 
with Policy LU 11.5 (Jobs to Housing Balance) by allowing a wider range of commercial activities in 
locations accessible to residential neighborhoods, reducing commuting distances, and supporting local 
employment opportunities.  
Action LU 11.10 (Small Business Support) calls for reducing regulatory barriers, updating outdated use 
definitions, and improving the clarity and usability of the Zoning Code, which in effect can help small 
businesses better navigate requirements, avoid displacement, and expand operations. Additionally, 
streamlined permitting and clearer regulations will support implementation of Action LU 11.11 
(Commercial Displacement) by helping allow businesses to continue operating during redevelopment 
and by encouraging the return of displaced businesses to new or renovated commercial spaces. 
Together, these policies and actions reinforce the General Plan’s commitment to a resilient local 
economy.  
The CZCU will also implement Policy LU 12.1 (Revenue Generators) by supporting land uses that 
diversify tax revenue sources and by aligning commercial regulations with the City’s broader efforts to 
sustain essential public services. Through the CZCU, San Mateo can better retain a strong mix of local-
serving and regionally oriented businesses, respond to economic trends, and develop diverse types of 
commercial uses that advances the City’s economic development and sustainability goals. 
Shopping Areas in Transition and Environmental Justice 
As San Mateo’s commercial areas continue to adapt to changing market conditions, community needs, 
and long-term land use goals, the CZCU provides an opportunity to implement regulations that 
strengthen neighborhood-serving uses, promote equitable access to services, and guide the 
redevelopment of key shopping areas. 
GOAL LU-7 establishes the City’s commitment to supporting the transition of existing shopping areas 
into vibrant, mixed-use districts that provide a range of housing, shopping, services, jobs, and 
community amenities. Consistent with Policy LU 7.1 (Shopping Areas in Transition), the CZCU will help 
guide the redevelopment of older commercial centers and corridors by allowing higher-density, mixed-
use redevelopment that integrates restaurants, services, diverse commercial uses, housing, and open 
space while continuing to preserve essential neighborhood-serving uses.  
GOAL LU-8 emphasizes promoting equitable health opportunities across all San Mateo neighborhoods, 
especially in equity priority communities. In line with Policy LU 8.14 (Retail Food Sources), the CZCU will 
~ 
PLACEWORKS 
167 of 719

--- page 168 ---
January 9, 2026 | Page 7 
support efforts to retain and expand access to affordable, healthy food options, including grocery 
stores, specialty food markets, farmers markets, community gardens, and convenience stores offering 
fresh food to areas with the most need. By updating the existing use definitions, broadening permitted 
food-related uses, and reducing regulatory barriers in commercial districts, the CZCU will support 
attracting a diverse range of food options in San Mateo.  
State Law Compliance 
There are no recent state laws related to uses regulated in commercial zoning districts that need to be 
addressed in the CZCU. 
Best Practice Options and Considerations 
This section outlines best practices and key considerations drawn from City staff input, state guidance, 
and comparable jurisdictions. The Project Team reviewed best practices from comparable jurisdictions 
to ensure the updated regulations reflect current planning standards successfully applied in comparable 
communities. These references help identify approaches that are clear, consistent, and adaptable to 
San Mateo’s goals and current built environment. The comparable jurisdictions referenced in the white 
paper include the cities of Milpitas and Stockton. The following considerations will help inform updates 
to the C1, C2, C3, and C4 Districts use regulations: 
• 
The list of permitted uses and uses that required a SUP in the C1, C2, C3, and C4 Districts would 
benefit from being modernized. For example, these four Districts include a use called “printing, 
lithographing, or publishing establishments for newspaper and business cards,” which is an 
overly specific use name. Instead, this use can be consolidated within a broader category with 
similar uses and impacts. Per Policy LU 3.3, Neighborhood Commercial and Service Uses, Action 
LU 3.18, Permitted Uses, and Policy LU 11.1, Economic Development, the City will evaluate all 
permitted uses and uses that require a SUP in these Districts while also retaining and 
modernizing local-serving retail and service use types.  
• 
The lists of permitted and special uses would benefit from being consolidated and reorganized 
into a comprehensive, user-friendly use table organized with broader use categories (e.g., 
Residential, Commercial, Vehicle-Related, Industrial, etc.). Many existing uses are outdated and 
difficult to navigate in these four Districts (as well as the rest of the SMMC) because they are 
presented as individual sections rather than grouped in a table. Additionally, several uses 
include supplemental regulations that would be more effectively presented as either (1) 
reference notes within a use table, or (2) moved and organized as their own section of the 
Zoning Code in a “Specific to Use” chapter, instead of being listed as text in separate 
subsections.  
• 
The current organization of uses in the commercial district chapters follows a cumulative 
structure, where each successive district includes all uses permitted in the previous one (i.e., 
~ 
PLACEWORKS 
168 of 719

--- page 169 ---
January 9, 2026 | Page 8 
the C2 District includes all C1 uses, the C3 District includes all C1 and C2 uses, and the C4 District 
includes all C1, C2, and C3 uses). Replacing this structure with a modern, comprehensive use 
table would align the SMMC with best practices, improve clarity, and reduce confusion. A new 
approach would also better distinguish the permitted uses and development standards unique 
to each district and could, through more intentional selection of uses, better achieve the intent 
of each of the General Plan’s distinctive use classifications. This update is especially important 
given the City’s current challenges interpreting parking regulations in the C3 District, which is 
intended for larger-scale, auto-oriented commercial uses but also allows neighborhood-serving 
uses typical of the C1 District. 
• 
The CZCU will adopt provisions to regulate potentially controversial or impactful uses, such as 
service stations, 24-hour convenience stores, fast-food restaurants, and drive-through 
establishments. For example, the CZCU will distinguish when these uses are permitted by right 
versus when they require a conditional use permit (the anticipated new term for SUPs), which 
will vary where and how the use is allowed based on the purpose of the specific zoning district. 
The CZCU will also include a chapter dedicated to providing development and operational 
requirements specific to uses deemed potentially controversial or impactful. Specific 
regulations for these potentially controversial or impactful uses will include, but not be limited 
to, distance requirements to other uses, hours of operation requirements, and site 
maintenance regulations. These changes support maintaining vibrant and compatible 
commercial areas consistent with Policy LU 3.4, Neighborhood Commercial Preservation, Policy 
LU 3.6, Service Commercial/Light Industrial, and Policy LU 12.1, Revenue Generators. 
• 
As discussed in the previous note, the CZCU will include development and operational 
requirements for uses that may be deemed potentially controversial or impactful, such as 
service stations. Given the shift away from fossil fuels and toward electric vehicles, the Project 
Team will research comparable jurisdictions and best practices for allowing and regulating 
service stations for non-gas-fueled vehicles. The Project Team will review different types of 
service stations to allow, such as those for electric vehicles, and consider allowing revenue-
generating ancillary uses on the same sites, such as convenience markets and vehicle washing. 
The Project Team will also assess how to shift away from ancillary uses at service stations that 
may be more similar to industrial uses, such as vehicle repair. Additionally, the Project Team 
will assess development and operational requirements for drive-through establishments given 
the changing landscape of how these facilities function with online ordering and mobile pick-
up options (e.g., Uber Eats and DoorDash). This assessment will consider factors such as site 
circulation and queuing, pedestrian safety, impacts to adjacent uses, and noise levels. The 
Project Team will also review comparable jurisdictions and emerging best practices for 
regulating drive-through operations to ensure that standards align with evolving business 
models, new technologies, and community goals. 
• 
As part of the CZCU, the City is aiming to support and incentivize grocery stores and other key 
neighborhood-serving commercial uses. To address this goal of supporting and incentivizing 
~ 
PLACEWORKS 
169 of 719

--- page 170 ---
January 9, 2026 | Page 9 
other key neighborhood-serving commercial uses, the City will consider adopting the following 
provisions in the CZCU:  
o 
Modernize the purpose statement sections of the commercial districts, except for 
Service Commercial, to address the City’s stated goals;  
o 
Allow uses by right in appropriate commercial zoning districts to reduce barriers to 
receiving entitlements; 
o 
Modify development standards (e.g., setbacks and height limits) and design standards 
(e.g., landscaping and shading) to increase the feasibility of project development;  
o 
Allow for ministerial or expedited review processes for qualifying grocery stores and 
small markets; 
o 
Update use definitions to be flexible and incorporate a wide range of modern types of 
grocery stores (e.g., small markets and food cooperatives); and/or  
o 
Provide incentives to encourage grocery stores in large mixed-use developments. The 
Project Team will research what types of incentives may be offered to developers to 
increase the number of food store options in San Mateo.  
• 
As part of the CZCU, the City is aiming to support and incentivize the development of hotel and 
lodging uses, which are critical to the City’s regional and visitor economy and an important 
revenue source. To address this goal, the City will consider adopting the following provisions in 
the CZCU, which advances Policy LU 3.7, Hotel, and Policy LU 3.8, Visitor Economy, by 
facilitating new hotel development and reinforcing the visitor economy: 
o 
Modernize the purpose statement for applicable commercial districts to reflect the 
City’s economic development and tourism objectives; 
o 
Consider a lodging/hospitality overlay that allows hotel and lodging uses by right in 
appropriate commercial zoning districts and/or areas of the City to reduce barriers to 
receiving entitlements; 
o 
Modify development standards (e.g., setbacks and height limits) to increase the 
feasibility of new hotel development and reinvestment in existing lodging options; 
o 
Establish ministerial or expedited review processes for qualifying hotel projects to 
shorten approval timelines; 
o 
Update use definitions to be flexible and incorporate a broad range of modern lodging 
uses (e.g., boutique hotels and extended-stay accommodations); and/or 
o 
Provide incentives within large mixed-use developments or opportunity areas to 
encourage the inclusion of hotels and a range of lodging options in project 
developments. 
~ 
PLACEWORKS 
170 of 719

--- page 171 ---
January 9, 2026 | Page 10 
• 
While the new commercial zoning districts will allow a range of modern, frequently utilized, 
and revenue-generating uses, the allowed uses will be compared for consistency with the goals 
of the Economic Development Work Plan, such as expanding and diversifying the City’s tax 
revenue sources, enhancing entertainment and cultural opportunities, and supporting and 
increasing employment training opportunities, which are efforts aligned with Policy LU 3.5, 
Support Service Uses, and Policy LU 3.8, Visitor Economy. 
• 
As part of the CZCU, the City will research the distinctions between office- and employment-
based uses, and retail, restaurant, personal services, and entertainment uses in the existing 
Code, and review how comparable jurisdictions address this distinction in their use definitions 
and development and operational standards. The CZCU will ensure that regulations are clear 
and applied consistently to the appropriate allowed uses across commercial districts, and that 
development standards are appropriately aligned with the specific scale and function of each 
use.

that apply.): 
o 
Allow them in all of the commercial districts 
o 
Allow them only in regional commercial areas  
o 
Require a conditional use permit 
o 
Require a revokable administrative permit with performance standards 
o 
Prohibit them entirely 
o

better serve San Mateo’s neighborhoods? 
•

•

MEMORANDUM  
DATE 
January 22, 2026 
TO 
City of San Mateo 
FROM 
PlaceWorks 
SUBJECT 
Key Issues White Paper – Single-Family Residential Regulations 
Introduction 
Single-family, low-density residential districts are evolving to meet the changing needs of Bay Area 
households. Today, more residents are working from home, families experiencing housing affordability 
constraints are more likely to include multiple generations living under one roof, and more older adults 
are choosing to age in place. To address these changes, California has introduced an increasing number 
of new housing legislation that aims to support the evolving needs of residents and address statewide 
housing shortages. Some of these laws aim to increase housing opportunities in single-family residential 
districts, including measures like Senate Bill (SB) 9, SB 1123, and more flexible accessory dwelling units 
(ADUs) regulation. 
The regulations for the Single-Family Residential (R1) District are found in Chapter 27.18 in the City’s 
Zoning Code in Title 27 of the San Mateo Municipal Code (SMMC). This chapter, which has not been 
comprehensively updated since 1992, aims to create, preserve, and enhance single-family areas with 
larger yard areas. To comply with state law (SB 9), the City adopted Chapter 27.21 in 2022, creating a 
Two-Unit Development Residential Overlay District for R1 zones. This overlay provides specific 
regulations for two-unit developments and urban lot splits. The upcoming Comprehensive Zoning Code 
Update (CZCU) will update the R1 District (Chapter 27.18) to reflect recent best practices and state law, 
while retaining the existing SB 9 overlay district, which is currently consistent with State law.  
This white paper includes a review of the existing single-family residential (SFR) regulations in the Zoning 
Code, directions and policies in the General Plan, recent state bills, best practice options and 
considerations, and questions to help facilitate public input on this topic.   
City and Community Goals  
The City has several goals for updating the current SFR regulations, including complying with recent 
state housing laws, converting subjective design guidelines into transparent and objective standards in 
the code and accompanying Objective Design Standards, simplifying development standards, and 
streamlining the approval process. Building a single-family home should be a clear and relatively easy 
172 of 719

--- page 173 ---
January 22, 2026 | Page 2 
process for someone unfamiliar with development who may only ever build or remodel a home once in 
their life.  The City is seeking feedback on ways to simplify this process for property owners and provide 
flexibility to meet the needs of the City’s diverse housing types and households, while ensuring new 
homes fit in with their neighbors. As part of this effort, the City is assessing parking regulations to 
determine the right requirements for single-family properties, studying similar cities to understand how 
they have made it easier to build and adapt homes, and learning from the public about what they want 
when it comes to site design, public noticing, special cases and exceptions, and how to treat special 
uses and neighborhood-serving commercial uses. 
Overview of Key Issue Topic 
As a result of recent state laws and regulations, jurisdictions must comply with statewide housing 
legislation which focuses on reducing constraints for housing development across different zoning 
districts and streamlining review of housing. Jurisdictions are required to comply with state housing law, 
which is implemented through local Zoning Codes. Single-family residential (SFR) regulations that are 
clear and well-designed are important for building context-sensitive neighborhoods and providing 
diverse housing types. 
Adopting Zoning Code regulations based on the current environment and future community goals can 
lead to the development of new housing opportunities while being designed to blend in with the visual 
and social fabric of existing neighborhoods. Incorporating development standards, such as building 
height, setbacks, and lot sizes, that are sensitive to the existing community can help ensure new housing 
is compatible with the surrounding environment. At the same time, adopting development standards 
that reflect recent state laws provides property owners opportunities to develop a greater variety of 
housing types on their own lots to increase the housing supply for family, caregivers, and individuals 
seeking rental opportunities.  
In addition to the development standards in SFR neighborhoods, adopting clear and appropriate 
administration and procedural requirements for the Planning Application review process helps 
streamline the approval process for owners seeking additions, remodels and/or new construction. 
When the process for developing single-family homes is clear and easy-to-use, the cost of projects 
typically decreases and projects are approved faster, benefiting the property owners, residents, and the 
City alike as this can support increased homeowner investment in their neighborhoods. Well-written 
SFR regulations ensure compliance with state law requirements, promote community growth, and help 
provide a framework to streamline development while aligning with neighborhood priorities. 
Brief Summary of Current Title 27 (Zoning) Regulations 
In the current Zoning Code, Chapter 27.18 (R1 Districts – One Family Dwellings) establishes regulations 
for SFR development within the R1 District. Chapter 27.18 outlines the purpose of this District, 
permitted and special uses, and development standard regulations. The Chapter provides standards for 
173 of 719

--- page 174 ---
January 22, 2026 | Page 3 
SFR development including, but not limited to, building height and daylight plane, parcel size standards, 
floor area ratio, and setbacks and exceptions to setbacks (i.e., encroachments).  
This Chapter also contains the following development regulations unique to the City’s Zoning Code for 
SFR projects, organized under the following sections: 
• 
Section 27.18.035 (Substantial Removal of Existing Residence): This Section states that a Special 
Use Permit is required for the substantial removal of an existing residence without a change in 
use. This Section also outlines related requirements and exemptions.  
• 
Section 27.18.115 (San Mateo Park Planning Area): This Section provides setback regulations 
for the San Mateo Park Planning Area, which requires new residential products to be built at a 
deeper setback than allowed in other areas of San Mateo. 
The City also adopted the “Single-Family Dwelling Design Guidelines” in 2001, which were most recently 
revised in 2006. While these guidelines are required to apply to SFR projects through the Single-Family 
Dwelling Design Review (SFDDR) process per Section 27.08.032(b)(1), they exist as a standalone 
document that has not been codified in the SMMC. Because the guidelines are not written as objective 
design standards, often as considerations or suggestions rather than firm requirements, they can be 
difficult to interpret and enforce objectively, which may lead to inconsistencies in how requirements 
are applied to different projects. 
General Plan’s Direction and Policies  
This section provides the General Plan 2040 policies and action that will be considered and taken into 
account when drafting the updated SFR regulations in the SMMC. 
Policy LU 3.1 - Housing Diversity 
Policy LU 3.1, Housing Diversity, directs the City to “promote safe, attractive, and walkable residential 
neighborhoods with diverse types and sizes of homes for individuals, families, and households of all 
income levels.”  
To address this policy, the CZCU will establish clear and objective development and design standards 
for housing projects for a variety of types and sizes, to meet the needs of households of all income levels 
in San Mateo. Additionally, the City will review and revise the permitted housing types to encourage 
innovative and flexible housing opportunities. 
Policy CD 7.1 - Low-Density Residential Development  
Policy CD 7.1, Low Density Residential Development, directs the City to require new homes in the Low- 
and Very Low-Density residential zoning district (i.e., single-family dwellings, duplexes, triplexes, 
fourplexes, and ADUs) to be consistent with the objective design standards outlined in the City’s 
Residential Design Standards.  
174 of 719

--- page 175 ---
January 22, 2026 | Page 4 
To address this policy, since these design guidelines are overall subjective in nature, the City will need 
to adopt new objective design standards for single-family development in San Mateo. However, the 
adoption of these new standards will occur after the completion of the CZCU. Until the new regulations 
are adopted, the City will maintain the current practice of referencing and applying the existing design 
guidelines document. 
Policy CD 7.2 - Single-Family Design  
Policy CD 7.2, Single Family Design, directs the City to “encourage single-family additions and new 
dwellings that address the preservation and enhancement of neighborhood visual and architectural 
character through context-sensitive building scale, materials, architectural style and details, and 
privacy.”  
To address this policy, the CZCU will include clear and balanced development and design standards for 
SFR projects that consider the various neighborhood contexts of San Mateo. While these standards will 
consider the City’s existing architectural design, they will also recognize that high-quality, context-
sensitive design does not always equate to maintaining the existing architectural style of a 
neighborhood. In some San Mateo neighborhoods, such as those with primarily post-war ranch homes, 
a variety of architectural styles may result in a better neighborhood design. In others, deviation from 
more traditional architectural styles could be accomplished by using modern materials and 
incorporating similar proportions and roof forms. Together, the CZCU will support both cohesive and 
diverse architectural design styles to ensure flexibility for innovative design styles when determining 
the development and design regulations for SFR districts. As discussed above in Policy CD 7.1, while 
there will be some design standards in the Zoning Code, single-family design will primarily be addressed 
through the development of objective design standards, which is planned to happen after the adoption 
of the CZCU.  
State Law Compliance 
SB 9 (Chapter 162, Statutes of 2021) requires jurisdictions to ministerially approve either up to two 
primary dwelling units on a parcel in a single-family zone (i.e., two-unit housing developments), the 
subdivision of a single parcel into two parcels (i.e., urban lot splits), or both. In 2022, the City adopted 
a new Two-Unit Development Residential Overlay District (Chapter 27.21). Since this Chapter has been 
recently updated, the CZCU process will not be updating the City’s SB 9 regulations.  
SB 1123 (Chapter 294, Statutes of 2024) expands upon SB 684 (2023) to further streamline the 
development of small-scale infill housing projects in SFR zones. SB 684 streamlined approvals for infill 
developments of up to 10 homes on vacant lots in multi-family residential districts. SB 1123 extends the 
provisions of SB 684 to allow construction of up to 10 homes on qualifying single-family parcels near 
commercial zones, schools, transit, and other amenities. The CZCU will assess how other jurisdictions 
have incorporated regulations to address SB 1123 and determine how to best apply these new 
regulations in the CZCU. 
175 of 719

--- page 176 ---
January 22, 2026 | Page 5 
Additionally, the City’s current parking requirements for SFR uses need to be further reviewed and 
updated in accordance with recent state legislation. This includes Assembly Bill (AB) 2097 (2022), which 
generally restricts jurisdictions from imposing minimum parking requirements for residential, 
commercial, and certain other development types within one-half mile of a major transit stop. 
Best Practice Options and Considerations 
This section outlines best practices and key considerations drawn from City staff input, state guidance, 
and comparable jurisdictions. The Project Team reviewed best practices from comparable jurisdictions 
to ensure the updated regulations reflect current planning standards successfully applied in comparable 
communities. These references help identify approaches that are clear, consistent, and adaptable to 
San Mateo’s goals and current built environment. The comparable jurisdictions referenced in this white 
paper include the cities of Milpitas, Sacramento, and Stockton.  
• 
Milpitas was chosen because it was just comprehensively updated in 2025, the city has a 
similar built environment to San Mateo and is located nearby, and the code includes clear 
writing and consistent organization. 
• 
Sacramento was chosen because it was seen as a good example by city staff and contains a 
detailed list of administrative and permit processes and procedures.  
• 
Stockton was chosen because it was just comprehensively updated in 2025, the use 
regulations and development standards are clear and well-written, and there are detailed and 
easy-to-use site development and specific to use regulations. 
The following considerations will help inform how to update the City’s SFR regulations: 
• 
As discussed in the “Brief Summary of Current Title 27 (Zoning) Regulations” above, Section 
27.18.035 (Substantial Removal of Existing Residence) states that a Special Use Permit is 
required for the substantial removal of a residence that does not change the use of the 
structure. However, if the City decides to retain the Single Family Dwelling Design Review 
(SFDDR) process, substantial removals and remodel should be identified as a rebuild or a new 
single-family residence to establish a clear expectation for which project is subject to the 
process. The CZCU will also evaluate whether to remove planning application level review for 
substantial removal of walls or roofs if no second-story addition is proposed because single-
story detached residences on stand-alone lots typically generate little, if any, controversy, 
particularly when designed in compliance with the current design guidelines and forthcoming 
objective design standards. Additionally, if an existing residence does not currently comply 
with all development standards, the CZCU process will clarify if substantial removal of a 
residence requires the proposed building to fully conform to all current development 
standards (i.e., if the existing building is non-conforming to any development standards) or if a 
substantial removal shall require an applicant to rebuild the subject development.  
• 
The current Zoning Code outlines identical requirements of limits on front paving setback 
areas in Sections 27.18.070(d) (Front Yard) and 27.18.080(b)(4) (Side Yards). The CZCU process 
will determine how to combine these regulations under one section to be written more 
176 of 719

--- page 177 ---
January 22, 2026 | Page 6 
clearly and reflect how comparable jurisdictions organize similar regulations. Additionally, 
these sections do not provide specific landscaping and vegetation requirements within heavily 
paved areas. The CZCU will establish requirements that set a maximum amount of impervious 
surface allowed in front yards that applies equally to walkways and driveways. It will also 
consider setting a minimum amount of impervious surface needed to access garages and 
building entrances, particularly on narrow lots where access is challenging. This approach 
would give residents flexibility in how they allocate their allowed impervious surface 
allotment. Furthermore, it would specifically support senior residents by providing additional 
paved area to address walking and accessibility concerns, such as connecting ramps into 
existing homes. In addition, the CZCU will evaluate options for accommodating additional off-
street parking on paved areas in situations where AB 2097 limits parking requirements and 
when garages are converted to ADUs or JADUs.  
• 
Section 27.18.080 (Side Yards) and Section 27.18.070(b) (Garage Setback) require a minimum 
of a 20-foot setback for garages on street side yards and front yards. The CZCU process will 
evaluate if this requirement should be retained. Some other jurisdictions, such as the City of 
Milpitas and the City of Sacramento, do not provide additional requirements for setbacks or 
garages on street side yards and front yards, while the City of Stockton does also require a 
minimum of 20-foot setback. 
• 
The City will consider if projects proposing extensions of walls within nonconforming side 
yards should still follow the Site Plan and Architectural Review (SPAR) process as required in 
Section 27.18.080(c)(4). Although comparable jurisdictions also require a site plan and 
architectural review process for the extensions or expansions of nonconforming features in 
SFR zoning districts, the SMMC also includes the SFDDR process. The SFDDR should be 
evaluated to determine if this process is a better option for SFR projects with proposed 
extensions of nonconforming walls within side yards. 
• 
The CZCU will determine how to update the parking requirements in Section 27.18.110 (Off-
Street Parking) related to single-family development. For example, allowing tandem parking 
for SFR developments, making covered parking requirements more flexible, assessing the 
number of parking stalls required for larger homes, and looking at ways to incentivize off-
street parking in areas where parking requirements cannot be enforced. 
• 
The CZCU will review all single-family setback requirements (front, side and rear) to assess 
how San Mateo’s requirements compare with other similar jurisdictions and if any changes or 
updates are warranted.  
• 
To support General Plan Policy CD 7.2, Single-Family Design, the City will be looking at a future 
effort to convert the single-family design guidelines into objective design standards (ODS). For 
the most part, the Zoning Code will not include architectural design standards; however, the 
Code does have the ability to support design objectives, and to that extent, the CZCU will be 
assessing opportunities for appropriate inclusion of requirements to support single-family 
design goals. 
• 
The SMMC does not currently have provisions that address requirements for double frontage 
or through lots. Comparable jurisdictions such as the City of Milpitas and the City of Stockton 
177 of 719

--- page 178 ---
January 22, 2026 | Page 7 
include a description of front lot lines for through lots in the definitions section. The CZCU will 
adopt a similar approach to incorporate the definition for double frontage or through lots. 
• 
The SMMC currently provides very limited regulations that address irregularly shaped lots. 
Chapter 27.21 (Two-Unit Development Residential Overlay District – R1 Districts), which 
provides the City’s regulations to comply with SB 9 requiring jurisdictions to allow two-unit 
developments and urban lot splits in low-density residential districts, includes a definition for 
flag or panhandle lots (Section 27.21.020(f)) and incorporates minimum width requirements 
for access strips or easements for flag lots and landlocked parcels created through urban lot 
splits. Except for these regulations, the SMMC does not include standards that address 
irregularly shaped lots for the purposes of ensuring consistent and compatible development 
patterns, preserving the existing building configurations, and avoiding access issues as a result 
of atypical lot shapes. Comparable jurisdictions such as the City of Milpitas and the City of 
Stockton allow the Director to determine  lot depth, width, and setbacks for irregularly 
shaped lots in the definitions or Rules of Measurement sections. The CZCU will take a similar 
approach to incorporate regulations for irregularly shaped lots.  
• 
Small lots that do not meet current minimum lot requirements in the SMMC can pose 
challenges for development potential, nonconforming status, and consistency with existing 
neighborhood context. To address this issue, the CZCU will further research pathways to 
accommodate those parcels.  
• 
The permitted and special uses in the R1 District contain outdated use names (e.g., 
sanitariums) and regulations that apply to specific types of uses, such as the regulations for 
cemeteries, mausoleums, and columbaria (Section 27.18.120). The CZCU will add categories 
of uses (e.g., residential, commercial, industrial) to organize permitted and special uses to be 
easier to apply, reflect current practices, comply with state law, and provide regulations for 
specific uses allowed in that may benefit from additional development and operational 
standards.  
• 
The City has extensive noticing requirements which exceed state requirements and those of 
other comparable jurisdictions around the Bay Area. Redwood City follows state requirements 
(see the “State Law Compliance” section of the Public Noticing white paper for details on 
recent legislative changes) with the exception of additional requirements for the Downtown 
Specific Plan. Sunnyvale sends out notices to property owners with 300 feet.  By comparison 
the City of San Mateo requires notice to property owners and tenants within a radius of up to 
500 feet from the project site. There is a significant cost to the distribution of these notices, 
which limits residents’ ability to invest in their homes and neighborhoods. The CZCU will 
assess and recommend updates to the City’s notification requirements, in particular for 
single-family projects, to reduce cost burdens and better align with best practices while being 
mindful of community outreach expectations.  
178 of 719

--- page 179 ---
January 22, 2026 | Page 8

MEMORANDUM  
DATE 
January 22, 2026 
TO 
City of San Mateo 
FROM 
PlaceWorks 
SUBJECT 
Key Issues White Paper – Public Noticing 
Introduction 
Following adoption of Strive San Mateo General Plan 2040, the City of San Mateo started the 
Comprehensive Zoning Code Update (CZCU) project to implement and achieve consistency with the 
General Plan. As part of the CZCU, the administrative and procedural requirements of the San Mateo 
Municipal Code (SMMC) will be updated to comply with recent state laws and best practices for 
facilitating equitable community engagement, which is a General Plan priority. Appropriate public 
noticing periods are vital for the administration and procedural regulations in zoning codes. It is also 
vital that community members understand the ways in which they may be able to participate in the 
development review process and provide input on a proposal before it is approved, as well as what 
understand the authority the City has to approve, deny, or regulate a project. The CZCU offers an 
opportunity to update the City’s public noticing requirements to clarify and right-size community 
engagement and public input from residents and stakeholders so expectations surrounding this part of 
the application process are clear from the earliest stages of project initiation. It will also include 
application process requirements that can assist applicants in streamlining the application process and 
increase their ability to receive project approval in a timely manner.  
This white paper includes a review of the existing public noticing requirements in the zoning code, 
directions and policies in the General Plan, the current related zoning code provisions, two recent state 
bills related to public noticing requirements, best practice options and considerations, and questions 
that will be asked for the public to provide input.   
City and Community Goals  
The City’s current public noticing requirements for projects are more extensive than many nearby 
comparable jurisdictions, especially when it comes to single-family projects. The CZCU provides an 
opportunity to assess how best to provide the community with opportunities for public input and 
feedback in advance of new development and major policy decisions while managing expectations 
about how that input can be used to shape outcomes. Another objective is to find an appropriate 
balance that keeps residents well-informed about proposed development projects in compliance with 
182 of 719

--- page 183 ---
January 22, 2026 | Page 2 
state law and consistent with the General Plan while also not adding excessive time delays and costs to 
projects. With evolving and changing forms of communication, the City is also seeking to update the 
methods used to provide effective public notification and community engagement. 
Overview of Key Issue Topic 
In zoning codes, public noticing requirements ensure that residents are able to learn about and provide 
input on new projects and policies, make the community aware of public meetings and hearings, and 
ensure that the City meets legal requirements under state law. Properly notifying the public of 
upcoming public meetings and involving all relevant stakeholders early in the application review process 
keeps the public informed on changes happening in the community, provides applicants the opportunity 
to explain their proposed projects to residents and stakeholders, and allows residents and stakeholders 
to share feedback with applicants and decisionmakers. California law also includes several requirements 
that jurisdictions and property owners must adhere to for public noticing procedures, such as project 
timelines, distribution methods, and specific content required to be included in public notices.  
Local agencies have the option to exceed minimum legal public noticing requirements in an effort to 
broaden and deepen community engagement. However, in some cases, particularly regarding 
applications for housing developments, state law limits local agencies in what community engagement 
they can require of an applicant, as well as in what discretion the local agency has to regulate a project. 
In practice, the additional local efforts beyond the minimum legal requirements often are implemented 
by using multiple methods for outreach, hosting public workshops and study sessions, and distributing 
informational materials to residents and stakeholders. By understanding the preferred options for how 
residents and stakeholders receive notices of what type of events and occurrences happening in San 
Mateo, the zoning code can be tailored to adopt public noticing requirements and procedures that 
inform residents and stakeholders about the development projects happening citywide. 
Brief Summary of Current Title 27 (Zoning) Regulations 
The City follows specific procedures to notify residents about proposed projects. These procedures are 
outlined in Section 27.08.050 (Notice of Application) and other sections in the SMMC. Section 27.08.050 
(Notice of Application) includes the required methods for distributing public notices, radius for 
distribution, content required for inclusion in the notice, notice period requirements, and other public 
notice requirements. Additionally, this Section provides brief guidelines on what happens if the City fails 
to give notice in accordance with this Section and if public notice is not received by someone within the 
proposed project distribution radius.  
While the Section provides several public notice requirements for public hearings, the Zoning Code lacks 
specific guidelines for public meetings (e.g., Neighborhood Meetings). Furthermore, the Section does 
not define noticing radii for different types of applications or provide other regulations that would be 
beneficial to support applicants and the community. These requirements have been adopted by the 
183 of 719

--- page 184 ---
January 22, 2026 | Page 3 
City Council through resolutions with the most recent update in July 2024 (Resolution No. 65 (2024) - 
Early Community Engagement Process). 
Planning Application Guide 
To implement the Early Community Engagement Process adopted by the City Council, the City published 
the Planning Application Guide (PAG) in March 2025, which explains the planning application process 
and provides an overview of the public noticing requirements in the zoning code and resolution. The 
current process requires applicants to provide mailed notices to property owners and tenants either 
adjacent to the proposed project or within radii of 500 feet or 1,000 feet, depending on project type, 
and email notices to the City’s maintained interested parties list. Based on the project type, applicants 
may also be required to hold a neighborhood meeting prior to submitting the application.  Projects of a 
certain size are also offered the opportunity to attend an optional Planning Commission study session.  
The PAG organizes projects by four-tier levels that are based on the notice radius required and the 
corresponding approval body (Zoning Administrator, Planning Commission or City Council) for the 
project types listed. The PAG also provides direction on the review procedures, neighborhood 
notification and meeting process, materials for public study sessions, sample meeting notices, and 
additional resources for applicants.  
Currently, according to the PAG, an applicant might be required to go through at least two, and up to 
three or four, rounds of public noticing procedures, not including CEQA requirements or an appeals 
process. The Zoning Administrator, Planning Commission, and City Council each have the power to make 
the final decision on different application types. 
General Plan Direction and Policies  
The General Plan update process included the development of “10 Big Ideas” to highlight the guiding 
principles for the City over the next 20 years, blending goals and ideas from the past general plan with 
new concepts to address current challenges. One of these Big Ideas, "Strengthen Community 
Outreach," is a central principle for both the General Plan and the upcoming CZCU. This commitment 
requires prioritizing extensive community engagement through culturally sensitive methods to 
encourage early communication and broad representation. To achieve this in the CZCU process, the City 
will offer information in the predominant community languages, schedule meetings at convenient times 
and locations for community members and utilize multiple methods and mediums to support public 
awareness and participation. 
The following General Plan 2040 goals, policies, and actions on community engagement will inform the 
updated public noticing procedures that go into the zoning code. 
Goal LU-9 
Goal LU-9 was incorporated in General Plan 2040 to guide the City’s approach to inclusive community 
outreach and resident participation in planning decisions. Goal LU-9 states that the City will “include 
184 of 719

--- page 185 ---
January 22, 2026 | Page 4 
everyone in community development decisions for a shared, sustainable future.” The CZCU will 
implement this goal by clarifying the community engagement process of public noticing requirements 
and streamlining the application process for project approval.  
Implementing Policies and Actions 
To implement Goal LU-9, the General Plan includes several policies to facilitate equitable and inclusive 
processes for public input during the project approval process. Policy LU 9.1 (Inclusive Outreach) and 
Policy LU 9.2 (Equitable Engagement) address the General Plan’s principle to prioritize community 
engagement through culturally sensitive methods and equitable approach to engaging with low-income 
and historically underrepresented communities. Policy LU 9.3 (Development Projects) and Policy LU 9.4 
(Applicant Communications) ensures that the public input process during project review is clearly 
communicated with community members and stakeholders. Related to these efforts, Policy LU 13.2 
(Public Education), which falls under Goal LU-13 in the General Plan, requires the City to promote public 
awareness of the development review and permitting process. 
Actions under Goal LU-9 address the implementation of inclusive and equitable community 
engagement and public input processes. Action LU 9.5 (Community Partners) ensures that the City 
works with community partners to engage with equity priority communities in planning and policy 
decisions. Action LU 9.6 (Community Surveys) directs the City to periodically conduct community 
surveys representative of the City’s demographics to gauge service needs, policy preferences, and 
effective communication methods.  
Action LU 9.7 (Communications Strategy) directs the City to “develop a communications strategy that 
outlines goals and tactics to engage a broad cross-section of the community.” The Action includes a list 
of specific actions the City commits to undertaking in order to implement the communications strategy. 
Specifically, the Action commits the City to the following: 
• 
Prepare public notices and other materials in the predominant language(s) spoken in the 
community and provide interpretation services at meetings as needed; and 
• 
Make public notices and other important documents available in print at local libraries, 
community centers, or other gathering places. 
While implementation of these policies and actions go beyond the CZCU, including preparation of a 
communications strategy and conducting community surveys, they do serve to inform and guide the 
updated notification requirements in the zoning code. This includes assessment of requirements for 
preparing public notices in the determined predominant languages spoken in San Mateo and 
distribution methods that can be most effective in reaching targeted residents and stakeholders.  
State Law Compliance 
Recent changes in state housing legislation and Brown Act updates may affect local planning and zoning 
processes. The following briefly summarizes key updates from recent bills: 
185 of 719

--- page 186 ---
January 22, 2026 | Page 5 
• 
AB 2904 (2024) updates public noticing requirements, extending the review period for public 
hearings from 10 days to 20 days when considering changes to allowed land uses or 
development standards (zoning code amendments). 
• 
AB 1007 (2025) shortens the timeline for a responsible agency to act on qualifying residential 
and mixed-use project applications under the Permit Streamlining Act (PSA). (A responsible 
agency is any public agency, other than the lead agency, that holds discretionary approval 
authority over all or any part of a project.) The bill reduces the deadline to approve or deny a 
project from 90 days to 45 days, triggered by whichever of the following occurs last: (1) the 
lead agency’s approval of the project, or (2) the responsible agency’s determination that the 
application is complete. Certain state agencies (e.g., such as the California Coastal Commission 
and the San Francisco Bay Conservation and Development Commission) remain subject to the 
original 90-day timeline. Ultimately, AB 1007 expedites the approval process for these projects, 
cutting the previous PSA requirement in half. 
• 
SB 158 (2025) builds on the PSA by clarifying which housing projects qualify for streamlined 
approval under state law and shortens the approval timeline for certain projects that are 
exempt from environmental review. The PSA requires local jurisdictions to expedite the 
approval process for development projects by establishing time limits to approve or deny 
permit applications. In part, SB 158 clarified the definition of a "housing development project" 
to identify which projects qualify for the expedited approval timeline under the PSA. These 
housing projects include infill development, affordable housing when providing a certain 
percentage of units to lower-income households, and mixed-use projects that provide a certain 
percentage of total square footage for residential units. 
Best Practice Options and Considerations 
This section outlines best practice options and key considerations drawn from City staff input, state 
guidance, and comparable jurisdictions. The Project Team reviewed best practices from comparable 
jurisdictions to ensure the updated regulations reflect current planning standards successfully applied 
in comparable communities. These references help identify approaches that are clear, consistent, and 
adaptable to San Mateo’s goals and current built environment. The comparable jurisdictions referenced 
in this white paper include the cities of Costa Mesa, Milpitas, Sacramento, Stockton, and Santa Clara 
County.  
• 
Costa Mesa was chosen because part of the code was recently updated and was seen as a good 
example by City staff.  
• 
Milpitas was chosen because it was just comprehensively updated in 2025, the City has a similar 
built environment to San Mateo and is located nearby, and the code includes clear writing and 
consistent organization. 
186 of 719

--- page 187 ---
January 22, 2026 | Page 6 
• 
Sacramento was chosen because it was seen as a good example by City staff and contains a 
detailed list of administrative and permit processes and procedures.  
• 
Stockton was chosen because it was just comprehensively updated in 2025, the use regulations 
and development standards are clear and well-written, and there are detailed and easy-to-use 
site development and specific to use regulations.  
• 
Santa Clara County was chosen because it is located nearby, it contains clear writing and 
organization, and is well-organized by different residential district types (e.g., Rural, Urban 
Residential). 
The following considerations will help inform updates to the City’s public noticing requirements: 
• 
AB 2904 and SB 158 were updated recently and the City’s public noticing requirements do not 
fully comply with these state bills. While none of the comparable jurisdictions were found to 
have incorporated the latest housing laws yet either, the City should update the public noticing 
requirements to be in compliance with these two bills. Effectively, this will require a change 
from at least 10 days to 20 days of public notice before a hearing for projects that include a 
land use change or code amendment.  
• 
Comparable jurisdictions use various methods for public notices. Methods include emails and 
newsletters, website postings, social media posts, mailers, newspapers, and other online and 
printed materials. Comparable jurisdictions also distinguish public notices by type, such as 
mailed notices, published notices, posted notices, and additional notice types. Since the zoning 
code currently only includes mail and e-mail as the two public noticing methods, the code 
should be updated to expand the public noticing method options.  
• 
Per General Plan Action LU 9.7 (Communications Strategy) public notices and related materials 
should be translated to the predominant language(s) spoken citywide. To address this Action, 
the City is developing a Language Access Plan that will be starting in January 2026. The goal of 
the Language Access Plan is to ensure that the City provides meaningful access to information, 
programs, benefits, and services to people with limited English proficiency and ensure that 
language is not a barrier to accessing vital city services. 
• 
As discussed above, the current zoning code does not specify the radius requirements for 
different permit types. Instead, the City published guidelines for public noticing requirements 
by project type as the PAG. While the PAG does provide the specific radius requirements, the 
zoning code does not include most of the regulations written in the PAG. The Project Team will 
seek City Council direction to determine what radius requirements to codify.  
• 
Based on current requirements, an applicant might be required to take part in the following 
public notice periods:  
o 
Notice for a pre-application neighborhood meeting (mailed); 
187 of 719

--- page 188 ---
January 22, 2026 | Page 7 
o 
Notice of a public hearing notice for Planning Commission and City Council, if required, 
(mailed and property posting);  
o 
Notice of pending decision (mailed) and notice of decision (emailed); and 
o 
Notice of an appeal hearing (if filed) (mailed and property posting). 
While this number of noticing periods is generally consistent with comparable jurisdictions, it 
should still be assessed for consistency with General Plan goals and policies, effectiveness, and 
cost/time burden in relation to the value of communication efforts.
