MEMORANDUM  
DATE 
January 22, 2026 
TO 
City of San Mateo 
FROM 
PlaceWorks 
SUBJECT 
Key Issues White Paper – Open Space 
Introduction 
Open space is a defining element of the City of San Mateo’s identity, shaping the character of its 
neighborhoods, supporting environmental health and enhancing the overall quality of life for residents, 
workers and visitors. San Mateo’s open spaces provide essential places for recreation and community 
connection, directly supporting the Strive San Mateo General Plan 2040 goal to enhance San Mateo’s 
neighborhood fabric and quality of life. As the city continues to evolve, preserving and thoughtfully 
integrating open space into new development will be critical to preserve nature as the foundation of 
the city – another goal of the General Plan. Understanding the community’s core goals and values as 
related to open space will be vital to ensuring that future growth strengthens both livability and the 
city’s natural environment.  
Prior to starting the Comprehensive Zoning Code Update (CZCU) project, the City drafted four new high 
density districts for residential and mixed-use zones to implement the highest allowed densities 
referenced in the General Plan (99 units/acre and 130 units/acre). Public hearings to consider adoption 
of these new high density districts are planned for the first half of 2026 to provide clarity to the General 
Plan story limits that are already in effect. The work helped inform the private open space requirements 
and regulations discussed in this white paper. In addition, this white paper presents options for how 
open space, both private and publicly accessible, can be incorporated into new development.  
This white paper includes a review of the existing open space regulations in the zoning code, goals and 
policies in the General Plan, recent state bills, best practice options and considerations, and questions 
that will be asked for the public to provide input.   
City and Community Goals  
The City is following the General Plan’s direction to expand public access to open spaces and to 
incentivize a variety of open space types, including privately-owned public open space, community 
gardens, multifamily open space, and public facilities such as parks and recreation centers. Guided by 
this framework, the City is seeking to have a public discussion on open space requirements in the Zoning 
190 of 719

--- page 191 ---
January 22, 2026 | Page 2 
Code to address regulations that include, but are not limited to, public versus private open space 
requirements and providing open space on rooftops. The City has raised a question about whether some 
of the current SMMC open space regulations (e.g., ground-level housing units having doors opening 
directly to open space areas) are a constraint for infill development projects. The City is also interested 
in researching methods to achieve additional open space areas in more urbanized, built-out 
environments, and the subsequent regulations to support this goal. Throughout this process, the City is 
aiming to provide both passive and active types of open space regulations in the zoning code.  
Overview of Key Issue Topic 
Open space regulations in zoning codes must balance multiple interconnected planning objectives. 
These include being calibrated to accommodate residential density requirements from the General 
Plan, supporting the development of appropriate building types, providing amenities and benefits for 
residents of specific projects as well as the entire city, and ensuring economic feasibility for developers. 
Effective regulations also rely on a clear understanding of a community’s existing conditions and address 
key development considerations such as access to light, ventilation, fire safety, and privacy. 
Public and private open space regulations vary based on location, building type, and development 
intensity. Private open space may be required for individual residential units, such as balconies, patios, 
and yards, in order to provide residents with their own private usable outdoor areas. Private open space 
may also be allowed in different forms in the indoor and outdoor common areas of buildings, such as 
community gardens and rooftops, community lounges and rooms, and fitness centers. Effective private 
open space regulations correlate with density requirements, provide clear and adaptable rules, and 
allow for flexibility in design. 
Publicly accessible open spaces, including those provided on private property, play a crucial role in 
enhancing connectivity and recreational opportunities. These open spaces commonly include 
courtyards and plazas, paseos, gardens, atriums, widened sidewalks, and rooftop areas. These open 
spaces are invaluable public assets that serve as a shared resource for both residents and visitors. They 
support ground-floor commercial vibrancy and offer outdoor areas for residents to gather, work, and 
relax.   
Brief Summary of Current Title 27 (Zoning) Regulations 
As Part of a Development Project 
The current SMMC includes private open space requirements for higher density residential 
development projects, but this is not applicable to all projects as these requirements only apply to a 
few zoning districts. The R3 District (Multiple Family Dwellings - Medium Density) provides private 
minimum open space requirements for multifamily developments. Section 27.22.100 (Open Space 
Requirements) requires that a minimum of 200 square feet of “open parcel area” is provided for the 
first bedroom in each dwelling unit, plus a minimum of 100 square feet is provided for each additional 
191 of 719

--- page 192 ---
January 22, 2026 | Page 3 
bedroom. These calculations do not include required off-street parking areas or driveways. The Section 
also provides the following guidelines that distinguish how ground-level units and upper-floor units each 
can meet the open space requirements: 
• 
Open space for ground-level units: 
o 
Must be contiguous to the unit; 
o 
Must have a minimum dimension of 10 feet in any direction; and 
o 
May include setback areas. 
• 
Open space for upper-floor units:  
o 
May be met by open roof decks, balconies, lanais, or other open areas if the area 
provides a minimum usable area of 100 square feet and is not less than six feet in any 
direction; 
o 
Allowed to be combined for several units as common usable open space; and 
o 
May be located entirely or partially on the ground level, if there is a minimum 
dimension of 10 feet in any direction, and does not need to be contiguous to the 
building. 
For residential high-rise buildings in the R5 District (Section 27.26.080 (Development Bonuses for 
Residential High-rise Buildings)), providing certain indoor and outdoor private recreational open space 
areas are an option for developers to be granted bonuses for additional floor area. Additionally, the 
Downtown Residential Districts (Chapter 27.28 (Downtown Residential Districts)) provide open space 
requirements for private and common usable open space areas. For the three Downtown Residential 
Districts (i.e., R-4D, R-5D, and R-6D), open space can be provided as either private or common open 
space. Private open space requirements range from a minimum of 80 to 100 square feet per dwelling 
unit and must comply with minimum dimensional requirements. Common open space requirements 
can be provided instead of private open space requirements by providing 150 percent of the private 
open space requirement as shared common open space. For example, when the private open space 
requirement is a minimum of 100 square feet, a project can instead provide 150 square feet of shared 
common space open space.   
Open Space Standards Analysis and Formula in the Proposed High Density 
Residential Zone Chapters 
As discussed in the “Overview” section above, four new HDRZ Districts (two residential-only districts 
and two mixed-use districts) have been drafted in order to comply with the density requirements for 
the four new land use designations that came into effect through the adoption of Strive San Mateo 
General Plan 2040. As part of these new districts, new development standards were developed using 
the City’s existing open space regulations in the R3 and R5 Districts and best practices from comparable 
jurisdictions as reference examples; however, it should be noted that the resulting open space 
requirements are distinct and specifically calibrated for the four new draft HDRZ chapters.  
192 of 719

--- page 193 ---
January 22, 2026 | Page 4 
Based on this work, the open space requirements in these districts include the same formula for all four 
HDRZ districts, which offers flexibility for developers to provide private usable or common usable open 
space or a combination of both open space types based on the number of housing units in a 
development project. Applicants may meet the open space requirements by providing either or a 
combination of the following: 
• 
Private open space only: A minimum of 50 square feet of private usable open space per unit; 
or  
• 
Common open space only: A minimum of 75 square feet of common usable open space per 
unit; or  
• 
Combination of private and common open space:  
o 
At least 30 square feet of private usable open space per unit; and  
o 
An amount of common usable open space equal to 150% of the shortfall between 
the 50-square-foot private open space requirement and the amount of private open 
space actually provided.  
• 
Example:  
o 
If a development provides 30 square feet of private usable open space per unit, the 
remaining shortfall is 20 square feet (50 − 30).  
o 
The required common usable open space would therefore be 30 square feet per unit 
(20 × 150%). 
The SMMC does not include specific regulations for POPOS in Title 27 (Zoning) or anywhere else in the 
Municipal Code. However, Section 27.04.350 (Open Space) includes the following definition for the 
term “Commercial/executive common usable open space:” “open space in the form of a plaza, square, 
court, or other urban space which is at least 75% open to the sky, free from automotive traffic, and 
accessible to the public at large.” While this term is included as a definition, it is not mentioned nor are 
there any relevant regulations elsewhere in the SMMC. 
General Plan’s Direction and Policies  
Strive San Mateo General Plan 2040 includes several policies that direct the City on how to incorporate 
and address public and private open space areas. The five policies discussed in this section provide 
guidance on how to incorporate public and private open space regulations during the CZCU process.  
Privately-Owned Public Open Space (POPOS)  
Taken together, Policy LU 3.12, Publicly Accessible Spaces; Policy COS 6.3, Privately Owned Public 
Spaces; and Policy CD 6.7, Public Open Space Design direct the City to expand public access to open 
spaces by integrating both publicly and privately owned and maintained spaces into new developments 
and public buildings. Furthermore, Policy COS 5.10, Community Gardens, directs the City to support 
community gardens on sites with quasi-public uses and on public land, such as City parks or facilities, or 
193 of 719

--- page 194 ---
January 22, 2026 | Page 5 
as part of new private development, provided it is feasible and appropriate. To ensure these areas are 
functional, welcoming, and accessible to everyone, all policies require clear signage indicating public 
access, creative and innovative design, and the removal of physical or visual barriers. The combined 
purpose of these policies is to create a vibrant, interconnected network of public and private open 
spaces that improves the character and quality of San Mateo for all residents. 
The CZCU will introduce new open space regulations that apply to both private developments and new 
public buildings. The policies will require these developments to include publicly accessible unique, and 
functional open space areas, such as community gardens. To incentivize compliance, it will be 
determined whether to incorporate into the CZCU the option for developers to receive special 
incentives for including a specified amount of open space. The design requirements for these spaces 
would include clear signage, enhanced user amenities, easy access, and features that promote a safe, 
welcoming, and functional environment that are open for public use. Furthermore, it will be considered 
whether it will be required for the open space areas to incorporate specific types of design elements, 
such as landscaping, sunlight access, seating, and user safety and security measures. 
Policy CD 7.5 – Multifamily Open Space  
Policy CD 7.5, Multifamily Open Space, directs the City to “require that a portion of required open space 
for new multifamily projects be useable for passive or active recreation.” 
To address this policy, the CZCU’s new open space requirements for multifamily residential projects will 
require a certain percentage of private common open space to be passive and/or active recreational 
areas. 
Policy PSF 5.7 – Incentives for Public Facilities 
Policy PSF 5.7, Incentives for Public Facilities, directs the City to “provide incentives to developers for 
projects that include needed space for public facilities in new development.” 
To address this policy, the CZCU’s new open space requirements will provide a list of incentives to 
encourage developers to provide dedicated space regulated by the percentage of the lot area reserved 
for public facilities in new development. The CZCU will also include a list of options of the public facilities, 
such as parks and community and recreation centers, that will qualify for the incentives. 
State Law Compliance 
There are no recent state laws related to open space requirements that need to be addressed in the 
CZCU. 
194 of 719

--- page 195 ---
January 22, 2026 | Page 6 
Best Practice Options and Considerations 
This section outlines best practice options and key considerations drawn from City staff input, state 
guidance, and comparable jurisdictions. The Project Team reviewed best practices from comparable 
jurisdictions to ensure the updated regulations reflect current planning standards successfully applied 
in comparable communities. These references help identify approaches that are clear, consistent, and 
adaptable to San Mateo’s goals and current built environment. The comparable jurisdictions referenced 
in this white paper include the cities of Milpitas, Oakland, and San Francisco. The following 
considerations will help inform updates to the City’s open space regulations: 
• 
As discussed in the “Open Space Standards Analysis and Formula in the Proposed High Density 
Residential Zone Chapters” section above, the four draft HDRZ districts include requirements 
for private and common open space in new developments. The proposed open space 
requirements were guided by the General Plan and considered input from the development 
community in San Mateo. The proposed open space standards for the HDRZ chapters will be 
considered when determining the open space requirements for the other zoning district 
chapters. 
• 
As discussed in the “Privately Owned Public Open Space (POPOS)” subsection above, the term 
“commercial/executive common usable open space,” which might benefit from being clarified, 
allows POPOS to occur in the form of plazas, squares, courts, or other urban open spaces in 
some areas of the city. Renaming and shortening this term would improve the ease of use of 
the standards and clarify what types of POPOS fall under this term in the SMMC. There are also 
several other types of POPOS that the SMMC does not refer to and that are allowed in 
comparable jurisdictions, such as playgrounds, parks, urban gardens, view terraces, 
greenhouses, atriums, indoor parks, and pedestrian walkways. The CZCU will consider allowing 
these types of POPOS in the municipal code, as well as renaming the term. 
• 
General Plan Policy LU 3.12, Publicly Accessible Spaces, directs the City to incorporate privately 
owned and maintained publicly accessible spaces into new development projects. While the 
SMMC identifies private and common usable open space categories, the Zoning Code does not 
include any POPOS regulations for development projects. To incentivize privately owned and 
maintained publicly accessible open spaces, the CZCU may consider incorporating said 
regulations for new developments similar to those provisions included in the City of San 
Francisco (see Section 138 (Privately-Owned Public Open Space (POPOS) Requirements)).  
• 
General Plan Policy CD 6.7, Public Open Space Design, directs the City to promote innovative 
and creative designs to create functional spaces in new developments and public buildings. 
These regulations are currently not provided in the SMMC. The CZCU process will include 
reviewing the design requirements for public open spaces adopted in zoning codes of 
comparable jurisdictions. 
• 
General Plan Policy COS 6.3, Privately Owned Public Spaces, directs the City to enhance the 
accessibility, usability, and overall quality of privately owned public spaces. These regulations 
195 of 719

--- page 196 ---
January 22, 2026 | Page 7 
are currently not provided in the SMMC. The CZCU should include regulations to ensure that 
privately owned public spaces are accessible and safe for public use. Specific guidelines in the 
CZCU may refer to the City of San Francisco’s regulations in Section 138, which require 
adequate ingress and egress access, well-designed landscapes, lighting, and clear signage 
indicating public access. In mixed-use districts, additional standards may address public access 
hours and amenities such as waste collection and publicly accessible potable water sources.  
• 
General Plan Policy CD 7.5, Multifamily Open Space, directs the City to require multifamily 
projects include a portion of the open space area be useable for passive or active recreation. 
The CZCU process will include reviewing comparable jurisdictions to determine (1) what 
percentage of the required area will be dedicated for usable passive and/or active recreation, 
and (2) what types of passive and active recreation will be allowed in these areas. 
• 
General Plan Policy PSF 5.7, Incentives for Public Facilities, directs the City to provide incentives 
to developers that dedicate space in new developments for public spaces. The CZCU process 
will include reviewing comparable jurisdictions to determine the percentages of the lot area 
that must be allowed for public facility areas, as well as a list of options of the public facilities 
that will qualify for the incentives.  
• 
Most comparable jurisdictions include rooftops as a form of private open space and provide 
regulations to guide their development. Jurisdictions like the City of Milpitas provide specific 
standards for rooftop open spaces, such as required landscaping and accessibility to rooftop 
amenities. The CZCU process will consider reviewing the City of Milpitas and other comparable 
jurisdictions to determine the rooftop open space regulations that may be adopted in San 
Mateo. 
• 
In high-density residential districts, open space can be provided with higher intensities of 
development by using flexible, alternative types of open or recreational space that support 
both active and passive recreation. These spaces may accommodate passive uses such as 
landscaping, seating areas, and small gathering areas, as well as active uses, such as 
playgrounds, community rooms, or fitness areas. For example, the City of San Francisco and 
the City of Milpitas establish usable open space requirements per dwelling unit to encourage 
open spaces in multifamily developments. The CZCU process will consider incorporating 
passive and active types of open space areas in higher-density districts. 
• 
For projects that propose new residential units that are not single-family dwellings or ADUs, a 
separate section of the Municipal Code (Chapter 26.64 and Section 13.05.070) requires 
payment of an in-lieu/impact fee (identical formula) for City park and recreation facilities. 
However, private park and recreation facilities provided as part of a project can be used as 
partial credit against the in-lieu/impact fee. Section 26.64.030(b) describes the types of park 
and recreational facilities that are eligible for credit. The CZCU will consider how to best align 
the open space requirements in the Zoning Code with these other code sections to ensure that 
the facilities eligible for credit are clear, compatible with development and easy to understand. 
196 of 719

--- page 197 ---
January 22, 2026 | Page 8

MEMORANDUM  
DATE 
January 22, 2026 
TO 
City of San Mateo 
FROM 
PlaceWorks 
SUBJECT 
Key Issues White Paper – Setbacks and Buffers  
Introduction 
Building setbacks establish the required distance between structures and property lines, streets, or 
other site features, and are a fundamental tool for regulating the scale, placement, and massing of 
development. Building setbacks play a critical role in shaping the physical form of San Mateo’s 
neighborhoods, influencing streetscape character, access to light and air, and privacy. When 
thoughtfully designed, setback standards help create safe, comfortable, and visually cohesive 
neighborhoods, directly supporting the Strive San Mateo General Plan 2040 goal to enhance San 
Mateo’s Neighborhood Fabric and Quality of Life. As the city continues to evolve under the guidance 
of the General Plan, understanding how setback requirements function across different contexts will 
be vital to ensuring that standards for future development are compatible with existing 
neighborhoods and contribute to high-quality design.  
Prior to starting the Comprehensive Zoning Code Update (CZCU) project, the City drafted four new 
high-density districts for residential and mixed-use zones to implement the highest allowed densities 
referenced in the General Plan (99 units/acre and 130 units/acre). Public hearings to consider 
adoption of these new high density districts are planned for the first half of 2026 to provide clarity to 
the General Plan story limits that are already in effect. The work helped inform the setback and buffer 
requirements and regulations for the City’s higher density zoning districts and uses discussed in this 
white paper.  
This white paper also addresses the setback and buffer regulations for the lower-density residential 
districts and all commercial, office, and manufacturing districts. The lower-density residential zoning 
districts (i.e., R1 and R2) provide setback regulations (referred to as “yards”) for front, side, and rear 
areas. This white paper includes a review of the existing setback and buffer regulations in the Zoning 
Code, directions and policies in the General Plan, recent state bills, best practice options and 
considerations, and questions that will be asked for the public to provide input.   
199 of 719

--- page 200 ---
January 22, 2026 | Page 2 
City and Community Goals  
The City wants to ensure the setback, buffer, and build-to-line requirements in the SMMC meet their 
desired objectives while also allowing for flexibility to support a range of project types. The City’s 
current setback and build-to-line standards are very prescriptive with a variety of requirements based 
on location and adjacent zoning districts. In addition, the setback standards need to be calibrated to 
ensure that they support the new density regulations in General Plan 2040. The buffer standards 
should balance encouraging development with maintaining appropriate buffer distances between less 
compatible uses. Additionally, the City wants to ensure that setback standards for smaller parcels do 
not make the parcels undevelopable. 
Overview of Key Issue Topic 
In zoning codes, setback, buffer, and build-to-line regulations must each balance multiple planning 
objectives. These include being calibrated to accommodate residential density requirements from the 
General Plan, supporting the development of appropriate building types, complementing the existing 
neighborhood character, ensuring adequate light, ventilation, fire safety, and privacy provisions are 
provided for residents, and ensuring economic feasibility for developers. In addition, these standards 
must be calibrated differently for lower-density residential project types, such as those in R1 and R2 
Districts, versus medium- and higher-density residential districts to ensure that each district can 
feasibly achieve its intended density and design. Properly establishing setbacks, buffers, and build-to-
line standards based on the intended purpose and density of a zoning district requires analyzing the 
City’s existing regulations and conditions. 
To create projects that align with community objectives, setback and buffer regulations often vary 
depending on the location and building type. Setbacks are often regulated in the front, interior side, 
street/corner side, and rear areas for a parcel and are typically measured as the minimum distance 
from a property line, street, or other boundary that a structure must be located. Buffers, often 
landscaped areas, are designed to separate and mitigate incompatible land uses, such as residential 
uses from industrial uses. Build-to-line standards can be viewed as being the opposite of setback 
standards. Where setbacks measure the minimum distance from a boundary, build-to-line standards 
measure the minimum distance from a street where a building’s façade or architectural feature is 
required to be built in order to encourage a walkable, active, and safe street area. For higher-density 
residential and non-residential zoning districts, build-to-line standards and reduced setbacks often 
support transit-oriented and walkable urban development, while lower-density districts typically rely 
more on traditional setbacks and landscaped buffers to maintain a less urban single-family residential 
neighborhood character. 
Brief Summary of Current Title 27 (Zoning) Regulations 
The following list provides the sections in the SMMC that address setbacks, buffers, and build-to-line 
standards: 
200 of 719

--- page 201 ---
January 22, 2026 | Page 3 
• 
The lower-density residential zoning districts (i.e., R1 and R2) provide setback regulations for 
front, side, and rear areas. In the R1 District, these regulations vary by subdistrict, with different 
standards applied in the R1-A District compared to the R1-B and R1-C Districts. The R2 District applies 
the same setback regulations as the R1-B and R1-C Districts. Setback and buffer regulations for the 
R1 and R2 Districts also include provisions for garage front setbacks, landscaping requirements, and 
limits on the maximum area allowed for paving.  
• 
Additionally, the R1 and R2 Districts both contain specific standards governing exceptions to 
the setback requirements regarding how certain elements, such as awnings, patios, decks, bay 
windows, flagpoles, and chimneys, may project into or encroach on required setback areas. 
Section 27.18.100 (Structures and Building Projections in Required Yards) in the SMMC 
outlines the respective standards for various types of building projections and features 
permitted in all yards and specific standards for the projections permitted in front yards. 
• 
The medium- to higher-density residential zoning districts (i.e., R3, R4, R5, R4-D, R5-D, and R6-
D) also provide setback regulations for front, side, and rear areas. The setback standards 
provided for the R3 District are also applicable to the R4 and R5 Districts. The R3, R4, R5, R4-
D, and R5-D districts also provide build-to-line regulations for properties within the Gateway 
area as defined in the Downtown Specific Plan. 
• 
The commercial zoning districts (i.e., C1, C2, C3, and C4) provide requirements for  building 
buffers. The C1 District also provides setback regulations for parcels fronting on El Camino 
Real from 9th Avenue to Belmont and build-to-lines for parcels fronting on El Camino Real 
from State Route 92 (SR 2) to Belmont and the Central Business District, respectively.  
• 
The office zoning districts (i.e., E1 and E2) provide setback regulations along street frontages. 
The office zoning district regulations also require landscape buffers when parcels with office 
uses are contiguous to residential districts. 
• 
The manufacturing district (i.e., M1) provides front and side setback regulations. The 
manufacturing district regulations also require buffers for manufacturing use parcels 
contiguous to residential and open space districts. Additionally, the manufacturing district 
provides buffer regulations for fast-food establishments from residential districts, as well as 
buffers from other fast-food establishments except when they are located in shopping 
centers.    
General Plan’s Direction and Policies  
This section provides the General Plan 2040 policies and actions that will be considered and taken into 
account when drafting the new setback and buffer development standards in the SMMC. 
Commercial Development 
Policy LU 3.2, Commercial Development, and Policy CD 8.3, Respect Existing Scale and Rhythm, 
address the incorporation of context-sensitive design and appropriate transition for new mixed-use 
and commercial developments. Policy LU 3.2 provides types of transition that include intensity of use, 
height, bulk, and design. Policy CD 8.3 also provides examples of context-sensitive design that include 
201 of 719

--- page 202 ---
January 22, 2026 | Page 4 
providing breaks in the building face at spacings common to buildings in the area and stepping back 
upper floors.  
To address these policies, the City will review Zoning Code changes for the current buffer 
requirements for the commercial, executive park, and manufacturing zoning districts in order to 
include new, clearer, and more comprehensive buffer requirements for new development next to 
residential areas. The CZCU will ensure that updates to standards are guided by context-sensitive 
design to facilitate appropriate transition from residential to commercial developments. 
In addition, Policy LU 3.2 directs the City to ensure that “commercial development adjacent to 
residential areas appropriately address circulation, traffic, truck loading, trash/recycling, noise, visual 
impacts, public safety, hazardous materials storage, fire safety, air pollutant emissions, and odors in a 
way that minimizes impacts on neighboring uses.” To address this, the CZCU will update standards to 
better address these topics in order to minimize impacts of commercial uses on neighboring uses. It 
will also be determined whether these additional buffer regulations will be incorporated within each 
district’s regulations or be placed in a separate section that includes a list of performance standards 
that will apply to all zoning districts. 
Action CD 8.7, Commercial Development Adjacent to Residential, directs the City to “Develop and 
adopt objective design standards that define and require appropriate design transitions from 
commercial to residential zones.” The CZCU will develop objective design standards that include 
buffer regulations to ensure appropriate transition from commercial to residential zones. 
Single-Family Design 
Policy CD 7.2, Single-Family Design, directs the City to “encourage single-family additions and new 
dwellings that address the preservation and enhancement of neighborhood visual and architectural 
character through context-sensitive building scale, materials, architectural style and details, and 
privacy.” 
To address this policy, the CZCU will review Zoning Code changes for the current buffer and setback 
requirements for the single-family residential districts to ensure that single-family neighborhood 
visual character and privacy are preserved and enhanced.  
State Law Compliance 
There are currently no relevant state laws that address requirements for setbacks and buffers. 
 
 
202 of 719

--- page 203 ---
January 22, 2026 | Page 5 
Best Practice Options and Considerations 
This section outlines best practice options and key considerations drawn from City staff input, state 
guidance, and comparable jurisdictions. The Project Team reviewed best practices from comparable 
jurisdictions to ensure the updated regulations reflect current planning standards successfully applied 
in comparable communities. These references help identify approaches that are clear, consistent, and 
adaptable to San Mateo’s goals and current built environment. The comparable jurisdictions 
referenced in this white paper include Santa Clara County, City of Milpitas, and City of Stockton. The 
following considerations will help inform updates to the City’s setback, buffer, and build-to-line 
regulations: 
• 
For projects in the R1 and R2 Districts, Section 27.18.100 (Structures and Building Projections 
in Required Yards) provides the standards for permitted projections and encroachments into 
required setbacks. Relative to comparable jurisdictions (i.e., City of Stockton, City of Milpitas, 
and Santa Clara County), the City’s existing projection regulations are a combination of being 
overly specific for the list of encroachments included and also broad for how they are 
regulated. For example, while garden ornaments and play equipment are explicitly permitted, 
the Section does not specify how far they may encroach into a setback. In contrast, 
comparable jurisdictions utilize broader categories of projection types while maintaining clear, 
specific distance limits for various setback types. For example, the City of Stockton includes 
“architectural features” as one broad category that covers balconies, chimneys, and window 
types. The City of Stockton and City of Milpitas use “ramps and similar structures that provide 
access for persons with disabilities,” which could replace the City’s existing encroachment 
type of “steps which are necessary to provide access to the first floor of a permitted building, 
or to a parcel from a street or alley” (Section 27.18.100(a)(8)). The CZCU will consider 
consolidating and modernizing the existing list of allowed encroachments by grouping similar 
types into broader categories and adding or removing types based on this review. 
• 
As discussed above in the “City and Community’s Goals” section, the current setback 
requirements in the SMMC might be overly restrictive. There are also some specific setback, 
buffer, and build-to-line provisions that are unique to the SMMC compared to zoning codes of 
comparable jurisdictions and would benefit from being standardized for ease of use and 
clarity. These regulations, such as determining interior side and street side (corner lot) 
setbacks according to project type (e.g., one-family detached dwellings) in the R3, R4, and R5 
Districts and measuring setbacks based on measures such as half of the building or the size of 
the lot are not utilized as a measurement method in comparable jurisdictions and could pose 
challenges to development feasibility. The CZCU will consider updating the current setback 
regulations to increase development feasibility and improve the clarity of the regulations.  
• 
The current setback, buffer, and build-to-line standards for all zoning districts are included in 
the Zoning Code as individual subsections and are written as short sentences or longer 
paragraphs. Including the standards in this manner increases the difficulty for readers of the 
Zoning Code. Instead, setback requirements will be included directly in tables for each zoning 
district to be more user-friendly. 
203 of 719

--- page 204 ---
January 22, 2026 | Page 6 
• 
The current build-to-line regulations are currently specified only in the C1 District and are 
cross-referenced to apply across the C2, C3, and C4 Districts.0F0F1 The current build-to-line 
regulations only apply to properties with frontage on El Camino Real from State Route 92 to 
the Belmont city limit and the Q5 (Qualified Overlay District 5) District, at the Hillsdale 
Shopping Center. Although most comparable jurisdictions do not include build-to-line 
regulations, the City of Sacramento includes build-to-line regulations that apply to their 
highest density residential, central business district, and hospital zones. The CZCU will 
consider reviewing the build-to-line to determine if the regulations will continue to apply to 
all commercial districts. The CZCU will also determine if build-to-line regulations will apply to 
the highest density residential zones.  
• 
General Plan Policy LU 3.2, Commercial Development, will be referenced when updating 
buffer requirements for the commercial, executive park, and manufacturing zoning districts. 
The City will amend the current buffer requirements for new development next to residential 
areas to be clearer and more comprehensive. Additionally, based on this Policy, the CZCU will 
include updating existing and incorporating additional buffer regulations, including traffic, 
trash/recycling, and noise requirements, in order to minimize the impacts of commercial uses 
on neighboring uses. For these regulations, the CZCU will either incorporate these regulations 
within each district regulation or be placed in its own section with a list of performance 
standards applicable to all zoning districts. Based on a review of comparable jurisdictions, it is 
suggested to include these buffer regulations in one section. For example, the City of Stockton 
consolidates buffer requirements in one section in the Zoning Code, providing a clear, user-
friendly format.

S A N  M A T E O  Z O N I N G  C O D E  U P D A T E  
C I T Y  O F  S A N  M A T E O  
DRAFT COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT AND OUTREACH PLAN 
1 
A. Introduction 
This Community Engagement and Outreach Plan (the Outreach Plan) describes tools and 
strategies to solicit input from the San Mateo community on updates to the Zoning Code. 
Strive San Mateo continues its transformative work with a comprehensive update to the 
City’s Zoning Code (Title 27). The update will align the Zoning Code with the long-term 
vision in the adopted General Plan 2040, creating a clearer, more intuitive framework to 
support mixed-use development, economic development, environmental justice, and 
sustainability.  
General Plan 2040 guides decisions about zoning, development, and other land use or 
regulatory matters, and is built around goals, policies, and actions that must be 
implemented to achieve the community’s vision for the future. Preparation of the General 
Plan included extensive community outreach and engagement. The City of San Mateo (City) 
held or participated in over 80 community meetings to discuss the General Plan with 
residents, community advocates, stakeholders, and public officials. Input and direction 
from the public and City Council were incorporated into each component of the General 
Plan. Community input also resulted in changes needed to the Zoning Code to reflect the 
updated General Plan Land Use Map and other policy directions. Implementing General 
Plan 2040 is a significant focus of the comprehensive Zoning Code update. 
This Outreach Plan serves as a guide for engaging key stakeholders, community, the 
Planning Commission, and the City Council as part of the Zoning Code update. Table 1 
provides an overview of the outreach approach, with the steps that comprise the Zoning 
Code update process and specific outreach objectives, activities, and target groups for each 
step. A brief discussion of engagement for the main target groups follows the table. The 
Outreach Plan is a “living document” that may be modified over the course of the work 
program.

--- page 211 ---

S A N  M A T E O  Z O N I N G  C O D E  U P D A T E  
C I T Y  O F  S A N  M A T E O  
DRAFT COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT AND OUTREACH PLAN 
3 
B. Target Groups
San Mateo Community 
Engagement with the public will include up to two community meetings held virtually and 
two in-person community meetings to facilitate participation from residents and other 
members of the public. The focus of the meeting is educating the public on what is 
regulated in the Zoning Code, the proposed significant changes to the Zoning Code, with 
information provided on how the changes reflect planning priorities the public identified 
during the General Plan update process. 
The project team will publish the virtual community meeting and other opportunities for 
public involvement and input via eblast announcements, StriveSanMateo.org updates, 
social media, flyers, eblasts, and notification through a citywide mailer. 
Key Stakeholders 
The Zoning Code is a very detailed and technical document. Some important changes will 
be nuanced and complex to discuss. One outreach channel will focus on key stakeholders 
who are frequent users of the current code, informed about its challenges, and have 
worked with other codes in other jurisdictions.  
Potential key stakeholders include: 

“Expert Users” (e.g., applicants and property owners familiar with the code and the
City’s regulations and processes)

Chamber of Commerce

Affordable Housing Developers

Market-Rate Housing Developers

Commercial, Office, and/or Mixed-Use Developers

Housing Advocates

Industrial Users (?)

Real Estate Agents

Business Owners

Other community-based organization representatives

Property owners particularly in key areas of change for the zoning map

Residents in areas where a zoning code policy change could have a particular effect
212 of 719

--- page 213 ---
S A N  M A T E O  Z O N I N G  C O D E  U P D A T E  
C I T Y  O F  S A N  M A T E O  
DRAFT COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT AND OUTREACH PLAN 
4 
Engagement with key stakeholders could include targeted discussions regarding 
development standards (e.g., residential development standards), allowed land uses and 
standards for certain uses, and permit processing requirements. Some stakeholder input 
may be most effectively gathered in one-on-one interviews. Developers, for example, are 
likely to be more forthcoming on a one-on-one basis rather than in a room with their 
competitors. However, there will be opportunities to provide updates to the development 
community at the City’s quarterly developer round table meetings. 
Online surveys or email questionnaires may be used as initial contact to help narrow the 
topics or options for discussion, or as follow-up to refine or clarify content that has already 
been discussed.  
It is expected the stakeholder meetings and interviews will be by invitation and not open to 
the public to provide a venue for candid input on certain topics in a focused setting. 
PlaceWorks will prepare the following material: 

Draft email invitations for City staff to send to invitees

Meeting agendas

Discussion questions
PlaceWorks will facilitate the small group meetings and conduct the one-on-one interviews 
which may use the same discussion questions as the small group meetings. City staff are 
welcome to attend these meetings. The scope of work includes a total of 12 stakeholder 
meetings and/or one-on-one interviews. PlaceWorks anticipates these meetings will occur 
during the Zoning Code Assessment and Drafting Zoning Code phases as shown in Table 1. 
Property Owners with Proposed Zoning Changes 
General Plan 2040 changed land use designations on many parcels in the 10 study areas. 
This will result in the need for zoning changes to bring zoning designations into consistency 
with the General Plan. The Zoning Code update team is currently completing an analysis to 
identify specific parcels that will need zoning changes and the appropriate new zoning 
designation. Once that analysis is completed, the team will consider the most effective way 
to contact all property owners affected by zoning designation updates. In addition, targeted 
focus groups with certain areas where there are more property owners impacted by the 
proposed changes for consistency are an option for outreach surrounding the proposed 
zoning changes.

Planning Commission and City Council 
Engagement with the Planning Commission and City Council could include one-on-one 
briefings conducted by City staff and public study sessions. The scope of work includes five 
total Planning Commission or City Council study sessions. 
For the first City Council study session, the project team will provide a project overview and 
present the proposed outreach plan, general organization of the new Zoning Code, and 
approach to Specific Plans. Potential topics for subsequent meetings are listed below. 
All Planning Commission and City Council discussions will lead with explanations of how 
the City is implementing the Zoning Code in the adopted General Plan 2040 and 
responding to the input received during the General Plan update process. 
POTENTIAL ENGAGEMENT TOPICS 




o

challenging topics, such as building height, shared and unbundled parking,
use classifications for current and future uses, or bird-safe regulations.
o
Metropolitan Transportation Commission Transit-Oriented Communities
Policy compliance

February 19, 2026 
San Mateo City Council
330 West 20th Avenue 
San Mateo, CA 94403 
Subject: Comments on Integrating Climate Resilience in the City of San Mateo Zoning Code Update 
Dear San Mateo City Council, 
Thank you for the opportunity to provide comment on San Mateo’s Zoning Code update process. We 
believe the zoning code update is a crucial opportunity for the City to incorporate forward-looking sea 
level rise and flood resilience policies into the City’s guiding documents.  
San Mateo has a significant amount of housing, commercial building, essential infrastructure, and 
roadway in low-lying areas near the shoreline that face increasing risk as sea levels rise and storms 
intensify. If sea levels rise by 3.3 feet, a conservative estimate for 2100, this would put at risk 12,005 
residential parcels, 7 emergency shelter sites, 20 miles of highway, and many other assets.1 Additionally, 
rising groundwater may inundate contaminated sites near the shoreline, potentially posing a health risk 
to surrounding Bay ecosystems and community members (see maps attached).   
At 6.6 feet of sea level rise, San Mateo will have 1,028 acres of Plan Bay Area 2050 Growth Geographies2 
exposed to sea level rise and resulting groundwater rise (see maps attached). This means that more than 
50% of the geographic areas where future growth in housing and jobs is likely to happen during the next 
30 years will be at risk of flooding3. As these areas develop, it’s critical that new buildings and 
infrastructure be resilient to climate-driven flood risk.  
In order to address this issue, we recommend that the City create a Sea Level Rise and Groundwater 
Rise Overlay Zone for the area that will be impacted by 6.6 feet of sea level rise and the resulting 
groundwater rise (see maps attached). This approach is strongly supported by OneShoreline’s 2023 
3 Bay Conservation and Development Commission, Regional Shoreline Adaptation Plan Atlas 
https://rsap-atlas.bcdc.ca.gov/ 
2 “Plan Bay Area 2050 Growth Geographies”. 2021. 
https://planbayarea.org/sites/default/files/documents/PBA2050_Growth_Geographies_Oct2021_0.pdf?cb=01d08
5e9 
1 “County of San Mateo Sea Level Rise Vulnerability Assessment”, 2018. Pg 157. 
218 of 719

--- page 219 ---
Shoreline Policy Planning Guidance4 and the Bay Conservation and Development Commission (BCDC)’s 
Regional Shoreline Adaptation Plan (RSAP).5  
 
The RSAP sets standards for how all shoreline communities in the Bay will address sea level rise in 
subregional shoreline adaptation plans, as required by SB-272. The RSAP lists overlay zones for sea level 
rise and emergent groundwater as a suggested strategy to meet Adaptation Strategy Standard 5, 
“Preserve natural and undeveloped lands for shoreline resilience,” Adaptation Strategy Standard 6, 
“Minimize flood risks in areas with existing and planned development,” and Adaptation Strategy 8, 
“Promote safe, sustainable, and strategic growth and density.”6 Establishing an overlay zone during this 
Zoning Code update would allow the City to align land use policy with its forthcoming subregional 
shoreline adaptation plan and ensure consistency with RSAP requirements.  
 
OneShoreline’s Planning Policy Guidance provides model language for Sea Level Rise and Groundwater 
Rise overlay zones covering areas subject to the future conditions associated with 100-year flood plus 6.6 
feet of sea level rise. Within such an overlay, the Guidance encourages cities to establish design and 
performance standards requiring new developments to include the following: 
●​ Establish a minimum lowest floor elevation of 3 feet above the current base flood elevation, 
●​ Incorporate setbacks that place flood infrastructure as landward as feasible, preserving 
transition space for habitat migration and future levee widening or ecotone slopes7, 
●​ Contribute to regional shoreline infrastructure funds OR build/maintain shoreline infrastructure 
that provides protection to the FEMA + 6 standard, or if lesser, provide land rights/easements to 
enable the city or OneShoreline to meet that standard, 
●​ Evaluate and plan adaptations for future flood conditions for the project lifetime, 
●​ Conduct a watershed-level groundwater rise analysis to understand and address impacts such as 
buoyancy, seepage, infiltration, liquefaction, and corrosion, and plan for management of 
groundwater rise inboard of flood protection infrastructure, and 
●​ If located on or near a contaminated site, consider impacts of rising groundwater on 
contaminant mobilization and have a monitoring and remediation plan.  
 
There is precedent in San Mateo County to adopt an overlay zone. Burlingame, South San Francisco, and 
East Palo Alto are examples of nearby San Mateo County cities that have already implemented sea level 
rise overlay zones. 
 
7 This policy also aligns with the RSAP Guidelines. Ibid. Pgs 123, 157, 159, 197. 
6 Ibid. Pg 158-159. 
5 Bay Conservation and Development Commission Regional Shoreline Adaptation Plan, December 2024, Adaptation 
Strategy Standards 2-6, 8, 16, pp. 156-159, 163. 
https://www.bcdc.ca.gov/wp-content/uploads/sites/354/2024/12/regional-shoreline-adaptation-plan.pdf 
4 OneShoreline Policy Planning Guidance to Protect and Enhance Bay Shoreline Areas of San Mateo County, June 
2023, pp. 27-47. 
https://oneshoreline.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/09/OneShoreline-Planning-Policy-Guidance-Final-June-21-202
3-For-Web.pdf 
 
219 of 719

--- page 220 ---
Additionally, this recommendation aligns with San Mateo’s existing planning documents. The Safety 
Element, in Section 3.13, commits to “evaluate establishment of a sea level rise and/ or flood overlay 
zone as a primary mechanism for establishing adaptation policies, rules, or construction codes.” 
Additionally, Section 3.3 commits to "Ensure that new development, substantial retrofits, critical 
facilities, City-owned buildings, and existing and future flood control infrastructure are planned and 
designed to accommodate climate change hazards, including increases in flooding, sea level rise, and 
rising groundwater, based on the best available science.” 
 
Integrating overlay zones into the Zoning Code Update would operationalize these commitments and 
provide a durable regulatory framework that protects residents, infrastructure, ecological function, and 
future adaptation options. We look forward to partnering with the City to ensure that zoning and 
shoreline adaptation planning proceed in close alignment and position San Mateo for long-term 
resilience. 
 
Sincerely, 
 
David Lewis 
Executive Director 
Save The Bay 
 
Jennifer Chang Hetterly 
Bay Alive Coordinator 
Sierra Club Loma Prieta Chapter

Subject: Sierra Club Loma Prieta Chapter Comments on Comprehensive Zoning Code Update (CZCU) 
 
Dear Mayor Loraine and Members of the San Mateo City Council, Planning Commission and City Staff, 
The Sierra Club Loma Prieta Chapter's Sustainable Land Use Committee (SLU) advocates on land use issues 
in San Mateo and Santa Clara Counties. 
SLU offers these comments as an overview of key issues that we believe need to be addressed in the

228 of 719

--- page 229 ---
SAN MATEO, SANTA CLARA & SAN BENITO COUNTIES           
 
February 19, 2026​
 
Via Email: 
Citycouncil@cityofsanmateo.org 
planningcommission@cityofsanmateo.org 
zoningupdate@cityofsanmateo.org 
 
Subject: Key Issues for Comprehensive Zoning Code Update 
Dear Mayor Loraine and Members of the San Mateo City Council, Planning Commission and City 
Staff, 
The Sierra Club Loma Prieta Chapter's Sustainable Land Use Committee (SLU) advocates on land 
use issues in San Mateo and Santa Clara Counties. 
SLU offers the comments below as an overview of key issues that we believe need to be addressed in 
the Comprehensive Zoning Code Update (CZCU). These comments are consistent with the 
comments that SLU made for the General Plan 2040 (GP) process (see letters of March 1, 2024, 
November 16, 2023, September 7, 2023, August 9, 2023, and March 28, 2023).  
We have reviewed the White Papers on Open Space, Building Heights, and Setbacks & Buffers. They 
provide a good start to the CZCU process.  
1.​ Increase emphasis on the need for increased housing, particularly affordable housing. 
a.​ Streamline and reduce costs for development of housing. While the White Papers 
mention this in several areas, a more specific discussion should be incorporated into 
this policy statement. Simplifying the code, clarifying and standardizing requirements 
and not being overly prescriptive will reduce development costs and increase the 
likelihood of developing vitally needed housing. An enumeration of key requirements 
should be included at the outset.  
b.​ Increase focus on affordable housing. The affordable housing category is the most 
difficult type of housing to develop, as evidenced by past failures to meet affordable 
housing goals, while the goals for market rate housing are being met. This points to the 
need to simplify and streamline even more for fully affordable projects.  
2.​ Open Space: Ensure that the increased building heights leads to more Open Space as 
well as quality Open Space. 
sierraclub.org/loma-prieta ~ 3921 East Bayshore Road, Suite 204, Palo Alto, CA 94303   ​
Page 1 of 3 
229 of 719

--- page 230 ---
a.​ Ensure Open Space for Nature and Urban Greening: The focus in the draft Open 
Space White Papers is on recreation and gatherings, which are important. However, 
open space that is specifically for the value of nature needs to be a significant part of 
the reason and use for urban open space as well. Large trees and vegetation buffers 
will benefit human health, both physical and mental. They clean urban pollution, 
provide shading/cooling, wind breaks, runoff absorption, noise absorption, and wildlife 
habitat. Even city birds and bees need something more than pruned shrubs and 
courtyards. The urban forest, tall trees on streets and alleys, make the streets far more 
livable, increase property values and are especially desirable for walking and biking. The 
CZCU needs to reflect this overarching objective for greening our City, and the Sierra 
Club SLU Guidelines on Green Streets is one source of information on how to 
accomplish this1. 
b.​ Taller buildings can be linked to creating more open space: Mechanisms need to be 
included in the CZCU to incentivize developers to provide for more and better Open 
Space for parks/bike lanes/green streets/natural areas/etc. The White Paper mentions 
that incentives will be developed as part of the CZCU. SLU suggests that one possible 
incentive would be to allow higher heights, but not more density, if that leads to more 
common open space.  This could be accomplished by setting maximum housing 
density and open space requirements in a given zoning district, but not setting a 
maximum building height and possibly adding incentives for green open space that 
exceeds the minimum such as expedited plan review to reduce lag time development 
costs.   
3.​ Biological Safety Levels (BSL) adjacent to sensitive receptors 
a.​  Only Biological Safety Levels (BSL) 1 and 2 biolabs should be allowed in zoning for 
office, commercial, manufacturing, Life Safety, or similar non-residential uses. Most 
Industrial Zoning in peninsula cities should also be similarly restricted as they are 
adjacent to residential and other uses with sensitive receptors. 
b.​ Bio-lab uses should not be allowed within a specified distance of sensitive receptors 
including housing, schools, hospitals and emergency service locations. 
4.​ Focus on planning now for sea level rise and associated ground water rise.  
a.​ A Sea Level Rise (SLR) Overlay Zone should be considered for those areas with low 
ground elevations so that sea level rise and associated ground water rise, from climate 
change, will cause inundation of public infrastructure such as roads and underground 
utilities as well as private homes and businesses. The overlay zone could identify 
specific requirements for new developments including a requirement to raise first floor 
elevations above established flood levels to better prepare for future SLR and also a 
time line for capital outlays to raise roads and especially utilities out of corrosive rising 
brackish ground water.  
b.​ The overlay zone could include areas directly impacted by SLR as well as related 
impacts like creeks backing up with higher tides and causing flooding. 
1 See SLU White Paper on Green Corridor/Green Streets Guidelines which uses San Mateo as the example.  
sierraclub.org/loma-prieta ~ 3921 East Bayshore Road, Suite 204, Palo Alto, CA 94303   ​

c.​ The Health and Safety Element of the General Plan may also need modification to 
recognize the impacts of flooded infrastructure and other health and safety impacts of 
flooding from rising sea levels and ground water levels. 
SLU looks forward to further discussions with you on the CZCU.

for public hearing)

CITY OF SAN MATEO
Agenda Report
City Hall
330 W. 20th Avenue
San Mateo CA 94403
www.cityofsanmateo.org
Agenda Number:  9
Section Name: PUBLIC HEARING
Account Number: 25-3112
File ID: 26-10876
TO:
City Council
FROM:
Alex Khojikian, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
Community Development Department
MEETING DATE:
February 23, 2026 
SUBJECT:
General Plan - San Mateo's 2023-2031 Housing Element — Amendments
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt a Resolution to approve amendments to the General Plan’s Housing Element for the Sixth Housing Cycle (2023-
2031) that is compliant with State law, provides a plan for at least 7,015 new housing units, affirmatively furthers fair 
housing, and is exempt from review under the California Environmental Quality Act, based on the listed findings. 
BACKGROUND:
In May 2024, the City adopted its 2023-2031 (6th Cycle) Housing Element, which was subsequently certified by the California 
Department of Housing and Community Development (HCD) in July 2024. The Housing Element includes an inventory of sites 
that can potentially be developed with housing (“Sites Inventory”). State law requires that the Sites Inventory include enough 
housing opportunity sites with sufficient capacity to meet the City’s Regional Housing Needs Allocation (RHNA) of 7,105 
housing units between 2023 and 2031.
Following adoption of the Housing Element, the Housing Action Coalition (HAC) filed a lawsuit against the City, claiming that 
the Housing Element did not satisfy state law because sites listed on the Sites Inventory were not likely to serve as locations 
for future housing construction because of existing and active uses. In July 2025, the parties entered into a settlement 
agreement (“Settlement Agreement”). The City agreed to amend the Housing Element to remove certain sites from the Sites 
Inventory, limit the use of certain other sites for the purposes of “No Net Loss” calculations under Government Code Section 
658631.  In addition, since the adoption of the 2023-2031 Housing Element, voters approved Measure T in November 2024, 
which increased residential densities around San Mateo and increased the City’s housing capacity by approximately 19,760 
units. Also, since approval, the City has experienced a significant uptick in new development applications, with proposed 
projects with more than 5,200 residential units currently seeking entitlement. 
As part of these amendments, only the main Housing Element document and Appendix C (Housing Resources and Sites 
Inventory) will be updated. All other Housing Element appendices that were adopted by the City and certified by HCD in July 
2024 will remain unchanged. On December 18, 2025, HCD determined that the City’s proposed Housing Element 
amendments are in substantial compliance with California Government Code Section 65580 et seq. and can be certified once 
adopted locally. The HCD letter finding that the proposed Housing Element amendments will substantially comply with State 
Housing Element Law is included as Attachment 2.
A draft City Council Resolution to adopt the General Plan Amendments to the 2023-2031 Housing Element is included as 
Attachment 1. The findings included in the Resolution outline how the Housing Element is compliant with State law, provides 
1 Under Government Code Section 65863 if the City approves a development of a parcel identified in the Housing Element sites inventory with fewer 
units than shown in the Housing Element, the City must make findings that the Housing Element’s remaining sites have sufficient capacity to 
accommodate the remaining unmet RHNA by each income level. Also, the City may not disapprove a housing project on the basis that approval of the 
development would trigger the identification or zoning of additional adequate sites to accommodate the remaining RHNA.
244 of 719

--- page 245 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
a plan for at least 7,015 new housing units, affirmatively furthers fair housing, and is exempt from review under the California 
Environmental Quality Act (CEQA). Importantly, the proposed Housing Element amendments would only go into effect upon 
formal recertification by HCD, and not immediately upon City Council approval.  Findings to support the CEQA determination 
that the proposed Housing Element amendments are exempt from further environmental review are also included in the 
Resolution. The proposed Housing Element amendments are included as Attachment 3, with a tracked changes version 
included as Attachment 4.  
DISCUSSION:
This section details the changes made to the Sites Inventory in the proposed amendments. The categories of these changes 
are:
•
Sites removed from the Sites Inventory consistent with the Settlement Agreement
•
Sites remaining on the Sites Inventory but not counted for “No Net Loss” purposes, consistent with the Settlement 
Agreement
•
Sites added to the Sites Inventory where projects have been proposed (“Pipeline Projects”), adjusted since Housing 
Element adoption due to new, updated or withdrawn proposals
•
Changes to the “realistic capacity” assumptions for many of these sites due to the passage of Measure T.
Sites Inventory Removals
Consistent with the Settlement Agreement with HAC, the Housing Opportunity Sites Inventory (Appendix C, Table 2, see 
Attachment 5) was updated to remove the following seven sites:
1.
The Bridgepointe Shopping Center Parking Lot (portion of APN 035-466-100)
2.
1826 and 1850 S. Norfolk St. (Parkside Plaza Shopping Center) (APNs 035-381-020 and -030)
3.
71-77 Bovet, and 1750 El Camino Real (Borel Shopping Center) (APNs 039-011-450, -460, -470, -480, -500, -510)
4.
229 W. 20th Ave., 205 W. 20th Ave., and 2010 Pioneer Court (Consolidated Site H) (APNs 039-052-350, 039-060-010, 
and 039-060-020)
5.
150 W. 20th Ave. (APN 039-030-220)
6.
4060 S. El Camino Real (Villa Plaza) (APN 042-241-180) 
7.
5 N. San Mateo Dr., 123 Baldwin Ave., 117 Baldwin Ave., 26 N. San Mateo Dr., 27 N. San Mateo Dr. (Downtown 
Parking Lots) (APNs 032-312-250, -270, -150, -100 and -070) 
If the property owner for any of these seven sites objects to its removal from the Sites Inventory, then that location will 
remain on the inventory but will not be counted for “No Net Loss” purposes, pursuant to the Settlement Agreement.  This 
is what occurred with the Bel Mateo/Olympic Shopping Center site (No. 4 in the list immediately below) and 1311 S. El 
Camino Real (No. 5 below) that were originally planned for removal from the Sites Inventory. 
Sites Remaining on the Sites Inventory but not Counted for “No Net Loss” Purposes
The following sites will remain on the Sites Inventory, but will not be used for “No Net Loss” purposes. However, if any of 
these five sites become the subject of a housing development preliminary application or a formal application, then the site 
may be considered for “No Net Loss” purposes.
1.
1500 Fashion Island Blvd. (County Office Building) (APN 035-550-040)
2.
1900 S. Norfolk St. (The Atrium) (APN 035-391-090)
3.
1650 Borel Place (APN 039-011-400)
4.
Bel Mateo/Olympic Shopping Center (APNs 042-242-050, -060, -070, -160; 042-243-020, 042-244-040, -050; 042-
245-040, -050, -060, -070, -080, -090, -100, -110, -120, -130; 042-263-010, 042-264-010)
5.
1311 S. El Camino Real (APN 034-301-200)

--- page 246 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
Added to the Sites Inventory
Since adoption of the Housing Element in May 2024, several applications for new housing development projects have been 
submitted to the City seeking planning entitlements. These are now included as Pipeline Projects. The Pipeline Projects list 
includes all housing development projects that are under review (submitted applications), approved (entitled) and under 
construction (building permits issued). These include the following sites:
1.
600 E 3rd Ave. (Gateway) (APN 034-188-140)
2.
230 S. El Camino Real (APN 034-093-050)
3.
1 E. 4th Ave. (includes 15 E 4th Ave. and 25 E 4th Ave.) (APNs 034-143-260, -300, and -310)
4.
22 N. San Mateo Dr. (APN 032-321-340)
5.
2015 Pioneer Ct. (APN 039-060-860)
6.
3000 Clearview Way (APN 041-361-120)
7.
Hillsdale Mall Sites (APNs 039-490-170, 039-353-010, -020, -030, and -040, 042-121-080 and -060)
In addition to these sites, the City has added the site at 1770 S. Amphlett Blvd (former Marriott site; APN 035-241-220) to 
the Sites Inventory as new housing opportunity site.
Other Sites Inventory Changes
Some sites that were seeking planning entitlements and considered Pipeline Projects are no longer actively seeking those 
entitlements; therefore, they were removed as Pipeline Projects but remain as opportunity sites. In addition, some sites that 
were included as Pipeline Projects (with or without planning entitlement approvals) have resubmitted new applications for 
residential development projects with increased number housing units (due to the passage of Measure T). Those sites have 
been updated with the housing unit counts consistent with those new applications. 
Measure T Voter Approval
As previously stated, in November 2024, San Mateo voters approved Measure T, implementing Policy H1.20 of the Housing 
Element, which provided for focused growth near transit, the downtown, and other targeted areas. This increased potential 
residential development capacity around the City by approximately 20,000 units. The Housing Element Amendment updates 
language throughout the document accounting for the adoption of Measure T and removing language that addresses any 
potential actions to be taken in-lieu of the passage of Measure T.
Realistic Capacities
“Realistic capacities” are used in the opportunity sites methodology to determine the estimated number of housing units a 
given site is likely to produce, which may be different from the number of units the site is allowed to produce according to 
its base zoning density. With the passage of Measure T, the densities of General Plan 2040 and the realistic capacities for 
many of the housing opportunity sites have been updated to account for the increased capacity from 50 dwelling units per 
acre to 99 or 130 dwelling units per acre, depending on the land use designation. The Sites Inventory numbers in the revised 
Appendix C (Attachment 5) and the Housing Element have been updated to reflect these increased capacities. A tracked 
changes version of the revised Appendix C is included as Attachment 6.  
Also, since the update of General Plan 2040 and passage of Measure T, the City has received 14 preliminary/formal 
applications3 for residential development projects that largely take advantage of the increased densities and height limits in 
General Plan 2040. These new projects are being proposed at an average of 122% of base density4. The City analyzed these 
recent projects along with other residential development projects previously analyzed, and adjusted the realistic capacities 
of each site accordingly. On sites that were previously assigned a realistic capacity of 75% of the site’s zoning district density, 
the realistic capacities were increased to 90% of the base density of the site’s General Plan Land Use designation. For Pipeline 
3 At the time the Housing Element Amendment was being drafted. Since the draft was completed and publicly noticed, additional residential 
development projects have been submitted to the City, but will not be included in the Sites Inventory.
4 The average density does not include the housing development project proposals at the Hillsdale Mall site or the site at 3000 Clearview Dr. because 
these sites are significantly larger than 10 acres and are not representative of a majority of sites in the City that will likely develop during this planning 
period.

Projects, the project’s proposed unit count (affordable and market rate) was used.
Table 1 below provides a summary of the housing unit potential of the Sites Inventory based on all of the proposed updates. 
This includes the removal of several sites from the Inventory, the addition of new sites, changes to pipeline projects, voter 
approval of Measure T, and changes to the realistic capacities in calculating the estimated unit potential on each opportunity 
site. It shows the housing unit potential and excluding those sites the City will not use for the purpose of calculating “No Net 
Loss” (NNL), referred to as “NNL Sites.” In the table, the “Opportunity Sites” row does not include parcels that the City agreed 
to exclude under its settlement agreement with HAC.  If one were to include those parcels, the Opportunity Sites total would 
include potential for 9,334 housing units.  The table shows that, even with the removal of sites and limitations on using sites 
for “No Net Loss” purposes, the potential housing units in the Sites Inventory significantly exceeds its remaining RHNA (RHNA 
minus Pipeline Projects) by a 32% to 484% buffer depending on income category. Furthermore, as of December 2025, the 
City had issued permits for over 300 new ADUs, which puts San Mateo on track to significantly exceed the estimated 440 
new ADUs through the end of 2031, so that will further increase the buffer over the remaining RHNA.

Other Amendments
Other minor revisions in the Housing Element amendments include updates to implementation plan action items for Policy 
H 3.1 (Prevent Homelessness) to reflect the recently established emergency rental assistance program ($150,000 annually) 
and minor updates to actions under Policies H 3.4 (Expand Tenant Protections) and H 5.4.1 (Establish Tenant Protections in 
Local Ordinance), for internal consistency and to better align with recent Council action.  Additionally, Policy H 1.2 (Utilize 
Public Funding and Properties for New Affordable Housing) would be revised to give the City flexibility during budget deficit 
conditions from being required to set aside 20% of General Fund property tax revenues from former RDA areas (“Boomerang 
Funds”) to use for affordable housing development.  However, the City’s housing funds are currently in a strong position, 
between the Housing Set-Aside Reserve ($6.3 million), the Low and Moderate Income Housing Asset Fund ($5.4 million), 
and the City Housing Fund ($1.4 million).  As part of implementing Policy H 1.2, a City Council study session is planned for 
later this year to discuss the current use of these funds and a future strategy for leveraging other funding sources to support 
Housing Element implementation. Furthermore, the City’s current housing development pipeline includes over 5,200 
housing units9 with over 950 new affordable units, so compliance with the Housing Element’s affordable housing 
requirements is on track.
HCD Review
7 Number of pipeline units as of October 10, 2025; the date that the draft amendments were published for public review.

247 of 719

--- page 248 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
On October 10, 2025, the City published a draft of the Housing Element Amendments to the City’s website and sent a 
notification to the Housing Policy/Housing Element email distribution list to start the seven-day review period pursuant to 
Government Code Section 65585. No public comments were received that required changes to the draft Housing Element 
Amendments. On October 20, 2025, City staff sent the draft Housing Element Amendments to HCD for review.  On December 
18, 2025, City staff received the City received a letter from HCD determining that the City’s Housing Element - with the 
proposed amendments – would continue to be in substantial compliance with Government Code Section 65580 et seq. 
(Housing Element Law) and can be certified once adopted locally (Attachment 2).
Planning Commission Recommendation
Pursuant to Govt. Code Section 65353(a) and San Mateo Municipal Code Section Subsection 27.06.040(b), the Planning 
Commission must first hold a public hearing and provide a recommendation to the City Council on any proposed 
amendments to the General Plan. On January 27, 2026, the Planning Commission held a public hearing to consider the 
Housing Element Amendments. There were no verbal or written public comments. The Commission voted unanimously (5-
0) to recommend adoption of the 2023-2031 Housing Element Amendments to the City Council. 
Findings for Adoption
Given HCD’s finding of substantial compliance and the Planning Commission’s favorable recommendation, staff 
recommends that the City Council adopt the 2023-2031 Housing Element Amendments, based on the following key 
findings, and as expanded upon in the Draft Resolution in Attachment 1. 
1.
In accordance with Govt. Code Section 65300.5, the 2023-2031 Housing Element Amendments are internally 
consistent with General Plan 2040 that was adopted on March 18, 2024.
2.
The Housing Element Amendments substantially comply with the Housing Element Law, as provided in Govt. Code 
Section 65580 et seq., and contain all provisions required by the Housing Element Law as demonstrated in the 
Compliance with Statutory Provisions Table, adopted by City Council on May 20, 2024 in Resolution No. 37 (2024). 
The Housing Element Amendments revise only specific sections of Appendix C of the previously adopted Housing 
Element.  HCD has determined that the Housing Element will continue to be in substantial compliance with State 
law even after the Housing Element Amendments are adopted.
3.
Based on substantial evidence in the record, the existing uses on the non-vacant sites identified in the Sites Inventory 
to accommodate the City’s RHNA are likely to be discontinued during the planning period and/or do not need to be 
discontinued to accommodate the assigned housing units, and therefore do not constitute an impediment to 
planned residential development on the site during the planning period.  
The 2023-2031 Housing Element Amendments will be sent to HCD for formal certification following the City Council’ s 
review.
BUDGET IMPACT:
Although there are no direct budgetary impacts to taking this action, the preparation of the 2023-2031 Housing Element 
Amendments has involved significant City staff and consultant time. Funding for this work has come primarily from the 
City’s Advanced Planning Fund (Fund 225), which is funded by a fee collected with each building permit. To implement the 
Housing Element, a funding source is identified with each Housing Element policy and the Community Development 
Department will allocate appropriate staff and budgetary resources to complete each effort.
NOTICE PROVIDED:
All meeting noticing requirements were met. A public hearing notice for consideration of the City’s 2023-2031 Housing 
Element Amendments was published in the San Mateo Daily Journal. Notice of the Housing Element Amendments 
availability was displayed on the home page of the City’s website, posted to the City’s Housing Element website and a 
physical copy was made available at City Hall. Notice was also included in the City’s E-Newsletter and sent out to various 
email distribution lists, including the Housing Policy/Housing Element email distribution list.
248 of 719

--- page 249 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
The adoption of the 2023-2031 Housing Element Amendments are considered exempt under the “common sense” 
exemption under state CEQA Guidelines Section 15061(b)(3) because the project involves policies, programs, and actions to 
meet the City’s RHNA and AFFH goals that would not have any potential for causing a significant effect on the environment, 
and identifies programs and actions to remove constraints and promote affordable housing development. The proposed 
amendments involve an update to the Housing Element’s Sites Inventory, associated text amendments to align with these 
Sites Inventory updates and minor updates to various policies, programs, and actions to support meeting the City’s RHNA 
and AFFH goals. The 2023-2031 Housing Element is strictly a policy document that does not provide entitlements to any 
specific development projects and would not result in any direct or indirect physical changes to the environment. The 
proposed amendments are aligned with, and do not change, the Housing Element functioning as a policy document. Further, 
the proposed amendments do not include specific amendments to any land use regulations at this time, but rather update 
the policies and programs that will guide future implementation actions. Any subsequent implementation actions that 
include amendments to the City’s land use or zoning regulations would be subject to separate CEQA review at the time those 
amendments are prepared, if required. Therefore, it can be seen with certainty that the amendments to the City of San 
Mateo 2023-2031 Housing Element would not have a significant effect on the environment and are therefore exempt from 
CEQA review.
